For Simba, Azam and Mafunzo, the tournament is part of their preparations for this year's East and Central Africa club championship, scheduled to kick off from July 14 in Dar es Salaam.

Original_Search_Title: Wine Makers Decry Packaging Failure
Headline: Rwanda: Wine Makers Decry Packaging Failure
Body_Text:
Rwamagana/Ngoma-Banana wine producers in Eastern Province have decried lack of proper packaging in order to attract better markets.
Emile Murangabo, a member of a banana wine cooperative in Rwamagana, told The Sunday Times yesterday that there was great need for qualified packaging manufacturers.
He said that wine producers in Rwanda needed to create a distinct point of differentiation through their packaging, adding that customers packaging concerns should resonate with the producers' brand.
"The consumer is king. They have made some complaints about our packaging... we have to listen to them," he said.
"We need the government's help to promote our product, particularly on the packaging issue. Consumers as well as producers rail about packaging failures...the effect cuts across the board," he said.
Christine Murebwayire, another wine producer in Mutenderi sector in Ngoma district, also emphasised the importance of proper packaging.
She lamented that poor packaging affected the price of the wine on the international market, noting that the wine had a big market in Europe.
"We have one of the best wines in the world...but the fact that we can't pack it properly makes it inferior on the global market. We look forward to working with banks to get loans to do proper packaging," she said.
Aloys Kayibanda, a resident of Rwamagana district, noted that the bottles used to pack the wine where not user friendly.
He challenged the producers to do packaging that connect with the consumer in a meaningful way.
"Moving wine bottles can be quite a daunting task. Imagine unpacking your most prized bottle that has been cracked and is leaking on your other possessions. We always hear about packaging that doesn't work, but what about the packaging that doesn't exist"?
Banana wine producers in the province were recently promised support by the Ministry of Trade and Industry.

Original_Search_Title: The Good, Bad and Ugly of Euro 2012
Headline: Nigeria: The Good, Bad and Ugly of Euro 2012
Body_Text:
The European Championship have come and gone with some memorable moments that will forever be in our memory. Stars were made and some failed to shine. Tears were shed just as some celebrated all through the night.We bring some the good,bad and ugly moments of the European championship.
The good
Spain won the European championship thereby becoming the first team to retain the Euros and also the first team to win three consecutive international titles cementing their status as the greatest international team of all time.
Since their victory there have been debates about which team is the greatest of all time.Brazil of the 70's, West Germany of the 70's France team of 1998 to 2000 on the current Spanish national team.No matter the debate, this Spanish team can lay claim to be one of the greatests ever. The Spanish team demonstrated good team spirit throughout the tournament.
There were reports before the tournament of rifts between the Barcelona and Real Madrid players but Vicente Del Bosque was able to bring the squad together.
The players put aside their individual differences all for the good of the team which was one of the major factors behind the successful retainment of the trophy. The 2014 World Cup is just two years away and most of the current players bar any injuries will definitely make the squad.
The core of the team is still young which is a frightening prospect for rivals ahead the next World Cup. No one can bet against them winning their fourth straight title and if they achieve that, they will be the greatest team of all time
One good thing about the tournament was the record number of attendances. Euro 2012 set the record for both the highest aggregate attendance (1,440,896) and the highest average attendance per game (46,481).Ukraine and Poland should be proud for hosting a magnificent tournament
The bad
One of the bad side of Euro 2012 was that it was the second consecutive European Championship (after Euro 2008 held in Austria and Switzerland) in which neither of the hosts emerged from the group stage, as both Poland and Ukraine got eliminated. But it didn't stop the fans thronging to the stadiums to watch the games.
Another bad of the tournament was the debate about goal line technology resurfaced after Ukraine scored a goal against England with replay showing that the ball crossed the line. UEFA used five officials for every game in the tournament and still the fifth official did not see the ball crossing the line.Now that goal line technology has been approved by FIFA, UEFA should follow suit to reduce game changing calls.
Holland is the definition of bad in this tournament. Despite boasting of world class players like Robben,Van Persie,Sneijder,Van Da Vaart,Huntelar and Affellay, they lost all their three games in the competition becoming the first time that the Dutch failed to win a single game in the group stage in any competition.
There were fights and divisions in the squad as some players publicly criticised the coach for his tactics and team selection,the egos in the team was too much for the coach to handle resulting to first round exit.
France also started the competiton on high playing a one all draw with England but fell apart from then.There was a reported training ground fight involving Nasir and Laurent Blanc lost control of the dressing room.
After their elimination in the hands of eventual winners Spain,Nasir publicly challenged a journalist to a fight to sum up his woeful tournament.It was also a tournament in which some big players failed to shine.Defenders were at the top of their game as the top scoreres in the competiton finished with just three goals.It was not a good tournament for the forwards.
The ugly
Even before the ball was kicked at the Euros ,racism was the focal point of attention.Most analysts argued that Ukraine and Poland should never have been given the hosting rights due to the incidence of racism and violence in the countries.
Racism reared its ugly head during the tournament, The Croatian Football Federation was fined €80,000 for racist chants and throwing a banana at Mario Balotelli.
Champions Spain has also been fined for fans' racist chants directed at Balotelli. Russia too has been fined for racist chanting, and leads the overall tally with €195,000 in fines for chanting, displaying nationalist flags, and setting off fireworks.
Germany was fined for displaying a Nazi banner stating "God is with us," the inscription on the belt buckles all of all Wehrmacht soldiers during World War II, and for Nazi chants.
It seems Racism is increasing daily in football. FIFA needs to clamp down and eliminate racism once and for all out of football.

Original_Search_Title: More Mixed Reactions Over Free Public Toilets Use
Headline: Uganda: More Mixed Reactions Over Free Public Toilets Use
Body_Text:
Isa Kalanzi, 23, a porter at a construction firm in the city, has praised Jennifer Musisi, Kampala City Council Authority executive director, for scrapping the fee on public toilets.
Although sh200, the fee most toilets in the city charge is not something to worry about, paying it meant a lot to Kalanzi, a resident of Namuwongo, a city suburb.
Kalanzi, whose daily wage is sh2,500 will now not worry about paying a portion of his hard earned money for toilet use.
To most people in the lower income bracket, KCCA's announcement of scrapping user fee for toilets around the city could not have come at a better time.
When New Vision online visited some toilets, users said the attendants were not charging them for using the toilets.
According to KCCA, the is to improve sanitation in the city. The pay-and-use toilets were adopted by the defunct KCC more than a decade ago.
It was common for some places, especially the corridors and alleys to be strewn with human waste. While some known as the flying toilets were dumped in polythene bags.
A number of tycoons in the city came up and invested in the toilet business.
Seven years ago, there were about 20 public toilets serving millions of users. Today most shopping arcades have toilets total about 110 and they charge between sh3,00 to sh5,00.
George Lwanga, a toilet attendant in the city centre, who is implementing Musisi's directive, said he used to make about sh750,000 on a good day.
However, many fear that scrapping the toilet fees will compromise the zeal of attendants to keep the toilets clean.
"City toilets will look like kraals and people will not use them. It is better to pay sh200 to use a place that won't infect you with diseases," says Aida Akol, a banana hawker in the city.
Mohammad Moto said the toilets are better managed when users pay. He argues that Ugandans do not appreciate free services.
"Before city toilet started charging users, they were very dirty; the walls were smeared with human waste," he argues.
Livingstone Makumbe said he prefers paying because the service is always good. "You go to the toilets and find them clean," he points out.
Makumbe says KCCA should reduced fees to sh100.
However, some toilet attendants are still running business as usual.
Abdu Kerim, a toilet attendant in the city centre says they are not yet implementing it. "We use the money to buy water at sh200 for every 20 litre jerrycan, we buy toilet paper and also buy detergents," he explains.
At Ben Kiwanuka Street near Aponye Complex, an attendant says KCCA's directive does not apply to them.
New Vision online established that only toilets owned by KCCA were implementing Musisi's directive. KCCA manages only 11 public toliets in the city centre.

Original_Search_Title: Life on the Nation's Border
Headline: South Sudan: Yida - Life On the Nation's Border
Publisher: United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees
Body_Text:
Yida — Vivian Tan recently finished a temporary stint as UNHCR's senior regional public information officer for East Africa. Based in Nairobi, she coordinated coverage of the area, including the situation for almost half-a-million Somali refugees in Dadaab, the world's biggest refugee complex, and the unfolding displacement drama in the Sudans. She recently spent a few days in a remote area of South Sudan called Yida, where she discovered the hard way about conditions for tens of thousands of refugees as well as those helping them. Vivian, who will soon be heading to Thailand to become senior regional public information officer for East Asia, writes about this and other experiences.
Vivian's Yida Diary:
There's so much you take for granted when living in a city like Nairobi. Electricity, for one thing. And vegetables. Clean drinking water. Lavatory paper and flush toilets.
Up near the border of Sudan and South Sudan, in a remote spot called Yida where nearly 60,000 Sudanese refugees have gathered by late June, there is none of that. Not for the refugees, and not for the UNHCR colleagues I am living with for four days.
We're visiting from Juba and we've brought a few boxes of fruit and vegetables to supplement the rice-and-beans diet at the UNHCR compound. I've never seen sunburnt faces light up so fast. We have tomatoes and potatoes, they gasp and beam at lunch. Someone unveils a hand of bananas. A mob swoops in, everyone grabs one each and retreats to a corner, eyeing each other suspiciously. One guy rushes to his tent to hide the banana. I feel like I'm watching a wildlife documentary.
The novelty continues into dinner, when partners from other agencies turn up to verify rumours of vegetables in Yida. They walk around with headlights for closer scrutiny. Our whole compound is lit by a single light bulb. Once the generator sputters on, everyone flocks to the bulb like moths, diving for the power bar to recharge their gadgets.
In the dark, there is an occasional stomp and triumphant, "Hah!" Scorpions. Gulp. I check my e-mails with feet dangling in mid-air while colleagues brainstorm ideas for refugee projects like vegetable plots and grinding pots for maize. If they could channel their energy and enthusiasm, they could light up the whole camp.
Network - well that's another problem. I find a colleague standing on top of the car, phone glued to his ear. Another walks around the compound with his mobile strapped to a tall pole while a third perches on a tree branch. All searching for phone signals to tell their loved ones they're okay, even though they're sleeping on a site that was bombed last November.
Vanity goes out the window in a place like Yida. I haven't looked in the mirror in days. Pass a colleague one morning and he's got toothpaste froth around his mouth. Lick my lips and realize I've been walking around with an instant coffee moustache. Bed head? Just slap on a UNHCR cap until your next shower. There are people I wouldn't recognize without their caps.
Modesty is passé when you have to relieve yourself in a hole in the ground that's buzzing with flies or when a shower means cupping cold water from a bucket over your head, which is pointless because you get sweaty again after five minutes. Some do away with the formalities completely, earning nicknames like "Dirty Fred."
Only one man - our security officer - hasn't let himself go. He puts hot coal in an old iron and starts pressing his T-shirts over a carefully folded UNHCR blanket. Maybe it has nothing to do with pride. Maybe he's trying to kill the eggs of water-borne worms or flies that could burrow under his skin.
I don't know which scares me more - the thought of maggots crawling under my skin, or the risk of bombs falling in Yida. I'm lucky that I can pop in and out, and admire my colleagues for: Their decision to live close to a volatile border because the refugees don't want to move inland; their tolerance for the same diet of rice and beans every day. Some seem to live on cigarettes and coffee alone; their generosity with toilet paper and bottled water, only available an hour's drive away; their stoicism in the face of diarrhoea; their nonchalance towards fat shiny green-eyed flies buzzing out of their latrines; the audacity to wear flip-flops in scorpion-infested terrain; the gift of sleeping through long bumpy drives with dignity intact, mouths and knees closed; and their humour in rough times.
In short, UNHCR field work at its best.

Original_Search_Title: None Dead in Abuja Blast - Eyewitnesses
Headline: Nigeria: None Dead in Abuja Blast - Eyewitnesses
Body_Text:
An explosion outside the Park and Shop plaza along Aminu Kano Crescent in Wuse 2, Abuja Tuesday night left several wounded but none dead, eyewitnesses have said.
Eyewitnesses told Daily Trust that parking space in front of the plaza is used as a 'loading point' for taxi drivers and a few of them were still waiting for passengers when the explosion occurred.
The explosion dug a crater that appeared to have been about one meter in diameter. The crater has been filled by rescue and emergency agencies. The bomb was placed under a tree outside the perimeter fence of the plaza.
"I had just closed from my shop in Banex plaza some minutes after eight and was walking down to the junction. Suddenly I heard a loud blast behind me and my ears began to ring loudly. I did not know when I ran across the road, away from the sound of the blast. I could see smoke and dust near Banex plaza," an eyewitness who pleaded anonymity told Daily Trust.
A few minutes later, he and several others would pick up courage and approach the site of the blast.
"We stayed on the other side of the road though. We were still afraid to go near the building. I saw several people with injuries but none died. Later soldiers and mobile policemen came and cordoned off the road. There was glass everywhere on the road. It is only this morning that the glass on the road was swept that is why you cannot see any on the road now," he said.
A security guard on duty on Wednesday morning told Daily Trust that his colleagues who were on duty Tuesday night were about going in to sleep when the blast occurred.
"Our oga immediately called us that were not on duty and told us to report immediately to the plaza. This was around 9:30pm. Those we met on duty say they did not know what happened. They just heard the explosion and they all ran for safety," he said.
Another eyewitness who gave his name as Emma said he was standing not far from the building when the bomb went off.
"I was selling my bananas as usual. All of a sudden I heard a loud explosion. I threw my bananas away and ran for cover. For a while I could not hear anything and it seemed everywhere around me was spinning. It was later I discovered that I did not sustain any injury. Many people were injured but nobody died," he told Daily Trust.

Original_Search_Title: A Bleak Future in Perpetuity for Kiir's 'Banana' Republic of South Sudan?
Headline: South Sudan: A Bleak Future in Perpetuity for Kiir's 'Banana' Republic of South Sudan?
Body_Text:
QUOTE: "If the money is eaten by a few, all the rest will go hungry, and a hungry citizen is an angry citizen. This is as true in the Counties and States as it is in Juba. ..in order to fight this endemic disease in the courts we must have the laws to do so. Until these laws exist, our hands are tied in fighting corruptees, while they betray our commitment to serve and provide services to our long-suffering people." Pres. Kiir at Governors Forum, Juba, OCT.,02/2008
Our nation's First Independence Anniversary arrives sadly with President Kiir's perilously steering the country down the path which many typically failed states in Africa have gone before.
And like many of these African countries that are still wallowing in the abyss of bad governance and abject poverty, South Sudan has outstandingly set the worst record as a top corrupt country in the shortest time ever.
However, unlike many of these countries which embarked on independence with little or nothing, South Sudan, regardless of what Kiir's apologists might erroneously assert, didn't start from "scratch," its treasury was awash with blissful abundance of billions of real American dollars.
Furthermore, unlike Congo which achieved Independence in 1960 with only on engineer, South Sudan had already qualified pool of technocrats, professors, civil servants, UN experts, etc.. which Kiir injudiciously sidelined in favor of illiterate soldiers and unqualified fellow tribesmen and women.
Acceptably, the Kiir SPLM/A regime started on a wrong footing, which thus accelerated its descent into the situation of a 'banana republic' that became totally dependent on a single resource (oil) and governed by corrupt thugs.
Looking back to the one year of total independence, the majority of the citizens of the newest nation see no exhilaration to celebrate a hollow ceremony.
Painfully, they've become less assured of the prosperity that they had rightly hoped would come with independence for which they made tremendous sacrifice.
Kiir's legacy as leader of the new nation will be remembered for the calamity he has effectuated that includes massive corruption blatantly perpetuated by top SPLM/A leaders.
Tragically, after eight years in power, "with his hands still tied," Kiir has abysmally failed to persecute not a single suspect despite personally being cognizant of more than 75 'thieves.'
This blame is equally shared by a utterly compromised parliament which can't pass the needed legislation that would expedite the arrest and prosecution of the 4-billion-dollar thieves.
Typical of failed banana republics, our attenuated parliamentarians pay more allegiance to president Kiir personally than to the nation
More inimical, however, is Kiir's absolute failure to achieve pacification and resolution of the ceaseless inter- and intra-tribal slaughter, often of genocidal proportions like the ever recurrent Nuer and Dinka against Murle or vice versa.
Thus, since SPLM/A assumed power in 2005, our nation has been bleeding unforgivably despite the nation having a massively overblown army (SPLA) and other security organs, all consuming nearly half the national budget.
By persistently avoiding constituting the often-called-for Truth and Reconciliation Commission which in essence would have led to the resolution of these tribal aggressions, president Kiir and his SPLM/A party will be held responsible for the ongoing tribal killings.
This disharmonious aggressiveness has unfortunately even trickled down to students who're as easily agitated to fight mini-wars against other tribes, as disgracefully exhibited at the national Juba University, a foreboding development for so-called future leaders.
More puzzling for the young nation is that nearly seven years after signing the peace accord (CPA), multiple anti-SPLM/A rebels still operate in those states of Jonglei and Unity, and instead of resolving holistically the problems causing these rebellions, we see the Kiir regime naively reintegrating piecemeal these rebel ranks into the SPLA.
The future is even more bleak and uncertain in regards to the projected rehabilitation of the oil industry as there are inexplicable complications: will South Sudan build 1, 2 or 3 pipelines and will the oil be wholly or partially exported through Kenya, Ethiopia or Djibouti?
Another related issue aggravating the economic woes facing the peoples is the total domination of the economy by foreigners emanating mainly from lawless and failed states of Somalia, Eritrea and Ethiopia along with unscrupulous traders from Lebanon and East Africa, all colluding with corrupt officials to under develop South Sudan.
Finally, if our nation is to progress in peace, all outstanding land grabbing or territorial expansionist polices by other belligerent tribes against other so-called non-dominant tribes must cease immediately otherwise we shall never achieve national cohesion.
In light of the inevitable economic mess brought about by a messy 'kirronomics,' one wonders how much the common man and woman will bear in the second year of independence.
Yes, we're now a free country, but in our long struggle that cost more than 4 millions lives in totality, South Sudanese had hopefully anticipated that the freedom gained would translate not into more suffering in perpetuity but into tangible dividends.

Original_Search_Title: Bank Gives Sh25 Billion for City Facelift
Headline: Kenya: Bank Gives Sh25 Billion for City Facelift
Body_Text:
THE Ministry of Nairobi Metropolitan Development has been awarded more than Sh25 billion by world bank to construct roads and put up street lights in the city. Minister Jamleck Kamau made the announcement on Saturday in Karuri Village in Banana, Kiambu during an inspection tour of the roads in the area. He said the money will also rehabilitate the city's sewerage system alongside the roads and street lights.
Kamau added that the tenders will be awarded to local contractors. "We now have to recognize and give jobs to our own contractors rather than outsourcing from China since they are now proving themselves that they can deliver good services to the country," said Kamau. The minister said that the ministry is yet to identify the areas and projects that they will channel the money to within the metropolitan area and improve services.
He said that the ministry is behind schedule in installing CCTV cameras within Nairobi and on 19th July tendering will start and evaluation will be done as quickly as possible. "The installation process will be done in three phases first in the central business area then further away from the CBD and then cover the whole of the metropolitan area," he said.
The minister said that they want to have new technology and thats where they are working towards. He said street lights will be powered by solar energy since the electricity cost incurred have become a heavy burden for the local overnment. "We want to have lighting which at the end of the day does not require paying of bills," said Kamau.

Original_Search_Title: N$95 Million Question Not Answered - Swanu
Headline: Namibia: N$95 Million Question Not Answered - Swanu
Body_Text:
RESPONDERS to questions about the transfer of N$95 million from the Ministry of Works and Transport into a TransNamib account were evasive and shifted responsibility, said Swanu deputy secretary Unaani Kauami.
Swanu MP Usutuaije Maamberua wanted Works Minister Erkki Nghimtina, Finance Minister Saara Kuugongelwa-Amadhila and Prime Minister Nahas Angula to say why the transfer was made to TransNamib, and what the procedures were for the transfer.
The purpose of Maamberua's questioning, said Kauami, was to ascertain whether the rule of law and principle of legality were followed.
He said the transfer was made despite an audit report indicating weak financial controls at TransNamib.
"It is only after realising the intention of the question and the implications that the honourable members politely declined to answer the questions," Kauami said. "The cardinal questions were not answered."
Among these, he said, was how much money was in the infrastructure development account of TransNamib on April 7, and who had made the transfer - the Permanent Secretary of Finance, Permanent Secretary of Works, or somebody else.
Furthermore, said Kauami, the rules and regulations governing the contingency fund from which the transfer was made, were not provided.
The party dismissed the assertion made by Kuugongelwa-Amadhila that the transfer was made in the national interest, saying that disbursement of public funds must be done in accordance with the law.
"[Otherwise] we are encouraging a banana republic and anarchy that is inconsistent with our constitutional dispensation anchored on the principle of the rule of law," Kauami said.

Original_Search_Title: Artists Write to UN Leader Ban Ki-Moon on 'A Matter of Life and Death'
Headline: Africa: Artists Write to UN Leader Ban Ki-Moon on 'A Matter of Life and Death'
Body_Text:
International artists including Keira Knightley, Yoko Ono, Scarlett Johansson, Coldplay, Tim Roth, Annie Lennox and Kevin Spacey have joined forces to demand governments take immediate action to help improve regulation of the international arms trade.
Later today, representatives from more than 190 governments meet in New York for the first day of a month-long negotiation at the United Nations to agree a Arms Trade Treaty, which will control the supply of weapons, ammunition and armaments.
An effective Arms Trade Treaty would be based on a "Golden Rule": if there is a substantial risk that arms exported to another country are likely to be used for serious human rights abuses; violations of international humanitarian law; or to undermine sustainable development, those arms supplies must be stopped.
More than 30 high-profile Oxfam and Amnesty International supporters including Paul Conroy - the British war photographer injured in the mortar attack that killed Sunday Times reporter Marie Colvin and French photo journalist Remi Ochlik, in Homs, Syria, earlier this year - have urged governments to deliver a strong and effective treaty that helps protect human rights by preventing the flow of arms to irresponsible users.
The letter has been sent to the UN Secretary-General, who will play a vital role in the negotiations, by keeping the talks on track to deliver a strong treaty that will really save lives.
The letter states:
"Every year an average of two bullets for every person on this planet is produced. With so few global rules governing the arms trade, no one really knows where all those bullets will end up - or whose lives they will tear apart.
"Under the current system, there are less global controls on the sales of ammunition and guns than on bananas and bottled water. It's a ridiculous situation.
"As supporters of Amnesty International and Oxfam, we urge governments to step forward and deliver a robust, effective treaty that protects human rights. A treaty that puts a stop to the needless deaths and injuries which occur every day as a result of armed violence and conflict. The decisions taken around this treaty really are a matter of life and death."
The deadly and poorly regulated trade in arms leads to serious human rights abuses, armed violence, conflict, poverty and organized crime around the world.
The lack of clear binding principles governing decisions on international arms transfers combined with patchy diverse and poorly implemented national regulations are inadequate to deal with the increasingly globalized nature of the arms trade. As a result, irresponsible users are allowed to violate international humanitarian and human rights law.
Colombian rock band Los Aterciopelados said: "In Colombia, for decades civilians have been victims of armed conflict. Displacement and violence are everyday occurrences. The ceaseless flow of arms fuels this terrible reality. But this isn't just the story of our country. It's vital that leaders implement a strong Arms Trade Treaty. It is urgently needed that they control arms to protect human rights."
Anna Macdonald, Oxfam's Head of Control Arms, said: "This impressive range of public figures from many walks of life urging governments to sign-up to a robust treaty this month underlines the importance of the treaty negotiations.
"This is a critical moment. The world has never before agreed to have a set of international rules on the arms trade. This is a chance of a lifetime, a chance of a generation, to make a difference by stopping the trade flows of arms going into the wrong hands."
Brian Wood, Amnesty International's Manager of Arms Control, Security Trade and Human Rights, said: "These are big voices echoing a global civil society message to world leaders that is clear: seize this historic opportunity to agree a Golden Rule to make a strong arms trade treaty that can stamp out irresponsible arms trading, stop shattering the lives of millions and help protect everyone's human rights."
The full list of signatories includes:
Gillian Anderson Actor, USA.
Los Aterciopelados Musicians, Colombia.
Harry Belafonte Singer, songwriter, actor and social activist, USA.
Gael Garcia Bernal Actor, Mexico.
Paul Bettany Actor, UK.
Jane Birkin Actor/Singer, UK.
Miguel Bose Singer, Spain.
Rahul Bose Actor, India.
Helena Christensen Photographer and model, Denmark.
Chris Martin, Guy Berryman, Jonny Buckland, Will Champion Members of Coldplay - Rock band, UK.
Jennifer Connelly Actor, USA.
Paul Conroy War photographer, UK.
Sophie Dahl Writer and model, UK.
Kristin Davis Actor, USA.
Andrea Echiverri Musician, singer and guitarist, Colombia.
Los Fabulosos Cadillacs Musicians, Argentina.
Livia Firth Film Producer and Eco-Age Creative Director, UK.
Anjelica Huston Actor, USA.
Eddie Izzard Comedian, actor and writer, UK.
Bianca Jagger Chair of Bianca Jagger Human Rights Foundation, UK.
Emmanuel Jal Musician and activist, South Sudan
Scarlett Johansson Actor, USA.

Keira Knightley Actor, UK.
