According to the LA Act, a town council will appoint a CEO after the minister had been consulted. There is no phrase that the minister needs to approve the appointment, the current council is arguing.
In the meantime, Ganaseb says that Ekandjo "has no power" over his appointment or removal, and that it was all in the hands of the council.
"If he had the power to do so this would be a banana republic. It's the council who appointed me. And I will only be willing to leave if they pay me out the full term," Ganaseb told The Namibian.
The town council will have an extra-ordinary meeting today to discuss Ekandjo's demands.
The Namibian was unable to reach the Minister for further comments yesterday.

Original_Search_Title: Kazooba's Music Set His School a Class Apart
Headline: Uganda: Kazooba's Music Set His School a Class Apart
Body_Text:
When I arrived at Nyakisoroza trading centre, a five-year-old volunteered to lead me to the school where a teacher has become the darling of an entire village because of music.
Emmanuel Kazooba is the head teacher at Nyakisoroza Primary School.
He has held the position since 2008 and with him, also came his love for music.
He has almost single-handed seen the school win district music, dance and drama festivals three years in a row. In 2011, Nyakisoroza represented Rukungiri district at the regional music festival in Mbarara, and won the competetion. A fete the school won.
Unfortunately, the school failed to make it to Kampala for the national event due to lack of funds from Rukungiri local government.
Kazooba's love for music began at a tender age, but it was only in his P.6, when he watched a conductor lead a choir that he got made a decision to join.
This was at Bubaare Primary School where that year's schools music, dance and drama competition was held.
"I was mesmerised by the way John Kasigazi conducted the choir. The costumes also, which included banana fibres and traditional instruments, really got me thinking of music; they were in perfect harmony," he says.
Born on June 6, 1971, Kazooba started school at Bubaare Primary School.
Thereafter, he attended Bubaare Senior Secondary School for O-Level, which he completed in 1986.
He, however, dropped out of school for nine years due to an ailment doctors had failed to diagnose.
Luckily for him, he lived to tell the tale and in 1995, upon recovery, enrolled at Rukungiri Primary Teachers College where he graduated with a Grade III certificate in 1997.
Kazooba is uncomfortable talking about his family largely because he does not really know his parents and he was brought up by guardians. Although he has never seen his father, he recently reunited with his aging mother.
From 1998 to 2007 he was a teacher at Kahoko Primary School and during that time he upgraded to a diploma in education at the National Teacher's College, Kabale, majoring in English and music.
"During the time I was at college I paid attention to music, but I did not compose much then, but after college, I combined the school choir and local music clubs to entertain residents at weddings and public functions," he said.
In 2010 he composed the Nyakisoroza school anthem, making Nyakisoroza one of the first primary school in the county to have its own anthem, the composition has since earned Kazooba superstar status.
"Whenever we sing our school anthem we feel elated because we stand out from the rest," Jonathan Birungi a Primary Seven pupil said.
Justus Birungi the chairman of the school management committee is also full of praise for Kazooba.
"Kazooba's first order of business when he came here in 2008 was to set up a school choir. It is this choir that has made the school stand out. Many pupils now want to come here from other schools to learn music," says Birungi.
Birungi adds that Kazooba does not wait to get fancy costumes and modern instruments so as to teach the pupils; he improvises with local materials.
The school's Parent's Teacher's Association chairperson, Penina Tumushabe, intimated that the school never got a facilitation grant to erect new structures, so there was an exodus of pupils to schools that had new buildings.
But when Kazooba lifted the school through music, pupils have started returning. The enrollment has since increased from 350 in 2007 to the current 640.
Kazooba has cut a niche for himself and the school in that whenever there is an opportunity to perform at a public event, he gets first priority.
"People just cannot get enough of us. Whenever we are performing, they do not leave until we are done," Lilian Natukunda, a pupil in P.6, said.
The father of three said pupils should not look down upon music because it has become a leading income earner to many the world over. He said pupils will take time to appreciate music, especially if they are not exposed to modern music instruments.
"There is big potential among the pupils. The Ministry of Education should consider giving music instruments to schools, which prioritise music, dance and drama," he suggested.
Kazooba is grateful to Alice Kayabuki who paid his school fees from Primary One to the time he joined Primary Teachers' College and his wife who helps him with composing songs.

Original_Search_Title: Kiambu Paedophile Gets Life Sentence
Headline: Kenya: Kiambu Paedophile Gets Life Sentence
Body_Text:
A middle aged ex- convict was yesterday sentenced to life by a Kiambu court for defiling a three-year-old girl. Stephen Gitatu Njoki was sentenced by principal magistrate Charity Oluoch after being found guilty of defiling the minor at Kahawa West estate in Kasarani on May 24, 2011.
The court heard that the accused who then sold bananas along the newly constructed northern by pass and had before served a jail term, defiled the minor while seated on a wheelbarrow where he had made the girl sit on his laps with her dress pushed up and her pants pulled down.
The girl's mother who witnessed in court said that when she prepared lunch on that material day she went out to call her children and that is when she found her child with the accused.
She called out for help where an administration police patrolling the china constructed bypass came to her aid. The accused was arrested and taken to Kiamumbi police station where the complainant recorded a statement.
The minor was later taken to Nairobi Women Hospital for medical examination and treatment. A report presented in court indicted that examination by police surgeon Zephaniah Kamau and Dr. Thuo from Nairobi women hospital confirmed that the accused had defiled the minor.

Original_Search_Title: On the Shores of the River Niger
Headline: Nigeria: On the Shores of the River Niger
Body_Text:
There is a lot of cleaning up to be done at the Onitsha end of the iconic River Niger.
The environment around the river shore is not one anybody interested in aesthetic beauty would like to behold. The garbage heaps dotting the stretch of Osse Ogbe Ijaw to the foot of the famous River Niger Bridge - a distance of over two kilometres - ironically forms the embankment to the river.
These, along with shanty wooden kiosks which serve as homes to a great many of the inhabitants of this area, menial economic activities ranging from the hawking of sachet water to vending of food in plastic containers to the local gin popularly called "sapele water" to the whiff of Indian hemp, are a pointer to class of Nigerians that live on the shores of the Onitsha end of the Niger River.
The river, according to elementary geography, was discovered by Mungo Park many years ago and has its source in Guinea. It passes through Mali, Niger, Benin and Nigeria with the River Benue in Nigeria as its major tributary. It is reported to be the principal river in western Africa, extending about 4,180 kilometres and with a drainage basin of 2, 117, 700 square kilometres, discharging through a massive delta otherwise known as the Niger Delta into the gulf of Guinea and the Atlantic ocean.
Like many rivers in Africa, the Niger is brownish especially during the rainy season when flood water and other debris would have emptied into it, and it shrinks in size during the dry season. The Onitsha end of the river is actually its estuary having joined the Atlantic Ocean in Rivers state through its stretch in Ogbaru council area of Anambra State.
But life within the Onitsha shores of the river is indeed an eye sore. As earlier noted, various menial economic activities take place within the stretch which is generally filthy. There are no toilet facilities within the dry land bordering the river and residents - both old and young defecate into the river and also take their bath there. Besides, the environment's filthiness as it were, the whole area stinks.
Yet, within the several kiosks that dot the area, serving as both home and business premises where mainly fish, hemp, gin and poorly prepared food are sold, residents go about acting as if all is normal. Very young boys and girls are seen hawking banana, sachet water and groundnuts on trays from place to place in the heat of the sun.
It is not clear if they go to school since the hawking is done when schools are supposed to be in session. And they have a distinct character of suspecting anyone in their midst they are not familiar with including even the police personnel on patrol at the foot of the bridge.
At the Osse Ogbe Ijaw stretch, this reporter had the bitter experience of being man-handled by four fierce-looking young men whose ages range from 25 to 30 for daring to come into their world and making inquiries about the leadership of the various groups of artisans and or land excavators doing brisk business there. Their breath on close contact oozed with the stench of marijuana. They even seized the camera of an independent camera man hired for the purpose of taking pictures. It took the intervention of policemen on patrol in the area to get the equipment released with a warning that shots should not be taken.
With that sorted out, none of the residents of the area volunteered information on their activities. THISDAY Life gathered that the young men were hostile because the stretch of land had been a subject of dispute between a certain "big man" who bought over the place from the Anambra State government three years ago, thereby displacing some lowly-placed individuals whose kiosks from where they eke out a living were demolished. Three people it was learnt died in the fracas then.
However, a middle-aged man who gave his name as Emeka later on told THISDAY that though they had a company licensed to excavate land from the river but that he would not say more than that because their "Oga" travelled out of town. He said we should make out time to arrange an interview with him upon his possible return to town next week.
Isiadinso Okoye who spoke to THISDAY from his dilapidated fishing boat on the river bank said they normally paddle from Omambala river in Otuocha, a tributary of the River Niger in Anambra East Council Area of the state to Osse Ogbe Ijaw to sell fish caught in the course of their fishing expedition, saying that residents of Onitsha come to the river shore to buy from there. He said that they could sell a fish for as high as N700 or less depending on the size and can make as much as N3000 or N4000.
Another resident of the area who spoke tongue-in-cheek and on condition of anonymity said they would like the state governor to direct the state Environment Sanitation Agency (ANSEPA) Onitsha office to come and help clear the heaps of garbage in the area and improve environmental sanitation therein.
He said he would also like a situation where government can provide public conveniences and a primary health centre, adding that people defecate into the river, bath in it and at times cook with the water and that these things have far-reaching health implications for the residents of the area.
There were also regular policemen on patrol the foot of the Niger River Bridge and the crescent running form the National Inland Waterways Authority, Onitsha Area office, to the "Abada" market down to Uga junction. One of the officers told THISDAY on condition of anonymity that they were from the Area Command Unit, Onitsha and were on surveillance of the area to checkmate the activities of armed robbers and kidnappers. He said their job was routine and that at night another batch of policemen from Fegge police station in Onitsha will also patrol the area.

Original_Search_Title: 'Prophet One' Goes Bananas 'I'm Even Going to Insult More'
Headline: Ghana: 'Prophet One' Goes Bananas 'I'm Even Going to Insult More'
Body_Text:
Kumasi — The Founder and Leader of the Ebenezer Miracle Worship Centre, Rev. Opambour Ebenezer Adarkwa Yiadom, and entire staff of his radio station, Mercury 91.5FM, have been knocking their heads against walls, cursing The Chronicle and staff of the paper over the publication made by the paper last week Friday.
Following the story which was published in the Friday edition of the paper, in which the "Man of God" was reported to have thrown a barrage of insults on a colleague pastor, "Prophet One," as he wishes to be referred to, and his staff have been muddying the waters the more.
The unrepentant "Man of God," who is fast gaining notoriety as a 'chief of insults," chose to abandon the word of God, and take on the title of 'King of Insults' with all the venom he could spit on the paper, the reporter, and all those who work on The Chronicle. He also launched verbal attacks on all people who hold contrary views to his.
With his lieutenants in full backing, the so-called "Prophet One" boasted: "I am even going to insult more. What I did was just a tip of the iceberg, let's wait and see."
The controversial pastor and his staff did not understand why the paper chose to capture in full the side of Prophet One, and gave little account of what Evangelist Akwasi Awuah, his opponent, might have said.
The crew of Mercury 91.5FM morning show, apparently taking a cue from the action of their boss, did not even take the trouble to read the story to really find out what account had been given of their opponent, Evangelist Awuah, but decided to mount a defence for their pay master.
'Prophet One', on Thursday, went berserk on his radio station, launching a verbal attack on a colleague pastor, Akwasi Awuah, who also features on a local radio station, Boss 93.7FM. In the heat of the Man of God's anger, he hurled various insults on his pastor colleague, questioning his right to insult him (Prophet One).
"Who are you? Small boy, yet you insult at will. You insult one pastor to the other. Are you mad? If he is mad, then let him restrict his madness to his household," Prophet One charged.
The man of God, in his full element, further charged on the producers of the radio show on which his opponent launched the attacks on him.
"...and to the producer who is encouraging the insults on my personality, I will pray to destroy your job."
"As for him, leave him alone, akpeteshie (local gin) has already destroyed him," one of Prophet One's young pastors interjected.
"I will put your destiny in the toilet," he charged on. What seems to have caused much discomfort to the man of God seem are the allegations that he is an occultist.
"Can anyone point to any spiritualist who I have consulted?" he queried. He also flaunted his wealth and the number of people he had helped financially.
The founder and leader of the Ebenezer Miracle Worship Centre and others like him, including Bishop Obinim, leader of the International Way Gospel Church, now facing trial for allegedly destroying equipment belonging to an Accra radio station, have become a source of concern for Ghanaian Christendom, with many questioning their real spiritual motives.
The Christian Council of Ghana and other Christian organisations have, on several platforms, expressed their displeasure at the behaviour of some pastors running one-man churches, complaining that their actions were derailing the good works of other credible Christians in the country.

Original_Search_Title: Don Jazzy's New Album Without D'Banj - a Review
Headline: Nigeria: Don Jazzy's New Album Without D'Banj - a Review
Body_Text:
Don Jazzy no doubt is a man who keeps his word; so when he announced yesterday that he would be releasing a compilation album that will hold new works from his Marvins- Tiwa Savage, Dr. Sid, Wande Coal and D'Prince titled Solar Plexus today, this writer has been on the ready and in the coming paragraphs takes a curious yet critical look at this album that comes barely two months after D'banj left the pack to take on the international music market.
Solar Plexus ordinarily means that part of the abdomen including the stomach that is particularly vulnerable to the effects of a blow; so maybe Don jazzy is trying to say this album will literally knock the wind out of you - entertainment wise that is.
The album compilation holds twelve tracks with Tiwa Savage disturbingly doing only one track out of the twelve while Dr Sid, Wande Coal and D'Prince enjoy three each for themselves. This may be due to the fact that she is a recent sign on to the label and the whole compilation had been put together in a rush with Don Jazzy being under intense pressure to be out quickly with his label.
Solar plexus has I'm a Marvin presumably done by all four but then it is difficult to determine when each male Marvin individually takes on the song as the versus had gone the same way both melody and voice- tone wise. Only Tiwa Savage who thankfully is a female had broken the male monotony with her rich, sensual voice. This is noticeably different and perhaps not up to what the Mo'hits all stars had hit us with in 2007
However, the track I am a Marvin exudes a new music sense and style quite unlike what Don Jazzy has ever produced before. Anyone with good music ears and love for perfect harmony will enjoy the number but then if you are inpatient and already quivering to get into a dancing feat then you maybe be moving on to other tracks.
In Solar Plexus, Tiwa Savage has Oma Ga to her credit, Wande Coal drops See me ri, Forever, and Pretty girls, Dr Sid Yolo, CPR and Chocolate while D'Prince finishes with Take Banana, Why you over there and Amarachi. All four Marvins do their songs individually and independently. They do not jump in to help each other at choruses or bridges save for the Producer himself Don Jazzy who comes in occasional yet expectedly to do vocal backup.
In all, the album is tight. But then there may be moments when the up beat tempo maintained in one track drops considerably in the next track. The sound and production quality is on point but still not ahead of what had been in the Mo'hits era. This album no doubt will have hits that will rock clubs and parties for months to come but then one reality stares really hard in the face - D'banj is sorely missed in this compilation.

Original_Search_Title: Paul Kagame - You Can Not Build Democracy on Sand
Headline: Rwanda: Paul Kagame - You Can Not Build Democracy on Sand
Body_Text:
As the President of a small poor country whose economic and social progress is indisputable, the Head of State elaborates on his concept of good governance, and reveals the two sides of him: both democratic and authoritarian.
It's like a bad film, one that is impossible to produce here because the actors, the director and the audience don't exist
Power has not made Paul Kagame any heavier, literally and figuratively. One morning in the Eastern Province, a hundred kilometres from Kigali, in front of 50,000 people who descended from all the surrounding hills to attend his meeting, the President dressed in black jeans and blue shirt keenly listens to the voice of the people. Behind a microphone, a crowd of ordinary Rwandans form a procession to ask him questions: issues of cows, land, compensation, minor conflicts with the administration, lyrical poems chanted in Kinyarwanda, poorly healed wounds of the genocide ... Kagame responds when he does not call the Mayor, the deputy, Madame the Governor or the Minister concerned to answer in his place.
The latter do not seem at ease, as the boss demands that they be concise, clear and convincing. At 54, the man Time magazine ranks among the hundred most influential people in the world and American universities are competing to invite for their inaugural lectures, whose eldest son is studying at West Point Military Academy and whose only hobbies are tennis and reading books on development economics, continues, twelve years after his accession to the presidency, to lead Rwanda Inc. as a mwalimu - a teacher, in Swahili - leads a class where each student is seated in front of a computer. Here everything is calm, order, vigilance. No noisy music to be heard from ngandas as in neighbouring DR Congo, no screaming people drunk with beer or banana wine, everyone is at home, quiet and busy working to an unreasonable degree.
Under the guise of peace and a security that is ubiquitous, invisible and meticulously organised, the memory of the genocide is everywhere all the time. Often, the casual visitor feels crushed under the weight of the dead and the unspoken. The faces are serious and bursts of laughter rare. Diligently transforming the demons of the past into creative and productive energy with a hardness that ignores divergent views of the opposition or the whims of NGOs, that is the challenge Paul Kagame is faced with. Judging by the rather brilliant socioeconomic outcomes, achieved by a country that has become a model in this field, it is poised to succeed. Western-style democracy will have to wait...
On July 1st, independent Rwanda turns 50. Is there something to celebrate?
It depends on what you mean by that. It's our history, and we must own it, with its share of good events and tragedies. It will be a day for reflection, not an occasion for festivities or uncontrolled public spending. I know elsewhere on the continent the fiftieth anniversary is often celebrated with pomp and rejoicing, but we do not feel obliged to do the same. In general, for obvious reasons of cost, this date is also combined with another very close one on July 4th, which marks the anniversary of Rwanda's liberation from genocidal forces. This year, both events will be commemorated on July 1st, with sobriety.
Especially since 1 July 1962 was not a day of joy for all Rwandans...
Yes indeed. That day will therefore be placed in its historical context, with no spirit of revenge.
It is also an opportunity to reflect on what democracy is in the Rwandan context. For you, obviously, the construction of a strong state, able to overcome community conflicts, takes priority over the Western-style model of democracy. Am I wrong?
Yes and no. Before being a concept applicable everywhere, democracy must first be a reality. It must come from the people, how they lead their daily lives, in the way they manage and conduct their lives. Democracy is not an abstract theory; it is the product of a context. Look around you: there is no unique form of democracy but different democratic systems, ranging from constitutional monarchies to direct popular representation. Democracy must conform to the aspirations, to the history and culture of the people among whom it claims to take root; otherwise it is doomed to failure. This is what we're trying to do in Rwanda. Rwanda is neither France nor Great Britain or Belgium.
In Africa, democracy cannot be decreed, it has to be built, it has to be prepared...
Say that there is a universal model of democracy; let's call it the Western model, if it suits you. In any case, the aspirations of Rwandans are not, in terms of freedoms, representativeness and prosperity, different from those of other people. But a model is not the same as ready-to-wear. It must adapt to the wearer. Again, we have our own history, our own traditions and a particular social organization that the tragedies of the last century, including the genocide, have harshly traumatized and abused. All these features bring about a rate of construction and a democratic architecture of our own. Sometimes the Rwandan society is described as a sheep-like, blind society, led with a whip by a leadership straight from 1984's George Orwell. This is stupid. I am not a doctor issuing mandatory orders to the people on what to do or not do by holding them by the throat. Those outsiders who depict us as a kind of anthill with no brain do not respect us and do not deserve our respect. We are open to advice on democratic development, provided this is in good faith, but we do not like the prescriptions, let alone orders.
Rwanda is often criticized for the way the authority sees the role of the media. For you, do the media have to echo the collective achievements and mobilize public opinion in favour of the state, rather than educate people to think critically and independently? Are you afraid of freedom of the press?
Absolutely not. Just read some Rwandan newspapers in Kinyarwanda to realize how the image of a government muzzling the press is wrong. Criticism is frequent. Excess or even insults, are recurrent there. I told you that we do not like prescriptions: there are none here beyond the limits set by law common to all democracies, which penalizes defamation or the promotion of genocide ideology. Otherwise, my opinion on this subject differs from the one you attribute to me. I am convinced that media is a key institution for development, in that they help to go beyond the dogmas through debate and criticism. Everyone, be a Rwandan or a foreigner, is also free to start a newspaper, radio or TV here. The only constraints are those of the market. That said, I'm not naive. As much as I am against the notion of policing and editorial censorship - and you'll find books by genocide deniers on sale openly in Kigali bookstores - I am against this tendency of many media professionals defining in their own terms what to do or not do.
Again, we do not like the prescriptions, whatever their origin. It is not up to the media, much less when they are foreign, whose independence is also relative, to dictate us the way to go.
Your country is regularly praised for its respect of good governance. However, three generals and a colonel are now under judicial investigation for their alleged involvement in traffic of minerals from the DR Congo. Does this not give grounds to the NGOs that accuse you of participating in looting the wealth of your neighbour?
First of all, I do not have to justify myself, much less to respond to NGOs who would be better off examining their own conscience with regard to their role during and after the genocide. Secondly, this case demonstrates the opposite of what you are suggesting. It is precisely because we have zero tolerance when it comes to fighting against corruption, and because the Rwanda Defense Forces adhere to the most rigorous moral and professional standards in the world, that this investigation was opened. Finally, do not expect me to give detailed comments about a process that belongs only to the justice system.
Several leaders of the Rwandan opposition in exile, including your former director of cabinet Théogène Rudasingwa, and also former senior officials of the army, now refugees in South Africa, were leaders that were very close to you. How do you explain that they somehow betrayed you?
They have not betrayed me; they betrayed themselves and betrayed the people of Rwanda. In any case, if some of my colleagues are not up to the task of the mission with which they were entrusted, I shouldn't be blamed for it. I trust, I delegate, but I demand accountability. I verify and sanction shortcomings. Some cannot tolerate this and choose to leave rather than face up to their responsibilities. It's human nature... There's no need to go into the criminal records of these people: it is public knowledge.
The violence in the arguments used against you is surprising. You are painted as a kind of monstrous Machiavelli, guilty not only of having ordered the assassination of two presidents - Habyarimana and Kabila - but also the extermination of the Tutsi of Rwanda to reach power. Why do you attract such hatred?
I believe only psychiatry would be of help to us in responding to your question. This is no longer in the domain of the rational. It is not in my area of expertise and I have neither the time nor the desire to enter into the subconscious of sick minds.
There was another grenade attack in Kigali in January. Who is responsible?
The investigation is ongoing. It has led, as in the previous acts of terrorism of this type, to the same network: a connection between the ex-generals in exile in South Africa and the genocidaires of the FDLR [Democratic Forces for the liberation of Rwanda] based in Eastern DRC.
This collaboration between Tutsi and Hutu opposition, as illustrated by the recent rapprochement in Brussels between the Rwandese National Congress and the United Democratic Forces of Victory Ingabire - still detained in Rwanda - does it worry you?
No. It is the sort of thing that happens between people who have no other stand apart from the desire for revenge. But they have absolutely no impact nor influence here.
The Tutsi militia chief, Bosco Ntaganda, who operates in North Kivu, a General in the Congolese army, has been under an International Criminal Tribunal (ICC) mandate for five years. Do you support his arrest?
This is a matter for the DRC and not Rwanda. Two things however: it is vital to take into account the continued volatile context of this region and to understand the potential impact of such an action on the security situation. It may be positive, but it could equally well be very negative, yet this evaluation still hasn't been done. Second thing: my reservations about the functioning and impartiality of the ICC, that I have expressed repeatedly, remain intact
How is your relationship with President Kabila?
Good. We communicate.
Why did you reject the new French Ambassador proposed by Paris?
Let's not personalise issues. It is a decision of government, even though I have ultimate responsibility. On assessment, it appears there were certain details in the CV of the proposed individual that were not suitable. We therefore asked for another name to be proposed. It's a normal procedure, quite routine.
It would seem that your response would be negative no matter the candidate proposed by Alain Juppé.
That is not correct. We are capable of making the distinction between France and its Minister of Foreign Affairs. Our wish to have a relationship of active cooperation and friendship with France remains unchanged.
But still, without an Ambassador, nothing moves. Do you think that if Francois Hollande is elected on 6 May, France and Rwanda will finally have a fresh start?
I don't know Francois Hollande but I remain open to pursue dialogue with the head of state chosen by the French people - whoever that will be.
You were the only African to openly endorse the nomination of the American Jim Yong Kim to head the World Bank. Was the Nigerian candidate Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala not suitable?
Let's be precise. I welcomed Jim Yong Kim's nomination by President Obama and I congratulated him on his election. I happen to know Jim well and he had done remarkable work in Rwanda in the health sector - I wasn't going to hide this fact or the admiration that I have for him. But it doesn't mean that I don't like Okonjo-Iweala, who I also know and who is also very qualified.
Do you find it normal that the World Bank is reserved for an American, the IMF for a European, and Africa is perpetually marginalized in all major international institutions?
No. But I also know that we live in the real world not one of our dreams. The observation you have just made is merely an expression of the balance of power that Africa will only achieve on one condition - that we show a united front. However, we cannot even agree on the position of the chairperson of the African Union Commission.
Who do you choose between Jean Ping and Nkosazana Dlamini-Zuma?
I'll choose the candidate that Africa chooses
Is a coup d'état, like the one that happened in Mali, possible in Rwanda?
Mutinying soldiers leave their barracks to storm the Presidency? It's like a bad film, one that is impossible to produce here because the actors, the director and the audience don't exist. But what happened in Bamako is an example of what I just told you about democracy. Mali was a country with a reputation of democracy, even as such praised by the media and NGOs. However, just like building a house and forgetting the foundation, you cannot build democracy on sand.
Your compatriots shift between admiration and fear of you. Often both at once. Is this fear of you necessary to exercise power?
Certainly not fear. Respect, yes, absolutely. But I strongly doubt that you have carried out a scientific survey to back up your assertion. Do it and you will see that in the judgment of Rwandans, the positive far outweighs what you assume to be fear.
I will not repeat the question on what you intend to do in 2017, at the end of your last mandate...
That's good. I have already answered this a hundred times. So, if you don't believe me, just wait and see.
A Putin-Medvedev scenario, where your successor plays the role of Medvedev, and you that of Putin, would this be science-fiction?
The fact that it has already happened in Russia proves that it is not science-fiction. But when it comes to Rwanda, it is, quite simply, fiction.
This interview was conducted and first published in the French version by Jeune Afrique.

Original_Search_Title: Insecurity - Arewa Youths Back Azazi
Headline: Nigeria: Insecurity - Arewa Youths Back Azazi
Body_Text:
President of Arewa Youth Consultative Forum, AYCF, Alhaji Yerima Shettima has urged Nigerians to stop castigating the National Security Adviser, NSA, Gen. Andrew Azazi(rtd) for blaming Peoples Democratic Party, PDP, for the level of insecurity in the country.
Shettima said rather than berate the NSA, security agencies should look at ways of nipping the insecurity problems in the bud.
Addressing a press conference in Lagos, yesterday, Shettima said that rather than raising eyebrows over the comments, security agencies should endeavour to look at the fundamental issues Azazi raised.
Shettima said: "Azazi's office is not political. He owes a duty to Nigeria. The security issue in Nigeria makes the survival of the nation more important than a political party.
"Nigerians are all aware of comments made during elections by some politicians who felt that because they lost their chances for the Presidency they will make the nation ungovernable."
"We cannot continue to run the nation like a Banana Republic. In other climes, people who make such inflammatory political comments would be picked up for investigations and questioning."

Original_Search_Title: Hungary Based Citizen Activist On His Human Rights Award and Work (with Halifa)
Headline: Gambia: Hungary Based Citizen Activist On His Human Rights Award and Work (with Halifa)
Body_Text:
A Gambian based in Hungary, Gibril Deen, who is a political science specialist and human rights activist, on Saturday, 28 April 2012, held a press conference at the offices of the Daily News on Kombo Sillah Drive, to share with the media his achievements in the field of Human Rights activism in Hungary. He described himself as the first Gambian and first African to win this prestigious award in the fight against racism in the West given by the Hungarian Parliament.
Mr. Deen, who is in the Gambia to attend the 51 Ordinary Session of the African Commission on Human and People's Rights (ACHPR), said he worked with an organization called the Mahatma Gandhi Human Rights organization which was formed in 1992.
According to him, he received this anti-racism award in the 21st century on March 2012 in recognition of his relentless effort to fight against the racism.
He said the aim of the press conference is to inform the Gambian public and the Gambian journalists, in particular, that he was the first Gambian who was awarded with the highest award by the Hungarian Parliament with the anti racism award in the fight against racism in Hungary and together with seven other individuals from the Western countries. He added that he was also delighted when he saw his name as the first African receiving this award.
He also highlighted some of their intervention areas in the fight against racism, noting that different tools are used by their association and one of which is the football matches they organize among and between the different races to encourage cooperation and friendliness and also the introduction of tolerant education in the higher schools.
He explained that when the blacks people are playing among the white folks they used to encounter all types of harassment and embarrassment with the throwing of bananas and other rubbish on them in the field of play even if the former are well in the game. He said the fans display intolerance because of the lack of respect for Black people. This, he said, through their fight against this acts of demonstrated racism the phenomenon has been reducing significantly.
The Hungary based Gambian Human Rights activist also said that they do go to the prison to see the conditions of the people who are the prisons. Most of these people found in the prison, according to him, would prefer to change their status to become refugees so as they will be allowed to stay in the country.
Mr Deen said they monitor the issues of human right violations of people who are in prison regarding their countries of origin so as to enable them get refugee status to stay in Hungary
He further explained that another tool that his organization utilizes in its human rights promotion work is to organize cultural activities in to promote African culture as they celebrate May 24th as African Day.
Mr. Deen revealed that his organisation has been given an Observer Status in the ACHPR in 2006.
He said they are organizing a program to help the victims of
racism through football and to promote tolerance education in young people between the ages of 14 years and above to eliminate the feeling of racism in them.
Sports, he concluded, is one of the tools that they have being using to fight against racism successfully.

Original_Search_Title: Uhuru Urged to Run As Independent Candidate
Headline: Kenya: Uhuru Urged to Run As Independent Candidate
Body_Text:
An aspirant for Kiambaa constituency parliamentary seat has advised Uhuru Kenyatta to unify Kenyans by vying for the presidency as an independent candidate.
Evans Gichia, who is the co-ordinator of G7 activities in Kiambaa constituency, said only an independent candidate will unite the country. Gichia cautioned Uhuru that MPs from Central may not join him in his new political vehicle. He was addressing leaders and Wananchi during Labour Day celebrations at Banana Hill, Kiambaa Constituency.
He said most MPs do no want to join new political parties because they risk losing their seats. Aspirants are required by the constitution to join a party at least three months before the elections and current MPs are not exempted. Gichia told Uhuru that most parties are tribal or regional. Currently no political party can Kanu in its hey days when the former ruling party has supporters across the country.
He said if Uhuru joins any of the current parties, he will find it difficult to attract genuine supporters from regions outside Central. Gichia at the same time castigated the registrar of political parties for registering the Party of Independent Candidates of Kenya saying the party's name is confusing Kenyans since under the new constitution "independent candidates" cannot be members of any political party. Gichia said the registration of PICK should be reversed or the party be compelled to change it's name permanently to correct the anomaly.

Original_Search_Title: Little Akoth On Road to Recovery
Headline: Uganda: Little Akoth On Road to Recovery
Body_Text:
On January 16, 2012, Health&Beauty  published a story titled, "Four-year-old haunted by strange disease".  Gertrude Akoth, the little girl who was featured in the story, can now  sit — thanks to her sympathisers who ran to her rescue.
The orphan's buttocks had been eaten up, leaving two deep holes. Akoth had struggled with the ailment amidst abject poverty in Maluku slums, deep inside Tororo municipality.
Before Akoth's mother succumbed to HIV early in 2011, she took Akoth to hospital where she was given creams and tablets, but her condition did not improve.
A compassionate neighbour, Rosemary Nekesa, took on guardianship of Akoth after her mother's death. However, the banana vendor, who has other children to look after, could not afford to pay for Akoth's medical bills on her meagre income.
At the time of the publishing the story, Akoth had suffered from the condition for a year.
Luckily, Good Samaritans came to her rescue. A cancer charitable foundation picked Akoth and Nekesa from Tororo and took her to Kampala for treatment.
Akoth was taken to Mulago where she was diagnosed with three conditions; spina bifida (a birth defect in which the bones of the spine (vertebrae) do not form properly around the spinal cord), a deformity in the legs and pressure sores.
Before she developed the sores, Akoth had undergone an unsuccessful operation to correct the spina bifida defect.Elizabeth Namukose, a counselling psychologist at the cancer foundation, says a doctor at Mulago Hospital revealed that the operation messed up the girls' sensory nerves, leaving her legs paralysed. Akoth was, therefore, confined to one place for long hours, sparking off the pressure sores.
The specialists at Mulago advised that Akoth's wounds be kept clean. Currently, the holes are covering up and healing.
Frequently, Akoth receives treatment from CoRSU, a private non-profit organisation offering preventive, curative and rehabilitation services for people with disability.
"A doctor at CoRSU says there is a possibility of her walking again, though currently she needs support, such as clutches. Eventually, she will be able to move on her own," Namukose says.
Despite the ailment, Akoth is a jolly intelligent girl. She spends most of her time reciting the alphabet. She loves learning and is a fast learner, but may not be able to go to school because Nekesa does not have a stable source of income.
In fact, Nekesa usually travels back to Tororo with Akoth to check on her other children, which sometimes affects Akoth's hospital appointments. Akoth has to travel back and forth because the cancer foundation requires a patient to have a care taker at all times.
Health&Beauty would like to thank everyone that heeded to the call and offered any form of assistance to Akoth and her guardian.

Original_Search_Title: Inflation Falls to 13 Percent but Food Prices Rise
Headline: Kenya: Inflation Falls to 13 Percent but Food Prices Rise
Body_Text:
Kenya's rate of inflation slowed to 13 per cent in April from from 15.61 per cent in March, though consumers are still paying highly for basic food products, transport, energy and housing.
Statistics released by the Kenya National Bureau of Statistics showed the consumer price index increased by 0.92 per cent from 132.51 in March to 133.74 in April. CPI measures the average change in prices of key goods and services over a period.
Food and Non Alcoholic drinks index rose by 1.21 per cent between March and April. This was due to an increase in prices of basic food products such as milk, potatoes, maize flour and onions during the month. There has been a shortage of milk in the country in the last month occasioned by scarcity of animal feeds. The price of a half litre packet of milk for instance, continued to increase from an average of Sh38 in March to Sh40 in April 2012, noted KNBS.
The data however shows that over the same period, there were significant falls in the prices of tomatoes, sugar, cooking fat, bananas and spinach and also cooking gas. Housing, water, electricity, gas and other fuels' index went up by 0.89 per cent during the month, whereas the prices of electricity and kerosene went up by 1.5 a litre 2.7 per cent respectively following higher adjustment by the Energy Regulatory Commission.
This was the fifth consecutive month for the inflation rate to decline, since it hit an all time high of 19.72 per cent in November last year. "Despite increases observed in respect of most of the Indices between the two consecutive months of March and April 2012, the overall year on year inflation continued to decelerate due to the base effect in the inflation computation," read the KNBS statement. The Central Bank of Kenya has since December opted to maintain its key lending rate at 18 per cent to maintain the downward momentum of the inflation rate.

Original_Search_Title: KRA Witness Disowns Document in Sh41.2 Million Case Against Mututho
Headline: Kenya: KRA Witness Disowns Document in Sh41.2 Million Case Against Mututho
Body_Text:
An accounts assistant at Kenya Revenue Authority yesterday disowned documents presented in court as evidence in a case in which Naivasha MP John Mututho is charged with obtaining more than Sh41.2 million by pretence.
Elijah Ndegwa Chimera was put to task by lawyer Cliff Ombetta representing the MP to explain the procedure followed in the documents they presented in court as evidence. He said he did no know who removed some papers in a set of documents.
The witness found himself in a difficult situation when asked to explain some writings and numbers on the KRA documents in relation of tax duty. Ombetta was cross-examining Chimera in a case in which Mututho is accused of obtaining the money from Kenyatta National Hospital 11 years ago. The witness also admitted that there were several cancellation in the prosecution documents. He said he did not know who made the cancellation. Senior principal magistrate Lucy Nyambura of Anti-Corruption Court is hearing the case.
However, the legislator has denied all the chargers against him, saying the charges are politically motivated to silence him. When he first pleaded to the charges, Mututho said the charges were drawn against after joining Orange party instead of Banana party. Chimera who was a cashier at the KRA's office in Mombasa by the time of the alleged offence also was unable to explain several questions about KRA documents put to him by defence counsel.
The witness was also unable to explain why a company known as Excel Motor Mate Ltd had a pin number certificate 4 years prior to its registered in the year 2001. The case was adjourned until May 2. The magistrate fixed the next hearing date after special state prosecutor Patrick Kiage applied for adjournent. Pushed further to give explanation on the receipts, Chimera told the court his work was only receiving money and that he did not know other things on the documents before court.
Earlier in his evidence in chief Chimera took the court through several documents alleged to have been used in defrauding KNH of the money. He identified various documents bearing his name and denounced them, saying he never issued such documents. Mututho is charged that on December 28,2001 at KNH , knowingly used falsified receipts to obtain tax refund of Sh 41,371,515.15 from KNH.
He is also charged that on the same day and same place, with intend to defraud, knowingly and fraudulently uttered a forged receipts to an employee of KNH purporting to be genuine issued by Kenya Revenune Authority for payment of duty Value Added Tax amounting to Sh 5,185,055 in respect of import entry on behalf of KNH by M/s Countryside Suppliers Ltd. The case was adjourned until May 2, for further hearing. The magistrate fixed the next hearing date after special state prosecutor Patrick Kiage applied for adjournent.

Original_Search_Title: Former KNCHR Chairman Hassan Omar Hassan Says 'I Do'
Headline: Kenya: Former KNCHR Chairman Hassan Omar Hassan Says 'I Do'
Body_Text:
Mombasa city literally came to a halt on Saturday and Sunday with heavy traffic as former Kenya National Commission on Human Rights commissioner (KNCHR) and veteran activist, Hassan Omar Hassan joined the marriage bandwagon by exchanging vows with his long term girl friend Asli Osman Mahmoud.
She is a lawyer by profession while the former flamboyant KNCHR commissioner will be vying for the Mombasa County senate in the forthcoming general elections.
The two day nikkah (wedding) officially started when former chief Kadhi Sheikh Hammad Kassim conducted the much anticipated Islamic marriage ceremony at Baluchi Mosque, before the bridegroom and close relatives proceeded to Sabasaba Spaki where the bride was residing.
At Mombasa sports club on Sunday, thousands of men congregated to enjoy some biryani ngo'mbe at a well prepared lunch reception. Ripe bananas and special packed juices were also served. The red carpet was laid out at the podium ready to welcome the many top leaders that were expected. The ladies were not forgotten as they held their own reception at Jubilee building where they danced and made merry. Prime Minister Raila Odinga and his wife Ida joined in the ladies fun and even had a go on the dance floor, much to the delight of the women.
Others who attended the nuptials included Lands Minister James Orengo, Ambassadors Etienne de Poncins of France, Abdelilah Benryane of Morocco, Geert Aagaard Anderson, Mombasa businessman and tycoon Abu Joho, Grain Bulk Board of Directors Mohamed Jaffer, tycoon Tahir Said of the famed TSS, David Lang'at, Assistant Minister Ali Hassan Joho , Rift Valley Railways Group Chief Executive Brown Ondego, MPs Omar Zonga of Msambweni , Yussuf Hassan of Kamukunji, Deputy speaker Farah Maalim, KAA head of security Swaleh Slim among other invited guests.
The couple will be spending their honeymoon at the North and South Coast hotels before proceeding to North Africa. The Star wishes the newlyweds a blessed union.

Original_Search_Title: Comedian Jane Bussmann Cracks Ribs in Town
Headline: Uganda: Comedian Jane Bussmann Cracks Ribs in Town
Body_Text:
JANE Bussmann, a British comedian cum journalist, cracked Kampalans' ribs with her comedy piece on the rebel Joseph Kony and the war in northern Uganda, last Friday at Mish Mash, Kololo.
She told some scary stories (and some dumb ones too) about her Ugandan roller coaster experiences, for an entrance fee of sh90,000 and sh50,000 based on seating arrangement.
The author of Worst Date Ever Or How It Took a Comedy Writer to Reveal Africa's Secret War, she had a chat writer Mildred Apenyo. Excerpts
Hi Jane. Have you got the hang of bodabodas yet?
Hahaha, I don't think I'll ever get used to those. But also, I don't think I'll ever stop using them.
You keep coming back here, what's with you and Uganda?
The simsim paste, that's it. When I take it back home with me, I always have this conversation with mum where she's insisting we throw it away because it has gone bad and I am insisting we keep it because it's delicious.
I love blending it with bananas and freezing the concoction-best ice cream in the world. I will not be trying that. You take on Kony 2012?
Uganda's is a most incredible story; the reception of this Kony 2012 video shows how immense it is and how interested the world can be. Instead of all the fey trivia in columns the world over, all these human angle stories should be written. We could have two years' worth of Oprah from Uganda alone.
If you would have the chance to speak to Jason Russell right before he clicked upload, what would you have said?
"This is going to be much bigger than you imagine. Go up to more people who really know this story. Have an ear to the ground and get more perspectives."
This saga has shown that everybody is angry about militarisation and that postcolonial anger is much alive. But everybody in the world deserves to know these stories.
Did any steamy stuff happen between you and the hot peacemaker in your book that you've kept secret?
I always make a joke that if all these steamy things were happening in my life, I wouldn't be running around working. I'd be on site of these steamy things; doing them.

Original_Search_Title: Why NASS Should Expedite New CEMA Act
Headline: Nigeria: Why NASS Should Expedite New CEMA Act
Body_Text:
CEMA is the acronym for Customs and Excise Management Act.
Nigeria's CEMA CAP C45 is a 1958 encrypted paper gazette that has defined our key rules, regulations and notes for trade regulations, imports, exports, storage and cargo movements and productivity as related and operated by Nigeria Customs Service in modern day 2012.
CEMA CAP C45 is 54 years old, one of the oldest key laws operational in Nigeria and Africa; thus, its negative impact is better imagined as demonstrated by stagnation at our seaports and land borders despite overwhelming federal agencies competing to frustrate criminals.
By 1958, the cold war was in place and drivers of Nigeria's economy and colonial interests and colonial masters' markets uppermost in their minds.
Drafters of Nigeria's 1958 CEMA ruled on their perceived scope of the Nigerian economy, world trade facilities, world trade permissivities, capitalists cum socialist's perspectives and poorly organized Nigerian society that lacked adequate local professionals 54 years ago.
Current CEMA does not cover oil lifting rights, adequate revenue collection on oil and revenue generation monitoring in Nigeria's petroleum sub-sector. Nigeria's economy is petroleum pique dependent. CEMA Act should therefore be reformed to cater for proper accountability of oil proceeds as obtainable in Britain, Norway and elsewhere. It should regulate lifting rights, secure multinational investors' rights and privileges in the quest to shore up avoidable revenue leakages while bracing investors' protection from flagrant Federal Government agents.
That CEMA Act now constitutes a clog in the wheel of progress; is obsolete and reactionary to modernity in a challenging global world, inadequate and not in conformity with globalization.
Current CEMA Act limits powers of authorities of government and federal agents' capacity to reverse death of our moribund production industries.
It has ensured that Nigeria-bound goods and cargoes divert easily to neighbopuring countries and get smuggled back into Nigeria. A direct consequence is that several lives have been lost while combating avoidable regimes that aid smuggling of certain goods and cargo. Aside, several billions of funds wasted annually to checkmate amorphous local border anti-smuggling efforts.
Tinkering with CEMA Act over the years continued to constitute an albatross on roles of the Nigerian Customs Service in providing leading edge revenue generation for Nigeria. It created operational frustration to lilly-livered customs service management teams. That explained copy-cat syndrome that characterized our razzmatazz conduct in revenue generation and border policing by Customs of yester-years.
CEMA is open to judicial condemnation as it applied today in view of Freedom of Information Bill, Fundamental Rights Bill and other legalized freedoms protective rights bills within the United Nations (UN) and the Commonwealth.
President Goodluck Jonathan will do well to urgently encourage Nigeria's House of Representatives on their laudable goal and drive for modernisation of the CEMA Act.
As Nigeria celebrates her 52nd Independence anniversary this year, there can be no better gift to Nigerians from the National Assembly than to approve and present a passed bill reforming the 54 years old CEMA on trade and revenue generation.
The Minister of Trade and Investment, Minister of Transport and Minister of Finance in consultation with the Secretary to the Federal Government should partner to actualise and bequeath the legacy of a new CEMA Act for Nigeria.
The new CEMA must ban all unnecessary banana peel security agents from seaports and land borders as applicable in developed nations.
The proposed new CEMA Act, must be broad to consciously interact and cohesively interrelate cooperative coalition roles of critical inter-agencies of state that were created by the Nigerian state since independence in 1960 and emergence of the Nigeria Republic in 1963. In pursuance of her quest for industrialisation and economic trade hub, the CEMA Act should discourage attempts to reset Nigeria as a dumping ground for sub-standard goods and services.
Nigeria CEMA guidelines must fight corruption and plug loopholes exploited by cheap criminals who parade Federal Government agencies to hoodwink investors, discourage exact taxation and sabotage federal revenue generation and other interests in the maritime sub-sector.
The proposed 2012 CEMA Act reforms must circumvent extensive prosecution procedure of offenders; that generates escape route for saboteurs of state interests on importation, exportation, storage and movement of goods and cargoes, transportation and domiciliation of trade within Nigeria's territory.
Nigeria's CEMA Act must be proactive and amenable to emergent local exigencies in line with WCO AND WTO permitted windows on rights and privileges.
Chuks Agoha, is the Secretary, Conference of Alumni Association of Nigerian Universities

Original_Search_Title: My Story, My Woes - Esin
Headline: Nigeria: My Story, My Woes - Esin
Body_Text:
Remember Etim Esin? that stylish Nigerian Ex-international who waltzed his way into the hearts of millions of football lovers in this country following his ingenious style of playing which was similar to that of the ex-Argentine football star, Diego Maradona whom he was nicknamed after.
He was one of the finest footballers this country ever produced in the early 90s. Hi-Groove caught up with him recently where he shares the story of his misfortune in the field of soccer, and why he's called a rebel by his family members.
Excerpts:
Growing Up
I'm from a polygamous home, my father married four wives. Hailing from a famous aristocratic family, most of my siblings are lawyers by profession as wellas successful businessmen residing in Europe. My late father was an adviser to Clement Isong, governor of the old Cross River State. My family saw me as a rebel because of my decision to become a footballer as against their desire for qaulitative education.
I started my soccer career in the 1980s with Calabar Rovers. Later I joined the defunct Flash Flamingos and Iwuayanwu Nationale, now Heartland of Owerri in 1987. Despite the frustrations I faced in the cause of my career, I still count myself as being a lucky guy. And because of my talent, I was able to go professional. Soccer exposed me to the world and made me what I'm today.
Soccer in place of education
As a child, I used to escape from classroom to play football. Although my parents wanted us to be educated but I was the odd person out in my family because of the passion I have for football. Today, all my siblings are graduates but my love for football ruled me out of that dream. So, I can say the price I paid for choosing to become a footballer was my education.
My Downfall
There were many problems that worked against my career as a footballer. First, I was involved in a drug case after which I was also shot by armed robbers during the preparation for the Junior World Cup.
It happened that I was going to park my new 505 saloon car given to me by Emmanuel Iwuanyanwu, when armed robbers attacked me in Surulere. But later, those robbers sent an apology letter to me while I was still on admission at the Lagos University Teaching Hospital, LUTH. They said they never knew I was the one they attacked. And since God preserved my life, I had to forgive them.
Another problem that worked against my career was racism. Till date, there is still racism in football and my generation were among those that suffered colour- discrimination in Europe. The rape case I had in Belgium was in the early '90s.
It was a racial treatment- from a white family called Pedofiles. I was dating one of their daughters unknown to me that her father was also having a sexual affair with her. But because I was a black man dating a white lady, my girlfriend became mischievous.
What happened was that she got pregnant and by the time she gave birth to the child, he was a light-skinned child. But she knew I wouldn't marry her. That was how I got into trouble. In fact, the late MKO Abiola was determined to get me out of the trouble.
He sent lawyers from Edewor Chambers to defend me and paid the fees. At that time, he was in the middle of his presidential campaign. So, he told me that I should be patience. That was how I waited and waited until I got the news of his death. Apart from my ordeal, there were other black players who suffered similar problems in Europe. Unfortunately, FIFA cannot stamp out racism in Europe. I can only urge emerging players to learn from my mistakes.
If I was married then, such evil wouldn't have happened to me. So, I could be right to allude that my inability to marry early affected my career. But I thank God today that I am married to a wonderful woman. For me, my life is like a movie, from the drug case, to allegation of rape and later, shot by armed robbers before I travelled to Chile. But in all, I thank God that I'm still alive.
Lifestyle as a footballer
I played topflight football with style but it was shortlived due to my indiscipline. I was always different from my colleagues and everybody knew this claim. As a footballer then, I had many nicknames such as 'powerful,' Maradona', 'Superbrat,' 'Adidimba,' etc. Back in the days, I was among those who brought styles into football in this country.
I remember when I bought a 505 saloon car in the 1990s. It inspired many footballers as they were motivated to continue pursuing their passion. This also attracted a handful of rising footballers to consider the option of going into professional football.
In fact, many of them also travelled to Belgium. And if you look at the story of most Nigerian footballer stars today, you'd notice they all started from Belgium. I think Belgium provided Nigerian stars a platform to become who they are today.
Memorable and sad moments
My happiest moment was when I led Nigeria to qualify for the World Youth Championship in Chile, South America, in 1987.
While my saddest moment was when I was playing at the National Stadium in Surulere, Lagos, in a World Cup qualifying match against Angola in 1989, and my great friend, Samuel Okwaraji, slumped and died on the field of play. I could not stand it because we were too close. He was fond of calling me Maradona and I used to call him Ruud Gullit. We shared similar dream of taking Nigeria to the World Cup in 1990. But his death shattered the dream.
My Kind of Dressing
I have been fashion crazy right from my days in the pitch. Then, whenever I wear any dress, my colleagues would be envious of me. They used to think I'm a proud person because of my glamorous dress sense.
My mood also affect the way I dress. Sometimes, if you don't know me too well, my dressing could be deceptive; that you might think I'm a musicians, or an actor. Sometimes, people look at me twice so as to ascertain my true identity. I am creative in the way I dress.
Women and I
I think my good dress sense attracts female admirers to me. I was once rough with women but my focus now is on my family. In anything I do today, my family comes first.
Meeting my wife
Meeting my wife was not love at first sight. Her name is Mariam, a Fulani woman from Mali. She was the only woman who stood by me when the going was tough. Despite all the troubles and humiliations I suffered,God blessed me with a good wife.
Our marriage is blessed with a beautiful daughter. May be, if I have all the money in the world, I might not get the kind of woman I have today. She is loving, tolerable and easy-going. She believes so much in me,and not what she will benefit from me.
Hobbies
I like riding bicycle. I also like washing cars.
Smoking and Drinking
I smoke at my convenience but I want to stop smoking. For drinking, I'm not a drunk.
Foods and Fruits
I like the Malian food called Kuskus. Also, as an Akwa Ibom man, I live to eat Edikaeko, Afang etc. For fruits, I eat a lot of banana,oranges and others because fruit is good for every sport man.

Original_Search_Title: Mugizi's Wolokoso T-Shirts Runs Kampala and Beyond
Headline: Uganda: Mugizi's Wolokoso T-Shirts Runs Kampala and Beyond
Body_Text:
Hillary Mugizi is an extraordinary 24-year-old, who has harnessed slang to establish a T-shirt branding company.
Words and phrases such as: "Ow'esente Ze, Musoga, Wolokoso and Kyaba 2 Much," feature on his T-shirts. Whilst you use slang daily to show off or belittle others, Mugizi's company Ezi Wear, artistically uses it to make unique T-shirts. And it is this very company that has since catapulted him to success beyond his humble dreams.Orphaned at an early age, Mugizi says he did not feel the void because his aunts took him in and mothered him like their own.
"My five aunts shaped me into the man I have grown to become today. They taught me how to survive on my own and I owe all my achievements to them. They taught me to be forward-thinking," he says with a smile.
His rise started in 2010 at a friend's birthday party, where guests were asked to turn up in creative wear. Mugizi, then a third-year student of Industrial Fine Art at Makerere University, wore a simple self-designed T-shirt with graffiti; which wowed all the students in tuxedos and designer sneakers.
"I ended up stealing the show. I even got more attention than the birthday boy. A guest bought that T-shirt at a whopping Shs 100,000; I was totally overwhelmed," Mugizi reminisces.
Since then, the artist has enjoyed a steady climb to success. He took advantage of the wide reach of social media to market his art. Soon, he was receiving small deals from fellow students. He used part of the remittance to pay his accommodation fees in the lavish Akamwesi hostel. The balance would be used for buying art paints, and partying.
"I studied and majored in Drawing and Painting but as more deals kept coming, I realized I had to acquire skills in printing. So, I taught myself. You can only paint a few T-shirts in a day," Mugizi explains.
Fresh out of the university, Mugizi landed his first major deal - printing President Yoweri Museveni's 2011 presidential campaign T-shirts that bore the phrase 'Get the vote out'. Though he does not divulge how much money he made from the deal, he says he reinvested it in the business. The presidential deal was followed by yet another mega contract; one that introduced him to NGOs and corporate organizations. He was contracted by Basic Needs, an NGO that advocates for the mentally handicapped.
"That is one of the most challenging, though exciting, deals I have ever done. I knew I was dealing with a sensitive issue and yet I had to come up with interesting phrases. I am glad I nailed it," he boasts.
Mugizi, whose work mesmerized Basic Needs, creatively came up with popular prints such as Zivuddeko, Going Bananas, No Zonto and I am a Mental Case. Wowed by his work, the NGO has since offered him other deals. For all his works, the artist is best known for his Wolokoso T-shirts. Popular urban slang for "cheap talk", the phrase was popularized by musicians and politicians. However, since his T-shirts, Wolokoso has crossed borders.
"I wore this tee [T-shirt] in a Boston nightclub and a man offered to buy it at $100 [Shs 250, 000]. I referred him to Ezi Wear and he shipped in hundreds of tees. Now every Ugandan living in America knows about Wolokoso," one Humphrey posted on his Facebook page.
Mugizi calls his design The Ezi Way; made of colourful prints, calligraphy, graffiti and pictures, the label has a striking resemblance to that of French designer Christian Audigier. However, his style, sense of humour and local touch make them Ugandan. He complements his designs with a small Ezi logo of his dreadlocked head.
Mugizi says his dream is to turn Ezi Wear into a powerful label, complete with various lines for children, the elderly, boots, jeans and even the clergy. He also views competition as a motivating factor, saying: "The clothes industry is too big for me to run alone."
To boost the label, Mugizi has, together with four friends, started up an entertainment company called Blush Entertainment. The Kamwokya-based company doubles as Ezi Wear's main store and deals in videography, graphics designing and still photography.
"I am in no rush to make money. Whatever little I get, I invest it back into the business. That is how money is made. I want to have retired by the time I clock 30," Mugizi declares.

Original_Search_Title: Lorry Knocks a Woman
Headline: South Sudan: Lorry Knocks a Woman
Body_Text:
A tipper with a number plate 506B yesterday lost control and knocked a woman who was identified as Juan Betty a Bar attendant in custom market, she was rushed to Juba Teaching Hospital in a serious bleeding.
She was crushed on the left hip and on the stomach at around 2:00 pm in custom near Eden money transfer bank.
According to the traffic police in custom the driver was a Ugandan by name Jjuko Erisa after knocking the woman refused to stop as ordered but jumped off his vehicle leaving it running on it's own causing a lot of loses to those who were selling cigarettes, bananas, chapatti and roosted chicken and meat at the road side as the vehicle went on pushing them while the owners were racing for their lives.
In traffic police prison in Juba, Jjuko said the incidence happened when gas got finished in his lorry so it was not easy to control the brake and the woman was busy talking to anther person who was on motor cycle so he kept on shouting the vehicle has no brake people were leaving but the was just seeing and waiting on the way.
Finally the traffic police in Juba office said Jjuko will be in the custody until the woman complete all her medications and those who claim loses of damage to their products have to appeal after the woman is medically fit so so Jjuko answers all at onces.

Original_Search_Title: Fresh Dream as IRS Cuts Malaria in Kagera (1)
Headline: Tanzania: Fresh Dream as IRS Cuts Malaria in Kagera (1)
Body_Text:
THE annual Indoor Residual Spraying (IRS) has cut malaria cases in two regions by over 50 per cent in the last four years, latest reports show.
The regions where IRS is taking place every year are Kagera, Mwanza and Mara. Kagera's latest regional report also shows that the disease is now afflicting less of the population, noting that the number of expectant mothers using mosquito nets has increased from 35 per cent in 2004 to 70 per cent.
Kagera Regional Medical Officer (RMO), Mr Herman Emmanuel Kabinge, said that while deaths due to malaria have reduced, surveys on the impact of IRS and mosquito net distribution programmes have cut inpatient cases by three quarters in the last three years and reduced infant mortality rate by 48 per cent.
He said they also instituted a social campaign to sensitize the public about the new malaria treatments, including a number of radio and television programmes. "Together, these actions have had a startling impact on the disease," he said. Mr Kabirige said prevention of malaria during pregnancy has also been a key control intervention although the use of prophylaxis medication by pregnant women is low at 40 per cent against the 85 per cent target.
Along with past interventions, emphasis is on new cases monitoring and research. "When you get this kind of success you have to increase surveillance," he noted. The Kagera Regional Coordinator of Malaria Control, Mr Ahmed Rubwa, also told the 'Daily News on Saturday' that the decision to implement ACTs also included upgrading laboratory facilities and training of healthcare personnel, all closely modelled on the World Health Organization's Roll Back Malaria initiative.
He expressed optimism about Kagera's chances of attaining its goal of reaching 80 per cent of the population with prevention and treatment measures seen in the level of co-operation taking place between government, local NGOs and international donors, which include the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, the World Bank and the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation.
"Technological advances such as malaria rapid diagnosis tests (MRDTs) brought into our region in 2007 and the new artemisinin-based malaria drug, Coartem, have provided additional tools for tackling malaria," he said. He said that even low-tech solutions, such as awareness campaigns, insecticide spraying and provision of insecticide-treated bed nets, had been beyond the means of many rural people before 2007.
Kagera is known for its high banana production but for many, to this day, since 2006, it evokes images of children dying from malaria during a serious outbreak as it was plagued with nearepidemic levels in 2006 and 2007. As a result, Kagera became the first region on the mainland to implement the new generation of malaria treatments, artemisininbased combination therapies (ACTs).
"Working through the National Malaria Control Programme and the national public health infrastructure, the implementation of the new national treatment strategy for malaria included not only the purchase of the drugs, but also a new procurement process which includes quality assurance procedures," he said. The five-year project covers Lake Zone regions of Kagera, Mara and Mwanza, with a target of scaling up both reduction of cases and deaths by 80 per cent by 2013 and progress from malaria control to malaria pre-elimination and elimination.
Faced with this difficult situation, Kagera became the first region on Tanzania mainland to implement the new generation of malaria treatments, artemisininbased combination therapies (ACTs). Working through the National Malaria Control Programme and the national public health infrastructure, the implementation of the new national treatment strategy for malaria included not only the purchase of the drugs but also a new procurement process which includes quality assurance procedures and overview of the distribution system.
This was meant to ensure that no district gets shortages of the medicine. The decision to implement ACTs also included upgrading laboratory facilities and the training of healthcare personnel. To date, hundreds of medical staff have received training in the new treatment protocols. Other elements of malaria prevention were also initiated or expanded through this grant, including the 95 per cent subsidised distribution of over 500,000 insecticide-treated bed nets (thus providing virtually national coverage) and indoor residual spraying in selected areas.
He said they also instituted a social campaign to inform the population of the new malaria treatments, including a number of both radio and television programmes. "Together, these actions have had a startling impact on the epidemic", he said. Mortality rates among those five years of age have dropped by 48 per cent in the last three years, according to the 2009 region's health report.

Original_Search_Title: Adventures of an 'Obroni' in Accra
Headline: Ghana: Adventures of an 'Obroni' in Accra
Body_Text:
Stepping off the plane, coming from the U.S. via Paris and Amsterdam, I was greeted at Kotoka Airport by a blast of hot, humid wind. The type of air that you feel you can almost swim through, due to the thickness of the humidity. My white skin was already sweating bullets.
After finding my driver and where I would be staying, I was finally able to observe and reflect upon what I had encountered thus far. I spent some time in a hostel going back and forth to my Volunteer Organization for my week-long orientation into Ghanaian culture, and how to comport myself as a foreigner - a white man in black Africa!
Let me start off by saying that the people I have encountered here in Accra are among the friendliest that I have found in all my travels, whether in North America, South America or Europe. When I pass by children, their eyes light up, and bright smiles form on their faces. "Hello Obroni! How are you?" they ask. "I am fine Obibini. How are you?" And we all laugh.
Some of my favorite customs in Ghana are the greetings. I only know a few basic sayings in Twi, and I can't understand it when it is spoken - which I encounter everywhere, but I absolutely adore the handshake with the snap of the fingers.
Back home, there are countless ways of shaking hands amongst different age and ethnic groups, but the universal handshake that is known to all Ghanaians is my favorite over all others. It took me a few tries to get it right, and nobody taught me how, but now when I do it, I feel accepted and that I belong!
Being a foreigner, it is easy for me to get lost, but asking directions could not be easier in any other country. So many people are willing to help out and take me to where I want to go, but there is also the risk of talking too long and not getting to my destination when I had hoped. Time seems to pass slower here at the center of the world, than where I am from, but I don't mind that.
When it comes to matters of satisfying hunger, Ghanaian traditional food is something that has already penetrated my heart and taste buds. I have not had a dish that I didn't like. Although I have only been here one week, in my limited time, I have tried Red Red, Fufu, Tilapia and Banku, as well as some other staples such as chicken and rice with beef stew.
Every morsel I consumed was inundated with flavor! Nothing was bland. Every ingredient leaped from my fingers to my mouth (Americans are not accustomed to eating mostly with their hands).
The spices were not too hot, and not too mild, but perfectly balanced. I have not seen these meals prepared in the U.S., but I am sure I could open up a restaurant back home serving only Ghanaian food, and it would be very popular and profitable.
Traveling around Accra is definitely an eye-opening experience for an outsider. Getting to any location, normally involves taking a 'tro tro'. Since there are no signs that say where they stop, I have to ask many questions, so I don't miss my station.
In the U.S., I am used to navigation by means of bus stops, street signs, and memory. Having neither of these means at my disposal, I have to ask a lot of questions, but as I mentioned before, Ghanaians are always happy to answer.
I was briefly taught 'tro tro' etiquette and taxi driver bargaining by my Volunteer Organization (something that can be both fun and frustrating), and I have been able to see some of the city. I have waded on the shores of the Gulf of Guinea at La Beach, and have been hopelessly overwhelmed by all the sights, smells, and sounds of Makola Market, where I felt completely out of my element.
It was my first time ever seeing a market on such a large and busy scale, where everybody wanted to sell something to the Obroni. I did not want to be rude, so I talked with each person who approached me, but what I really wanted to do was explore. I didn't get very far, and would have to try again another day.
In the U.S., I had a car, and that was my main mode of transportation. I drove it everywhere, whether to work, a friend's place, or to the supermarket. Here in Accra, I walk 15 minutes to the |tro tro' stop, ride for 15 minutes and then walk another 15 minutes to work.
I am still trying to get used to finding the correct 'tro tro' for my destination, and while trying to remain polite, I have to make sure that I can make my way through the masses of people who are pushing and pulling every which way to climb on board.
I like walking through the city and the different neighborhoods. Each corner has its own feel. Every turn has its own sound. Every shop has its own smell.
