The pattern shows that the robbers operate between Mpigi and Masaka. They target passengers from one direction and are interested in only money. This means that it is not also unsafe to travel without money on you because when the gangsters strike, they threaten to kill whoever does not surrender money quickly.
These robbers exude unwavering confidence as they molest their victims in a way that is uncharacteristic of highway thugs who often strike with haste and withdraw quickly fearing that a police patrol unit, on routine surveillance could arrive at the scene.
So, how can armed robbers seize control of a major highway and for more than an hour terrorize passenger in three or five buses until they withdraw at will? Where do they withdraw to with their guns and loot? Who tips them about the on-coming buses to enable them strike with the high level of precision? And most importantly, where is police in this mix?
Security agencies in Uganda are know to unearth even the most obscure "rebel" groups - some without even a single gun. But how can armed men frequently seize control of a highway that leads to the country homes of the president, the vice president, the prime minister and more than half of the entire cabinet, without being apprehended?
Answers to these, and many more, questions can at best be explained in the context of the changing role and responsibilities of police in Uganda. Uganda police has found itself more involved in the fight for control of political power instead of playing its traditional role of keeping law and order. Therefore most of the resources have been invested in stopping peaceful demonstration while armed gangs control of the highways.
The few patrol units on the roads are ill-equipped and not motivated. So, they spend time chasing after motorists they see speaking on phone while driving as well trucks overloaded with bananas, charcoal and cattle - from whose drivers they extort money.
There are genuine fears that highway thugs could indeed be aide by security agencies who supply them with weapons and intelligence information.
This indeed is not without past precedent. Some senior police officers implicated of hiring guns to thugs by a judicial commission of inquiry into misconduct in the force are still in service. And two years ago, a senior officer was arrested at the scene of robbery gone wrong on Kampala-Mityana-Mubende road. Could this explain why thugs can take control of a highway for more than an hour and only withdraw at leisure?

Original_Search_Title: Rugby - League Decider
Headline: Uganda: Rugby - League Decider
Body_Text:
There may be four more rounds to go after this weekend's fixtures but the meeting between MTN Heathens and utl Kobs will decide the destination of the 2012 Nile Special Rugby league crown, writes Felix Eupal.
For every rugby aficionado, the place to be this Saturday afternoon is Kyadondo Rugby grounds. In perhaps the biggest game on the local rugby calendar, Heathens host Kobs in a match they badly need to win to keep their title hopes alive. Kobs, on the other hand, hold a four-point lead and will all but claim the title if they avoid defeat at the intimidating ground.
Both teams are heads and shoulders above the competition going by form and history. Heathens are the current dominant force having won the league three straight times but Kobs are the most successful club with...titles in their cabinet. On paper, the clash bears hallmarks of a classic after a dramatic first round fixture that Kobs edged 13-11. Therefore, the pressure will be on Kobsí dynamic duo of Kevin Makmot and Justin Kimono to justify the hype surrounding them as well as the tag of 'the future of Ugandan rugby.'
Makmot scored the winning penalty against Heathens, notched down a combined eight penalties against Pirates in both rounds this season while Kimono is the most dangerous player with an average of a try per game. The fact that Kobs are the sole unbeaten team and have already put potential banana skins like Mongers and G4S Pirates out of the way, Heathens is the only hurdle left. Better still, the four point cushion statistically implies that Kobs can still win the title even if they go on to lose the game provided they win - with a bonus point - all their remaining fixtures against Rhinos, XFM Impis, Nile and Toyota Buffaloes.
Going by this season's performances, Kobs are poised to achieve the feat with Buffaloes standing out as the only exception for an upset. Heathens are also in the same boat because they share the same fixtures with Kobs in the remaining encounters and none of those four teams has given them a scare in the last four years.
On the Heathens front, it won't just be their pride at stake but history as well, because a comeback to level the point's margin with Kobs and a defense of the title would make them the only club in Ugandan rugby to win the title four times in a row. Already they have the home advantage and will be buoyed by the new set of jerseys from sponsors MTN.

Original_Search_Title: Business Booms at Kaduna Fruit Market
Headline: Nigeria: Business Booms at Kaduna Fruit Market
Body_Text:
Kaduna — At the ever busy station fruit market in Kaduna, it is business as usual as both men and women are seen buying and selling fruits both in large and small quantity.
Every morning both buyers and sellers throng the various sections of the market to either buy or sell bananas, pineapple, water melon and pawpaw.
Young boys who help to arrange and carry the fruits from one place to the other are also not left out in the business as they also make brisk business.
At the close of each day, large vehicular movement is usually noticed at the Station Market as people drive in and out to buy fruits for consumption.
Our correspondent who spoke with a watermelon dealer, Sa'ad Ismaila, gathered that he buys watermelon from Jigawa, Kano, Zamfara, Kebbi, Sokoto, Yobe and Niger states.
"I am a dealer and I also sell in small quantities to people who resell. I sell a whole vehicle load of watermelon and I also sell in small quantities. I don't follow the vehicles because they already known me, so they just bring in watermelons from different states for me to buy," Sa'ad said.
A vehicle load of watermelon he said costs about N300, 000 depending on the size of the fruit. While he noted that it is possible to get over 1, 200 pieces of watermelon from one vehicle, he said he could sell one watermelon for N200, N300 or N400, depending on the size.
According to Sa'ad, he sometimes makes N20,000 profit when he sells a fully loaded vehicle depending on the buyer but makes more profit from selling in pieces to those who resell in small scale.
"I make a profit of N10,000 - N30,000 from one vehicle load of watermelon while sometimes I get lower than that depending on luck and the market. I have made up to N50,000 from one vehicle," he said.
On how he copes with the sale of watermelon being a perishable fruit, he said that the type of watermelon he buys is the very good and big ones that do not get spoilt on time.
"If I keep these watermelons in a very good place, they can stay for a week or two without getting spoilt and that is why I always like to go for the good ones even though they are more expensive," he said.
He also noted that if market moves very well, he could sell a whole vehicle load in a day but sometimes it could days before he finishes selling.
Sa'ad said he has been in the business for over seven years and considers it very profitable. He has built a house from the profit made from the business.

Original_Search_Title: Chidi's Assets, A Bridge and a Prayer
Headline: Nigeria: Chidi's Assets, A Bridge and a Prayer
Body_Text:
"There is no end to what you can accomplish if you don't care who gets the credit" - Florence Luscomb
I HAVE never met Professor Chidi Odinkalu, the Chairperson of the National Human Rights Commission (NHRC) in person, although I hear he is the type of Nigerian I should list among my friends.
Chidi and I belong to a cyberspace group which has many other people I know very well, most of whom think very highly of Chidi. Every time Chidi says or reacts to something someone says, you see evidence of learning, grooming and an open-mindedness rare even among educated people these days. Our group is passionate about most important things , and we often get all worked up for days when there is something to argue over. Chidi is always in the thick of it.
So why is Chidi on the title of this column today? Because, on 23rd of November, 2011, he, as is required by law, went and declared the totality of his assets and liabilities with the Code of Conduct Bureau. So what, you say. Everyone does that.
And you might even say many deliberately increase their assets to make allowances for what may "accrue" during the periods they hold offices; and then pray that the severe lack of capacity of the Code of Conduct Bureau to verify such exaggerated declarations will remain severe. They will then stay on the shelves of the Bureau until an exit declaration is made, which may not be cross-checked with the entry declaration either.
Going the extra mile
Chidi did not just go through the statutory ritual of declaring all his assets. You would think Chidi will be wary of telling relations, friends, in-laws and the entire public what he is worth. Some people who will say even your spouse(s) should not know what you are worth (but will make allowances for telling them your liabilities) will be shocked that Chidi went the extra mile of requesting that his declaration should receive full public disclosure.
In other words, even though the law does not compel him to disclose his assets and liabilities to the public, and most people will be reluctant to do this for fear of opening floodgates to relatives and sundry persons; kidnappers, armed robbers, and enemies who may reveal the lies in the declaration, he asks that his own declaration should be made public.
Many people will say it is fine to do this, if you do not own much. But judge for yourself whether Chidi's assets and liabilities are the type you declare quietly.
A Non-Governmental Organization, R2K (Right to Know) said details of Professor Odinkalu's assets and liabilities were:
i.Personal Earnings from two standard Bank Accounts with balances of N94,000 and $11,700;
ii.Two Barclays Bank accounts in the U.K containing £7,752 and £4,542;
iii.A mortgaged 3-bedroom house in Edmonton, London;
iv.A 4-bedroom house in Lekki, Lagos;
v.A Toyota RAV4 bought in 2004;
vi.A Kia Rio car bought in 2005;
vii.Shares and stocks worth about N3m;
viii.A personal pension plan managed by Friends Provident
ix.A 27 KVA Nioda generator;
x.7.5 KVA Inverter;
xi.10,000 Books which the Professor described as "invaluable."
Now, if you are among the 80 percent of Nigerians who earn less than N300 a day, Professor Chidi will be an incredibly wealthy man.
But if you were a ex-governor now under going trial in London, or many of his former colleagues whose cases before the EFCC and the Courts appear unlikely to ever be concluded, what the Professor has may be the value of what you could dash a girl friend. Even if you are a serving Governor, Minister or some powerful person in the corridors or bedrooms of power, the total value of Chidi's assets are what your wife could spend on a two-day trip to Dubai or London.
Bar in accountability and openness
The voluntary publication of Chidi's assets and liabilities has raised the bar in accountability and openness. I was close to the take-off of the late Umaru Musa 'Yar Adua's administration, and I remember the panic he caused when he decided, against the most vociferous advise, not to publish his declared assets and liabilities.
His decision was ground-breaking in terms of its impact, and it really set the cat among the pidgeons. Panic run right through all the political office holders and the hundreds angling to be appointed Special Advisers, Ministers, Chairmen of Boards etc. Will he demand that they did same, even against existing law? Will he change the law to compel the publication of declared assets and liabilities?
Will he hold it against them if they did not? Would it be worth holding a public office under Yar'Adua if one had to declare his entire assets (or some of it, and then risk exposure from those who know better)? In the end the late President said it was a personal decision of his publish, and he was not going to compel anyone to do so. His number two, now President Jonathan, also published his own declaration. I do not recall anyone else doing same. And the nation lost the opportunity to raise the moral standards of leadership.
The loudest message sent by Chidi is that those in position of responsibility should be responsible. There should be no reasons why people who sit in judgment over how billions of public funds are spent should not tell the public what they are worth. If you have a reason for hiding your real personal worth, you have no business approaching an elective office.
The Dundaye failed bridge
The failure to appreciate the burden of responsibility is the reason this nation is crumbling before our very eyes. It is the reason why the Dundaye Bridge which links Usumanu Danfodio University, Sokoto (U.D.U.S) where I am a Visiting Lecturer and Sokoto City could collapse again with the coming rains. The absence of the required levels of responsibility by the Federal Government is the reason why the patch-patch work done on the bridge when it first collapsed two years ago will give way.
This bridge which links Sokoto with one of the biggest universities in the North, which links Sokoto with six L.G.As and even with Niger Republic; and which is a vital economic asset may collapse again because neither the Federal Government nor the State Governments will bother to mobilise the funds to reconstruct it. When it does collapse again, we will close the University again.
Children of the rich and powerful will continue their studies in the U.K, Malaysia, Ghana and Ukrain, while those of the poor who go to U.D.U.S will sit at home. They will curse and hate the leaders whose personal wealth will build ten Dundaye Bridges. They will resent authority, resent their circumstances, and resent children of the rich and powerful who do not go to U.D.U.S.
Professor Chidi Odinkalu has challenged Nigerians who hold positions of trust and responsibility to take steps towards submitting themselves beyond ritual legal requirements, and to actually commit to one of the most important steps to fight corruption and official impunity. How I wish a wealthy northerner will shame the Federal Government and catchment States whose young people attend U.D.U.S, by reconstructing the vitally-strategic Dundaye Bridge.
AND NOW THIS
When Professor Chidi Odinkalu's declared assets and liabilities was published, my younger brother, Mouftah, posted this prayer: "Maulana Chidi, May your shadow never grow shorter. By di grace of God, school fees no go deplete those £12k and $11k.
Mortgage interest and repayment sef, no go chop dem. Dat N94k no go go down.... Na so so e go increase, with legit earnings. Keke NAPEP no go scratch either dat RAV4 or di Kia Rio. From dat Kia na to Range Rover..... and from dat RAV4 na straight to aeroplane... Dat Lekki house go born for Banana Island. E go born country house.... Your two generators no go knock. Mechanic and rewire no go come near dem.. Dat pension plan and those stock shares no go experience 2008 meltdown... and may you never use BUPA insurance." Could there be a better prayer?

Original_Search_Title: PDP National Chairmanship Race Gathers Momentum
Headline: Nigeria: PDP National Chairmanship Race Gathers Momentum
Body_Text:
Peterclaver Egbochue reports that the contestants are working assiduously to outsmart one another ahead of the party's convention
THE race for the Chairmanship position of the ruling Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) is obviously gathering momentum with all the aspirants putting finishing touches to their strategies.
This is coming on the heels of the national convention of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) that comes up later this month, during which national officers of the party would be elected.
Analysts say the chairmanship position has always elicited interest from various quarters which may not be unconnected with the kind of influence and authority the occupier wields on the president as well as other governors voted on the platform of the party.
It is this clout and affluence that follows that has always dotted the race with some kind of land mines, intrigues and 'banana peels'.
They observed that from the days of Chief Solomon Lar, who was the first chairman of the party to the tenure of Dr. Okwesilieze Nwodo, the position of the chairman of the party has always been a searing one as seen by the tempestuous tenures of Senator Barnabas Gemade,
Chief Audu Ogbe, Dr. Ahmadu Ali, Prince Vincent Ogbulafor and Dr. Nwodo himself.
Keen observers say there has never been any real contest since the very first one in 1999 upon the return of democracy when former President Olusegun Obasanjo foisted Chief Barnabas Gemade on the party despite hue and cries from party members
But Senator Mohammed Abba Aji noted that his interest to run for the position of the chairman was as a result of the pressure being put on him by his political associates from across the country.
The former presidential aide known in political circles for his enthusiasm in topical issues admitted that the size of the party has become a major challenge in managing its affairs to the satisfaction of all the members.
'So, when we say the PDP is the largest party in Africa, it is indeed the largest party in Africa. And because of its size, it would also have its share of prospects and problems, strengths and weaknesses just like any other organisation. But I think it has evolved well because it is still evolving.
'So, its evolution is still in progress. Most of its evolution is still formative and therefore you would still expect some formative challenges, the greatest of which, in my view, is either the party's failure to cater for all interests which is impossible or its membership expectations which by far exceed the capacity of the party to serve them', he enthused.
Aji argued that such rules and laws that require compliance from every member would provide a level playing field for all members to pursue their ambitions.
He described this as a major challenge which his administration will pursue. He added that he would reconcile aggrieved members of the party.
'Party members' happiness and acceptance of the outcome of any party process or decision depends on their individual interest and groupings. You see most people get or feel disillusioned when they lose. But we will endeavour to reconcile the family, and forge ahead again', he said.
Another aspirant to the chairmanship position is Ibrahim Birma, from Borno State who was once elected member of the National Assembly on the platform of the defunct United Nigeria Congress Party (UNCP) but was not inaugurated.
Speaking to newsmen recently on his ambition Birma who was also the secretary of PDP campaign organisation for the governorship and other elections in Borno State said his contributions has helped to advance the course of democracy and successes the party has recorded in the state this year.
Largely seen as inexperienced among the other contestants, Birma posited that experience in the past has not moved the party an inch forward, wondering what then is the relevance of that experience in determining who becomes the next chairman of the party.
'If the experience that the other contestants are now flaunting as what is needed to move the party forward, where were they before?
Experience is relevant, but it is not a determinant factor; politics or any other endeavour is all about harnessing human and material resources to achieve a set goal', he stated.
Since 1999, the PDP has not produced any chairman that ended his tenure meritoriously; Birma described the development as the fallout of intrigues and arm-twisting that had characterised the process of installing the previous national chairmen of the party, adding that the various interest groups which over time have constituted themselves as god-fathers had not helped the matter.
'I am not sure whether there was a time you have seen an aspirant under PDP crisscrossing this country for wide consultation.He expressed confidence that he would win the contest going by the favourable responses that have trailed his declaration in the contest.
'I have made up my mind that this aspiration is feasible and workable, I have spoken to some friends, some of them are here others are elsewhere but all the people that I met in the course of this pursuit have given me hope all through', he stated.
Those who had shown tremendous interest in the coveted office include, Dr. Shetima Mustafa, Ambassador Hassan Adamu, Alhaji Bamanga Tukur, the PDP National Publicity Secretary, Professor Rufai Alkali.
Others are, Alhaji Gambo Lawan, the PDP acting National Secretary, Dr. Musa Babayo, the PDP National Vice Chairman, North East, Senator Paul Wampana, Ambassador Idris Waziri, Professor Jibril Aminu, Alhaji Adamu Muazu, Senator Danjuma Goje, Dr Aliyu Modibbo Umar and Gambo Lawan.
Meanwhile, the hope of contestants from Adamawa State which is tipped as the most favoured state from the north east has continued to dwindle as a result of the level of intrigues playing out.
Those caught up in the power game are mostly the aspirants from Adamawa State. Those in the know say their ambition is currently being threatened by the hard position of the state governor; Murtala Nyako who sources reveal is determined to frustrate their efforts.
Nyako, it was gathered, had expressly tipped contenders from other zones for the office based on what he described as the anti-party activities of the men from his state.
He reportedly accused Aminu, Tukur and Adamu of working against the emergence of President Jonathan in the last election.
Aminu Iyawa, the Senior Special Assistant to the governor on Media and Publicity who spoke to newsmen recently posited that the state government and the state chapter of the party would frustrate efforts being made to see one of them emerge as the PDP national chairman on account that they did not work for the PDP during the last general elections in the state.
He vowed that the state government and the Adamawa chapter of the PDP would tirelessly work against any attempt to make any of Aminu, Tukur and Adamu the national chairman of the PDP.
'Our reasons for taking this decision are simple. The trio neither worked nor identified with the PDP struggle before we achieved the victory in the presidential, national and parliamentary elections last April', he stated.
Iyawa disclosed that since the three aspirants from the state are not competent on account of not being faithful to the party, the governor would not fold his hand to allow them get the post.
To achieve this, it was gathered that governor Nyako may have enlisted some pressure groups to get the party to change its stand on the zoning of the national chairmanship to the North-East.
The governor's choice of this option, a source revealed draws from the fact that once the party succeeds in zoning the chairmanship of the party to the zone, the contenders from Adamawa are not only frontline contestants, but have the clout required for such position which has placed them higher than other aspirants from other state in the zone.
The governor is also reported to have set aside huge financial war chest to sponsor an underdog from any of the other states to frustrate the effort of the trio who are largely seen as heavyweights.
Those in the know say Tukur's offence stemmed from the fact that he allowed his son, Awwal, to contest for the party's ticket for governorship with Nyako in 2007 and also for supporting his political foes calling for the dissolution of the state executive council of the party which he is reportedly controlling.
These actions were alleged to have infuriated Nyako, despite that Tukur joined the campaign train for him to emerge governor of the state.
Those in the know say Aminu and Nyako were former political associates and that the former influenced the latter's decision to defect fro
m the All Nigeria Peoples Party (ANPP) to contest for the governorship of the state in PDP

Original_Search_Title: In Country, It's Not All Work - There Is Play, Too
Headline: Rwanda: In Country, It's Not All Work - There Is Play, Too
Body_Text:
The Rwandan State has an enviable reputation. It functions efficiently, delivers public goods to its citizens and generally registers pluses in whatever it does. Smart, confident and intelligent young people (there is a sprinkling of 50 plus among them) run it.
This corps of highly capable and motivated people have all the country's programmes with all the facts and figures and different scenarios at their fingertips and tips of their tongues, depending on the circumstances.
They have the singular ability to cut through complicated policy issues with the assurance and expertise of a surgeon, and in the twinkling of an eye, come up with the most workable and suitable option. Knowledge oozes from every pore of their skin. It is hardly surprising since Rwanda has elected to be a knowledge-based and driven economy in the shortest time possible.
This is no exaggeration or wishful thinking. It is what is behind the Rwandan miracle - quick recovery from ruin, followed by equally rapid growth, all in a little over a decade and a half.
Of course, there are areas like service delivery where we are still falling short. The legendary efficiency has not yet been brought to bear on the remaining brakes to faster development. But when it does, which is sooner rather than later, the sloppy, indifferent and sluggish delivery of services will be history.
You may be tempted to think that Rwandan officials are an army of smart robots dismantling a backward system and quickly assembling a more sophisticated social and economic structure in its place. Or whizkids with their noses in books or cyberspace, smelling out the smartest ideas. Or even bored bureaucrats buried in some brain-numbing routine.
They are no automatons or super beings. As I found out at the recent national leadership retreat, they are actually ordinary humans - playful and funny and incredibly creative. They are good and at home with comedy as they are with policy issues.
Picture this. The head of Rwanda's high court discards his judicial robes for a clown's gown and becomes the country's chief comedian. He changes from the forbidding judge who intimidates counsel and litigants that appear before him alike to a joking, entertaining man. Or a renowned academic and the country's top governance expert comes down from the lectern to become the former's main accomplice. Unlikely but true.
The duo, in an act to rival any professional stand-up comedians, treated everyone to a hilarious performance that left many aching ribs. Even the usually restrained (at least in public) President Paul Kagame had such a hearty laugh that must have left its mark of pain.
No one escaped the quick wit and sharp barbs of the two-man comedy act. Not even the president of the republic - the most famous unpaid switchboard operator in the country's history.
And the president had set the tone for the hilarious ending of the most relaxed retreat ever. He had earlier humorously, but correctly, prescribed electricity as a potent family planning device.
Laughter, too, had been prescribed by the minister of health as a pleasant and cost-free life-prolonging exercise. And if her prescription has any effect, those who attended the closing of the retreat should have another five years added to their lives, judging from the amount and duration of laughter.
Nothing that took place during the retreat had escaped the sharp eye of the part-time comedians. Whether it was a ministerial shuffle (dance?) in time to the rhythm of mchaka mchaka or a semi-scientific debate on the merits (and dangers) of Coca cola and bananas - they heard and saw it all. Which can only mean one thing - they had been attentive and had taken in all the discussions. If this was a measure of the general attention of all participants, we can rest assured that they got it all and will implement whatever was decided, and the miracle will get bigger.
The stand-in comedians waded into English lexicon and popularised an otherwise unfashionable word, in the process stretching its usage beyond its limited range. The word issue has become so versatile it turns everything within range into an issue.
The young people, also known as the dot com did their thing as they only can do it. They provided entertainment by way of song and dance, which was expected. After all, as they revealed, they practice every weekend. Perhaps less so was the self-deprecating candour about how they juggle their weekend escapades with work - which apparently they do expertly.
The women, not to be outdone, put on a drama of some quality. So did the district mayors.
So, Rwandans have other talents that help them to maintain a balance between the demands of rapid economic and social transformation of their country and the ability to stand back and enjoy what they are creating. The balance, in the right proportions, is obviously needed for the Rwandan miracle to continue.
And as a character in Charles Dickens' Hard Times said in a nice, lisping way, "people cannot alwayth be aworking. They mutht also be amuthed". Rwandans have certainly found a way of doing both and gaining capital out of the experience.

Original_Search_Title: Names of Our Political Parties Meaningless
Headline: Kenya: Names of Our Political Parties Meaningless
Body_Text:
Political parties form one of the pillars in democratic society as they allow people who share common values on leadership to on the basis of their shared beliefs and help to organize a democratic system and ease the election process.
Visionaries like Harry Thuku of Kenya African Union and Ronald Ngala's Kenya African Democratic Union, for example, used nationalist associations to seek representation. As the names of their parties suggest, in that period, unity of Africans was vital in the movement for better representation of Africans in parliament.
Kenya was a one-party state until the first multi-party election in 1992 which saw an explosion of parties. Again, we can read the political mood of the time from the names of the parties. The Forum for Restoration of Democracy [FORD] and Democratic Party [DP] were inspiring names. The parties advocated for better democratic systems in an authoritarian regime. These are examples of good choices in political party names.
The Kenya African National Union, ruled until 2002 when the National Rainbow Coalition came to power. The name rainbow suggested diversity; the people who led this party came from all over Kenya. This was significant since politics in Kenya has been tribal. Kenyans were impressed to see leaders give up their tribal politics and speak in one voice.
NARC union soon bore several children: National Rainbow Coalition of Kenya, Orange Democratic Party, Party of National Unity among others. The ODM, broke away from NARC after the 2005 referendum when Kenyans voted on whether or not to adopt a draft of a new constitution. Those for the draft voted 'yes' symbolized by a banana; while those against it voted 'no' symbolized by an orange. The majority voted against the draft - the Orange 'won' and formed ODM. If the banana side also formed a party - Banana Democratic Movement, and proceeded to win the subsequent election would a banana republic have been the result?
ODM-Kenya, also a child of NARC, re-branded itself to become WIPER Democratic Party. Imagine attending an international meeting where you stand up and say "I am from the Wiper Democratic Party!" What is the meaning implied? Wiper is a part of the car- important during the rain but otherwise soon forgotten.
The choice of PNU, was not too shabby - better than Wiper and Orange - but even for PNU, the unity should be apparent and meaningful. What is 'rainbow' about the present Narc-Kenya? Why retain the name after the original rainbow members bowed out? The 2005 referendum broke something about the spirit and pattern of naming political parties. Anyone analysing the political landscape will think that Kenyans have a strong attachment to fruits and car parts.
In the UK the Conservative Party is mainly supported by the business and middle class for its values in low taxes and small government. Marginalized groups suffer under this regime since social welfare is not a priority to this party. You can derive this from the name. The Social Democratic Party of Germany was formed to represent the working class; it challenged the conservative rule of Chancellor Bismark, championing the cause of improved services for the socially weak. Social democracy can be easily explained.
In Kenya, it is tricky to derive the stand a party has on governance or social welfare, since parties prefer 'catchy' names that are easily chanted. If a party advocates for social welfare of citizens, it is more professional to use a name that reflects values, rather than a name that everyone laughs at during weekly news roundup shows.
We were inspired by the legacy of KAU, KADU, DP, FORD and other parties in recent times. How can we inspire a new generations using fruit? Calling yourself Party of Action (POA) may put you in the foreground, but voters know that all parties are meant to be parties of action. One can also read mischief in the United Republican Party. Are the members' republicans? How long will they be united? What is their unity about? Kenyans are crying out for intelligent inspiration.
Well, as Kenyans it is best to be optimistic. At least the Banana Party did not come into being. We do like fruit, but we cannot be a fruit basket yet. Political parties choose wisely your names and identities. Be on the right side of history. We will soon write on the symbols!
Priscilla Nyokabi Kanyua is the executive director of Kituo cha Sheria while

Original_Search_Title: First Things First, Please, a New Constitution!
Headline: Zimbabwe: First Things First, Please, a New Constitution!
Body_Text:
It is as simple as that and the Movement for Democratic Change better acquaint itself with this reality instead of showing us the soft part of their underbelly again.
No one wants elections more than the people of Zimbabwe because Zimbabweans have some cleaning up to do.
We want to rid ourselves of dead wood, of murderers and of thieves in both parliament and cabinet.
We want to rid ourselves of MDC pretenders who are in government, cabinet and in undeserved party posts.
But above all, Zimbabweans are itching to rid themselves of a coalition government.
This silly arrangement has shown how impossible it is for three parties to work together for a common cause.
We want elections now but not before a new Constitution and the MDC better get this clear in its head.
The absence of a Constitution has caused terrible hardships for people and we cannot be asked, once again, to enter a snake filled dungeon without a few sticks of matches.
Why does the MDC want to slip on the same banana twice?
The South African Foreign minister told her Parliament that a committee is drafting a new constitution in Zimbabwe, after which a referendum and then elections should be held.
"Our government, therefore, expects that there would be no deviation from the provisions of the GPA," she added.
I hope the MDC is listening.
What I found interesting is that immediately after the foreign minister's statement came out, ZANU-PF hawks attacked her and the MDC, warming up to the idea, said it will treat 2012 as a watershed year and vowed to prepare its structures for elections.
That is nonsense from both parties.
The MDC must put all its might in fighting for the writing and adoption of a new Constitution before considering elections. The necessary reforms must be put in place because we need a respectable Constitution that binds all of us; a Constitution that protects the people and the nation from each other while at the same time guaranteeing our freedoms.
Decisions on running the country cannot continue being made by a senile man while sitting alone on his bathroom stool. And the MDC should make the proper conclusion of writing and adoption of a new constitution its primary crusade, instead of telling us they are preparing for elections.
As for ZANU-PF, what do you expect?
They are benefitting from the tattered, irrelevant constitution which no one respects. We cannot continue abusing ourselves like this and the MDC has a responsibility not only to lead but to protect the people.
Just look at how even the Attorney General is behaving; he literally harbours lawbreakers from a certain political party; he does not bother to prosecute certain individuals for crimes that send people from the opposition to jail for decades.
Look at the arrogance of party people as they verbally attack foreign governments for suggesting that proper elections be held only after a new constitution has been installed.
Jonathan Moyo told the Foreign Minister to "shut up". How eloquent of him as a professor. All I can say about Jonathan Moyo is that it is painful to be ordinary, is it not?
Moyo wishes someone would listen to him because he is passé and is sending up bubbles from below the water tank so we know he is down there somewhere.
We don't care.
It is, however, amazing how the MDC continues to play second fiddle to Robert Mugabe.
At every turn before, during and after the formation of the Government of National Unity, the MDC was reduced to reacting to what ZANU-PF did and said.
The conditions under which they joined the GNU were very bad and were tilted in Mugabe's favour, especially considering that Mugabe had lost the elections.
The MDC soldiered on under the most severe conditions and tried to walk the tight rope, hoping that things would work out in the end.
I can no longer absolve the MDC when they agree to elections, thereby putting people's lives in danger.
All we need to do is to look at what happened before and we can see a duplicate of the same.
People will not be free to vote. Last time we lost 200 of our compatriots and no safety measures have been put in place to avoid a repeat of same.
We still have no constitution.
The voters' roll is a shambles.
The media is not free.
Draconian laws are still in place and the MDC itself continues to suffer curtailments in addressing meetings.
The MDC can still not protect anyone. But they are preparing for elections instead of reinforcing their battle strategy to win a new constitution.
First things first, please. The MDC is taking people for granted.
The security issue has not been addressed.
Prevailing conditions do not allow for elections and the MDC knows it.
Liberating a country is literally a relay race: those who start the race are never meant to finish it. This is something that politicians must understand.
Zimbabwe is in a mess because Mugabe refused to pass the baton to the next member of the relay team and now the MDC is preparing us for another round of similar disasters.
Politicians should stop taking people for fools.
And if Mugabe loses again, what will stop him from creating the same stalemate as we have now?
'We are ready for elections..." said MDC spokesperson Douglas Mwonzora the other day.
How does the MDC find enthusiasm in a graveyard?
How many concessions have the MDC made to Mugabe? How many concessions did the MDC get in return?
The heart of the matter is that Zimbabwe is not at all ready to hold free and fair elections at the moment and nobody knows this better than the MDC itself.
Zimbabweans want a clean government run by one party and to hell with coalition governments.
We cannot be coaxed into believing or trusting Robert Mugabe.
Before guaranteeing free and fair elections, will the MDC guarantee to protect the people's lives?
The MDC must hold back until all conditions to hold proper elections are established.
What beliefs do ZANU-PF and the MDC have that people should die for?
What vision for Zimbabwe do Mugabe and Tsvangirai have that is worth dying for?
Tsvangirai wanted to be president of Zimbabwe; Mugabe said no.
He wanted to move into the Prime Minister's residence, Mugabe said no.
Tsvangirai wanted an updated voters' roll, he didn't get that.
He wanted a new constitution, we are still waiting.
He wanted to appoint MDC governors, Mugabe said no.
Tsvangirai wanted to appoint MDC ambassadors, what happened?
He wanted Gono and Tomana fired, they were not.
He wanted Bennett sworn in as minister, Mugabe said no.
Tsvangirai wanted to prosecute those who murdered 200 MDC supporters, Mugabe says no.
Tsvangirai wanted the notorious Joseph Mwale arrested for murdering MDC operatives, Mugabe sent Mwale to protect Tsvangirai.
Mugabe said he wants elections again, Tsvangirai says they are preparing for such.
I am getting concerned.
Will these guys, who are sharing so many goodies amongst themselves, dump each other to please crowds at their rallies or they will hammer out yet another deal to stay at the high table together at the expense of democracy or, to be more accurate, the voters?
I fear we are being prepared for a sequel to the first government of national unity and that spells disaster.
The government of national unity will continue to split the nation as it has done since its inception.
There is no guarantee that the elections will be free and fair.
There is no guarantee that these people will abide by the outcome of the vote.
And, most importantly, there is no guarantee that our citizens will be safe.
Free and fair elections are born out of a free and fair campaign and a level playing ground.
We have none of the above.
I am Tanonoka Joseph Whande and that, my compatriots, is the way it is today, Thursday, March 8th 2012.

Original_Search_Title: End Gender Violence Now, Women Plead
Headline: Kenya: End Gender Violence Now, Women Plead
Body_Text:
Nairobi, Kenya — Kenya on Thursday joined the rest of the world in marking the International Women's Day with calls for concerted efforts and commitment to fight gender based violence.
In Nairobi, various professionals converged to celebrate the success achieved by women in Kenya but also highlighted the challenges that they are still facing.
Johnson & Johnson Manager for Sub Saharan Africa Rene Kiamba condemned the increased attacks on women and men based on 'love gone sour' situations.
"This is illegal, it is inhuman. Animals don't even do the things we do to each other. It is time for the law of love to overtake the law of force. We seem to love force," he decried.
Kiamba who passionately urged men and women to honour respect and reflect on their marriage vows said it was crucial for the Kenyan society to address the demeaning attacks that continue to affect many families.
He likened the latest cases of gender based violence to poverty. "The poverty of being unwanted, unloved and uncared for is the greatest poverty. We cannot continue this way. This is our core problem in our families."
He asked men and women not to cheapen themselves by engaging in unfruitful actions that leave them behind bars or hurt.
"Real men do not rape their daughters. This is a threat to the entire society. Anger and lack of love in the family threatens the very existence as a human race, if we don't deal with it, it will deal with us," he warned.
Supreme Court judge Njoki Ndung'u also raised concern at the rising number of reported cases of violence against men.
"There is an increase not on women-on-male violence, but on men-on-male violence, particularly sexual violence," she asserted.
However she said it went unreported because men who were victims of other men were afraid to seek medical help or report the incidences to the police.
James Jordan Foundation Chief Executive Officer Deloris Jordan took the opportunity to call for empowerment of young girls through nurturing good values that can help them in their future.
She emphasised on the need to ensure that every girl is given equal opportunities to education, career and even decent life.
Former vice President Moody Awori regretted that most girls waste their 12 years of primary and secondary education due to lack of supportive education systems for higher learning especially in the rural areas and also for the urban poor.
"It's heartbreaking to see young girls simply disappear from the scene every year particularly after high school. It is heartbreaking to see graduates selling bananas along the streets due to lack of employment and guidance," said Awori.
He also urged the government to tackle the large unemployment rates in the country, where most girls opt to undesirable lives due to desperation.
Public Health and Sanitation Minister Beth Mugo who is convalescing in the United States after undergoing therapy for cancer sent a message and pledged support from her ministry especially to help women deal with diseases that threaten their health. She also raised concerns over the undying negative practices such as female genital mutilation.

Original_Search_Title: Punishing Litterbugs Will Help Make City Tick Again
Headline: Zimbabwe: Punishing Litterbugs Will Help Make City Tick Again
Body_Text:
Litter disfigures much of Harare, making the capital city look tatty, to say nothing of the stench from piles of garbage piled up in many alleys and along open land. The problem is not insoluble. Some decades ago visitors remarked on how clean and tidy Harare was. Anyone who has visited Namibia will know that a litter-free culture can be created in Africa, drinkers are even expected to dump their bottle tops and smokers their butts in bins in cities there.
But for us to lick the problem several approaches must be followed. One was announced last week.
All shops must now have refuse bins and all kombis must have a bin on board. From Thursday shopkeepers and kombi operators without bins will be arrested.
We were startled over the assumption that there are shops without bins, but presumably the City of Harare and the Environmental Management Agency must know of some otherwise they would not be issuing dire warnings. The bins for kombis sound a good idea but we fear that most of these bins will remain empty unless further steps are taken to stop passengers using the windows to dump litter.
Which brings us to the second approach that is needed -- to stop individuals dumping rubbish in the streets or through their car and bus windows.
The bins blitz should help reduce the piles of garbage in alleys and remove one excuse for street dumping from bus passengers, but will do nothing more to stop people throwing into the street or through windows banana peel, orange peel, apple cores, chip packets, empty take-away boxes, paper bags with pie crumbs, drink cans, till slips, cigarette boxes and everything else that litters our streets.
Thanks to those magnificent ladies employed by the city council who continuously sweep the city centre, much of this is collected as it is dumped. But the true horror of what goes on can be seen early every morning, around 6.30am, as these women hit the streets with their brooms and start cleaning up the mess. Obviously the police cannot arrest every single litterbug, they would be picking up half the population of the city every day. But a start could be made. As the word got around that arrest was possible more people would stop throwing their rubbish around and take it home or dump it in a public bin.
Which brings us to the third leg of the unified approach, the responsibilities of the city council.
There are some public litter bins in the city centre, but too few. More are needed and larger ones are needed. Private companies have done something to alleviate the near complete lack of municipal bins, but more must be done by the council, to provide its own and to work out ways for companies to help in return for advertising.
Again a stroll down any busy street in any Namibian town or city will show what is required, proper bins with hinged tops seem to be favoured there and many belong to businesses who use the front of the bins to advertise their goods and services. We also need such public litter bins in suburban shopping centres, bus terminuses and other places where people stand or sit and wait. Kombi drivers are going to need access to large skips at terminuses to dump the rubbish their on-board bins collect, otherwise they will just empty them in the streets when no one is looking.
At the same time the city council needs to empty these bins, and collect the garbage in the bins it is making shops buy. City director of amenities Mr Dombo Chibanda promised last week to collect city centre garbage daily and said he would roll out daily collections to the suburbs.
Well shopping centres might need daily collections, but quite honestly the rest of us would like, for a start, for Mr Chibanda to collect our rubbish at least once a week for houses and twice a week for flats.
We notice Mayor Muchadeyi Masunda was at the launch of the litter campaign last week. Vast swathes of the eastern suburbs, including we suspect Mr Masunda's own street, have not had their garbage collected for two weeks now. Perhaps Mr Masunda could have a chat to Mr Chibanda on that before he gets more ambitious and stops collecting every day instead of not collecting once a week.
It is all very well, and an excellent idea, to make shops, bus operators and households to collect their garbage and store it properly until it is picked up, but the fourth leg of any campaign must see the city collecting that bagged and binned garbage regularly, perhaps on the same day each week, or the same time each day, that appears on its own advertisements.
But once all four legs are operational, not just the one announced last week, there is a good chance that we can restore Harare to the cleanliness it once enjoyed.

Original_Search_Title: A Look Back in Time...
Headline: Nigeria: A Look Back in Time...
Body_Text:
On at least two occasions some two years ago, I wrote about what I considered the outsourcing of justice from our shores (Ibori and the Outsourcing of Justice May 18; Outsourcing Justice: Time to Claim Our Profession Back! June 15).
Last week, former Delta State Governor, James Onanafe Ibori pleaded guilty at the Southwark Crown Court in London to charges of money laundering and conspiracy to defraud and was promptly convicted, to be sentenced next month. The evidence against him was damning, the words of the prosecutor and the Judge, scathing. At the end of the day we seemed no better than a banana republic, a Kleptocracy, a nation of anything goes.
Justice -outsourced though it was - has now been done, but at what cost to Nigeria's image? Our cover story this week examines this.
As some of those words seem to have come back to haunt us, in a manner of speaking, I thought it apposite to reproduce excerpts from both columns:
'As things stand, it appears that the EFCC and MET are each seeking to file extradition charges against the former Governor. Given the high drama that went on in the name of effecting his arrest before he somehow slipped out of the country in spite of (or, some say, because of) the tough talk, show of force and impressive sound bytes from the upper echelons of the law enforcement agencies and the presidency; given the antecedents of the various criminal cases against him which preceded this latest show; given the high controversy of transferring the criminal case against him from Kaduna to Asaba in spite of the fact the Federal High Court is one; to the equally high controversy of the setting up of a new Federal High Court in Asaba which eventually tried and acquitted him of all 170 counts of corruption; to the EFCC's choice of counsel for prosecuting the trial given an alleged earlier opinion he'd written for the office of the Attorney-General of the Federation stating that the man had no case to answer and in which the public were left wondering just who the EFCC was for; and given the role of the former Attorney-General of the Federation who often conducted himself as a rather bellicose lawyer and spokesman for certain individuals including Ibori, as opposed to the public good, not many are routing for the EFCC. Surprise, surprise. In any event, I'm not certain we have an extradition or mutual legal assistance treaty with the UAE.
'And, it seems, not many are rooting for our justice delivery system (investigation, arrest, trial, conviction, et al) either. They would rather take their chances with Britain's which is tried and tested and even with its imperfections, mostly works. We would rather outsource justice. Unlike outsourcing legal processes however it has nothing to do, at least directly, with economics and reducing overheads, but plenty to do with trust. Rightly or wrongly, fairly or unfairly, we do not trust a system that sometimes, as opposed to mostly, works. This is a terrible indictment of our criminal justice sector - not only the judiciary but the entire system inclusive of the Police and other investigative agencies and Prisons. We all know what is wrong, countless Administration of Justice reports have been written, brilliantly articulating the issues and White papers released but not implemented. The National Judicial Council has stepped up its game in recent years taking for instance, the unprecedented step of dismissing two Court of Appeal Justices, but more still needs to be done. We still have funny characters on the Bench who need to be gone. Yesterday. The chickens are coming home to roost. For us, embarrassingly, internationally!
'The arrest and eventual extradition of James Ibori to Britain to face trial is not, in my view, only about securing a conviction if the case against him is proved but more importantly, about going through the full rigours of the trial and all that pertain to it. Back home, there's the strong perception that all attempts to investigate or prosecute him were half hearted and programmed to fail. From the ex-convict saga through the 170 count charges of corruption of which he was acquitted through the bloodbath that seemed imminent between the law enforcement agencies and militants in the so-called attempt to effect his arrest in Oghara recently none, unfortunately, inspired confidence in our criminal justice delivery system
'If James Ibori is innocent and is being unduly persecuted and set up by his political detractors as he vehemently insists, by all means let him walk a free man; I've no problem with that. But only after facing a proper trial in which justice is not only done but is also manifestly seen to be done. He deserves no less, we deserve no less.
'And if this can only be achieved outside the country, tough.' - The Wig & Skirt, Ibori and the Outsourcing of Justice May 18, 2010.
'In my column of May 18, 2010, Ibori and the Outsourcing of Justice, I stated that embarrassingly for us the chickens were coming home to roost on the international front. Former Governor James Ibori's ex-convict saga through yet another saga culminating in his 170 count charge acquittal as well as the fiasco surrounding attempts to arrest him before he fled to Dubai, I pointed out, did not inspire confidence in our criminal justice delivery system.
'Barely three weeks after my column the stark reality of that damning observation was brought home to us vide the pronouncements of Judge Christopher Hardy at Southwark Crown Court whilst sentencing two of the former Governor's associates for money laundering and fraud. Last Monday, Judge Hardy uttered words which offered us no comfort, essentially slamming the Nigerian Judiciary as well as casting serious doubt on the country's commitment to fighting corruption and money laundering.
'Nigeria's judiciary, he said, had been usurped. He would therefore bring down to bear the full weight of British law to serve as punishment and deterrent. When you break down those words, the enormity of the indictment of our system begins to sink in. Usurped means appropriated, commandeered, seized, grabbed, taken over, arrogated, elbowed in, highjacked, supplanted, swapped, laid hold off, annexed, etcetera - take your pick, none looks good. Justice Hardy must have taken note of the shenanigans of the former Attorney-General of the Federation, Michael Aondoakaa, SAN and his stubborn refusal (with tacit support from his principal) to render mutual legal assistance to the British government in spite of Nigeria's international obligations to do so; the attempts by Nigerian officialdom to stall the Southwark Crown Court trial and indeed to help evade trial and bring one of the accused back to Nigeria; some letters to his court which purportedly emanated from the Federal High Court, Asaba which tried Ibori and the twists and turns the trial took, not to mention, the high drama that surrounded the several adjournments of the Federal High Court judgment. The Nigerian judiciary having been commandeered then, dependable British law to the rescue!
'Ibori's spokesman, Tony Eluemunor, in the wake of last week's sentencing blamed 'a mighty international attempt orchestrated from Nigeria'. Really? And were the jury which convicted the duo a part of that mighty international and orchestrated attempt?
'As I pointed out in that column, the judicial system in Britain mostly works and that is why the Nigerian public rooted for it as a veritable venue for trying Ibori if eventually extradited, and his associates. There's a strong public perception that justice will be obtained there but not here when some of our highly placed citizens are caught in the web of the law. And who can blame them? Examples abound. Cases drag on for years on end and some never see the light of day. When high profile convictions are eventually secured, they are often a mere slap on the wrist.
'... All said, it's up to the bar and bench to claim our profession back, and urgently. Else we endure even more scathing remarks from foreign jurisdictions and lose entirely the confidence of the international community in our justice delivery system. If this happens, we can kiss our dreams of foreign direct investments goodbye; no one wants to invest in a system that will not inspire confidence should they require their rights or liabilities determined and seek redress.' - The Wig & Skirt Outsourcing Justice: Time to Claim Our Profession Back! June 15, 2010.

Original_Search_Title: KNUST Chancellor Goes Bananas on Journalists
Headline: Ghana: KNUST Chancellor Goes Bananas on Journalists
Body_Text:
Kumasi — Drama unfolded at the Council Chamber of the Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST) last Monday, when the Vice Chancellor of the University, Prof. William Ellis Otoo, launched a vituperative attack on a reporter of Luv 99.5 FM, a subsidiary of the Multimedia Group Limited.
The statement by the Vice Chancellor was a clear deviation from his plea during the latter part of last year, when he appealed to the media to assist the university in its activities during a press interaction
The usually calm Vice Chancellor shocked media personnel at the chamber, when he subjected the broadcast journalist, Elton John Brobbey, to a spurious verbal assault, attacking him and the entire Multimedia Company for allegedly publishing negative stories about the university.
But, for the intervention of the Head of the Vodafone Business Solution, Mr. Derrick Appiah, the embarrassing scene created by Prof. Otoo would have degenerated into something untoward.
The whole drama, which lasted almost 30 minutes, began when the Vice Chancellor entered the Council Chamber to participate in a programme organised by Vodafone Ghana Limited, a mobile telecommunications network in Ghana.
When the Vice Chancellor entered the hall, he enquired if there was a Luv FM reporter around, and when Elton Brobbey identified himself as one, the drama began.
The emotionally-charged Prof. Otoo started: "Do you people think the university cannot operate without you; you bunch of illiterates sit behind the microphone and spew nonsense against the university, this is what Ghanaian Times did and was barred from this university," the Vice Chancellor ranted.
Prof. Otoo continued: "If not for Vodafone, I would have sacked all of you from this hall; who do you think you are? You think you can just sit in your studios and do whatever you like?"
The Vice Chancellor was said to have been angry as a result of a recent report about an armed robbery incident which occurred on the campus of the university, and was covered by one of the station's reporters, Ohemeng Tawiah.
Prof. Otoo did not understand why the reporter should grant interviews to some students who expressed worry about the state of security on campus, a situation, which was later admitted by the University Relations Officer.
As the drama unfolded, the reporter, who was at this juncture dumbfounded, could not reply, as he initially did not understand why the Vice Chancellor was behaving in such a strange manner.
What appeared to have made matters worse was a statement passed by the Registrar of the University, Mr. Yebo-Okra, to the effect that media personnel in the country were a gang of ignorant people employed by their family members who have opened radio stations.
"Who are they; don't mind them, they are just bunch of illiterates whose relatives have opened radio stations and have employed them; they don't know anything," Mr. Yebo-Okra raved worsening the terrible situation.
It, however, had to take the intervention of the Vodafone Executive to cool tempers down and enquire from the Chancellor why he was verbally assaulting journalists, before Prof. Otoo explained the reason behind his action.
His explanation got Elton Brobbey even more surprised, because he believed the station had been very professional in handling that particular story.
Elton explained that when the incident occurred, his colleague first contacted the KNUST Police, who confirmed the incident of robbery, before granting an interview to the University Relations Officer.
The reporter then asked the Vice Chancellor to point out specifically where his station acted unprofessionally in the handling of the story, but Prof. Otoo could not, and continued with his verbal attacks.
When the reporter was asked if he felt disturbed about the incident and wanted to comment on it, Elton answered in the negative, and said there was no reason for him to feel disturbed, because he believed the allegations were untrue.
He, however, said he would prefer his management to handle the matter officially.

Original_Search_Title: Artists Ride Wheels of Creative Fortune
Headline: Kenya: Artists Ride Wheels of Creative Fortune
Body_Text:
In the next couple of months, the Chinese capital of Beijing will host an event that is expected to grease the wheels of fortune that have been on the roll in Kenya's visual art scene.
It will host an exhibition of contemporary Kenyan art, featuring more than 30 local artists' works for show and sale.
The exhibition will be the culmination of a journey that began three weeks ago at the Nairobi National Museum where a Chinese promoter has been collecting the artwork in readiness for shipping to Beijing.
Followers of the Kenyan art scene say the Beijing exhibition is poised to be a game-changer for the artists, given the interest the Chinese have shown in the works of their compatriots such as Elkana Ong'esa and Mwaura Ndekere.
China is, however, not the first country to cultivate a rich taste for Kenyan art.
In the past decade alone, Kenyan artists have exhibited their works all over Europe, Asia, Central and North America, including Canada, the United States and Mexico.
Keen followers of the art scene know that Kenyan art has in the past five years gone global, giving the artists a spot on the international stage with huge monetary rewards that are expected to start rivalling those of the country's elite athletes.
Signs of the fortune that lies in contemporary Kenyan art came only recently with sale of the works of artists like Wanyu Brush and Adrian Nduma to international buyers for millions of shillings.
"After Adrian's story aired on NTV, we immediately received calls from parents wanting to know where their children could learn similar skills so that they too could reap the same benefits," said William Ndwiga, the managing director of Nairobi's Little Art Gallery, who featured on the same TV programme.
In the past, most parents have frowned on their children for choosing a career in the fine arts but attitudes are changing.
Nonetheless, few people realise how widely Kenyan artists have travelled, exhibited and sold their work for substantial prices internationally.
A look at the fortunes of local artists who have "gone global" with their art exhibiting and selling overseas, however demonstrates that a career in fine art is after all a worthy investment.
Exceptional
The list of most exceptional amounts that Kenyan artists have fetched for their art includes the Sh22 million price in the China.
Mwaura Ndekere, the Kenyatta University lecturer, whose painting was auctioned in China for charity, all those millions went to the Hope Schools foundation set up by Chinese nationals build schools in semi-arid parts of Kenya.
Other Kenyans have picked competitive prices selling their work in global markets such as the US, UK, Scandinavia, Switzerland and South Korea.
For instance, three artists from the Kibera-based art network named Maasai Mbili recently returned from Sweden with more than a half a million shillings from the sale of their art to refurbish their art studio.
"We managed to raise around 5000 kroner (Sh500,000)," said Gomba Otieno who went as an invited guest with M2 artists OK Rabala and Ashif to Stockholm.
"We sold 20 paintings," said Otieno, seated now in M2's spacious "slum" studio.
That means each piece sold for an average of Sh25,000 unlocking the value of art in a style that is yet to be seen on the local scene.
Camille Wekesa, another artist, struck a similar note when she exhibited in London a few years back and sold her "medium sized" paintings for the equivalent of Sh400,000 per piece.
More recently in London, Peterson Kamwathi became the first Kenyan artist to ever have his artwork included in a Sotheby's art auction.
His painting entitled Kadhis Court II was at the time (late 2011) priced at $3,500 or approximately Sh350,000.
Gakunju Kaigwa is another Kenyan artist who has exhibited in a number of overseas markets from Scotland to San Francisco and South Africa.
In Scotland, his tall stone sculpture sold for the equivalent of Sh120,000; his monumental wood sculptures sold for even more at Sh144,000 and Sh176,000 each.
Kaigwa did even better in the US, selling his bronze pieces in the range of Sh175,000 to Sh210,000.
Unfortunately, after he got his Master's degree in Public Art from the University of Dundee, he wasn't able to bring most of his sculptures home to Kenya, and was forced to leave a stock of untapped wealth overseas.
Kaigwa isn't the only Kenyan artist who has had challenges getting their artworks home following international exhibitions.
Bertiers Mbatia, for instance, had a terrible time getting his art shipped back from the Dak'Art Biennele in Senegal.
And Banana Hill artists affiliated with Shine Tani's gallery had problems getting their art returned from the US after an American promoter failed to return paintings she had failed to exhibit.
Shine is wiser today, especially after winning the prestigious German 'Missio' art award and travelling to five European countries, both exhibiting and visiting galleries and museums in Germany, France, Austria, Belgium and Switzerland.
He's also exhibited in Denmark, the US and Italy.
"Now I am more careful. I also set a standard price for my buyers whether they are local or international," says the managing director of the Banana Hill Art Gallery. "On average my work sells for KSh180,000."
That's a little less than what a former member of Shine's studio, Joseph Cartoon, charges per painting.
The art that he is sending to China is, for instance, prized at Sh300,000 on average.
He reduces his prices slightly if a buyer like the South Korean Kenyan art connoisseur Dr. J.H. Kwang buys 'in bulk', say 20 paintings at a time to sell in Seoul, where Kwang has an African Art Gallery of his own.
One artist who doesn't have a problem dealing with unsold art overseas is Kioko Mwitiki.
Kioko's monumental scrap metal wildlife sculptures (which he ships by the container-load to California) are so highly valued at the San Diego Zoo, where he has been a guest artist for two American summers.
For Kioko, whatever is not sold either remains on display or gets shipped to another state for exhibition and sale saving him the pain of shipping it back.
Display
"Some of my sculptures were shipped from California to Arizona, to be part of my Desert Museum display in Tuscon," Kioko said.
Like Mwaura Ndekere, part of the revenue made from the sale of Kioko's art goes to public service projects sponsored by the Foundation for Women, an American NGO.
"That is the least I can do for women, given that I was raised by a single mother, and I have a high regard for women," Kioko said.
Nonetheless, while Kioko may be comfortable shipping container-loads of his art to the States, a number of Kenyan artists who have also 'gone global' are now more cautious about losing track of their art once it is gone overseas.
Several have specifically sought to learn from the experience of artists like Shine Tani and even Bertiers Mbatia.
Mary Ogembo is one artist who enjoys attending international artist workshops but no longer creates large-scale paintings while working outside Kenya.
"I now create works that I can pack up and carry home with me. Otherwise, I don't know if I will ever see my work again."
One man who has devised a strategy to get around the problem of potentially losing artworks once they are sent abroad is Patrick Kinuthia.
"I have a brother in the US and he markets my work there. He only sells prints of my paintings, so the originals never get lost," Kinuthia said.
Commissions
The other drawback to exhibiting outside Kenya is that often the artist has no control over the commissions made by the international gallery where his or her art is on show.
For instance, just recently Kaafiri Kariuki was exhibiting in Zanzibar and a man from the Czech Republic bought his painting for $850 or around Sh75,000.
"But the commission claimed by the host hotel was extremely high. "At 60 per cent, the profit was hardly worth the effort," he noted.
Bad as it may sound, the artists say all this is evidence that their work has indeed gone global, giving them not only opportunities to market and sell for good money, but also gain greater visibility in the global art market.
It is such global marketing initiatives that have enabled Richard Onyango to showcase the largest private African art collection in the world owned by the Italian patron, Jean Pigozzi and made Samuel Githui's painting Traffic Jam a permanent museum collection in Milan.

Original_Search_Title: How Far This Country Has Sunk With Makubuya and Bbumba
Headline: Uganda: How Far This Country Has Sunk With Makubuya and Bbumba
Body_Text:
This year we mark 50 years since Uganda became an independent state. Twenty six of those years have been under the uninterrupted leadership of Yoweri Museveni and his band of revolutionaries.
It is enough time for us to say how far we have come and predict with a degree of certainty where we are going basing on major trends and happenings from the past and the present.
The entire life of the current (9th) Parliament, which is about a year old, has been one of probing and exposure of government's failures, especially on corruption. Six ministers have been forced to step aside or resign as a result.
What has caused the trouble for all the ministers is basically stealing or an attempt to acquire wealth by grabbing, outright theft or taking advantage of their official ministerial privileges to illegally gain an unfair economic advantage. All these acts of theft are carefully masked in the term 'corruption' that is now a fairly acceptable euphemism for raw theft.
Looking at the woes of the latest victims, especially Prof. Khiddu Makubuya, helps to summarise the state of Uganda under Museveni and where we are going if the same system and people are in charge in the near future.
How did Makubuya, a professor of Law with letters from the prestigious University of Yale, end up being led down the garden path (if at all) by a one Hassan Basajjabalaba? Basajjabalaba was initially known as a hides and skins trader. Now his name is synonymous with various dubious huge 'compensation' deals. This activity has bequeathed the title 'businessman' onto his name.
The 26 years of Uganda under Museveni have been the years of the breakdown of any semblance of order in as far as the proper workings of government and its institutions are concerned. Power and real money is now concentrated in the hands of a few cronies who are well connected politically like Basajjabalaba.
Those who aspire to have a share of these related commodities -power and money- must climb down the high horse and submit themselves to these cronies and do their bidding so as to partake in the grabbing at the high table.
That is how professors and other presumably respectable people under the Museveni regime have managed to roll themselves in the mud and do despicable things for their survival.
For his part, Makubuya as Attorney General stunned the world with some of his advice, especially when he claimed that Dr Kizza Besigye should not stand in the 2006 presidential election because he was 'tainted with illegalities.' Yet Besigye was only a suspect (and therefore presumed to be innocent) in cases which he later won! Makubuya remained Attorney General.
Many professors and distinguished people tied dried banana leaves around their bodies in support of the third term claiming that Museveni was the only one with a vision to lead Uganda. Many have privately expressed misgivings.
The source of this pragmatism is the knowledge of the power equation. For instance, if you fall sick and you are in the good books of the President and his cronies, you stand higher chances of acquiring an air ticket to Europe for treatment at the expense of the taxpayer. If you are on the wrong side of the same President and his cabal, you will have to take your chances in the death traps that our local hospitals have become and prepare to die like the rest of us.
So, the ultimate goal is to please the President and his cronies and do everything that is their bidding, including taking risks to break the law for the good of that same president instead of say resigning. But it does not stop with politicians alone. There is a photo on the Internet of a group of leaders of the Uganda Journalists Association receiving over Shs150 million in cash from Ms Amelia Kyambadde, then PPS to the President, to put up their headquarters-- something they have never done to date. Yet journalists are supposed to watch government and critique its expenditure.
With the sort of power equation that Museveni has built, we all become vulnerable and susceptible to being bought and silenced with money because there are no state institutions or a social safety net to take care of us in our times of need.
That is how low we have sunk 26 and 50 years on, and continue to sink. It will be very difficult to rise out of this predicament because the only insurance and guarantees one has for survival is being in the good books of those in the higher echelons of power. The saddest part is that to enter those good books may most times require us to do very bad things. The present and the future is worrying.
Mr Sengoba is a commentator on political and social issues

Original_Search_Title: Ugandans Are Coming
Headline: South Sudan: Ugandans Are Coming
Body_Text:
In the article, "The Kenyans are coming," we saw how Kenyans can invade this country employing various business skills and know how, and the North Sudanese apprehension that Kenyans are indeed serious competitors capable to invade this country economically. In this article, we shall see interesting colorful economic and technical ways and means Ugandans have used to invade South Sudan economically; what are the justifications and how to overcome this threat.
It was four years ago. I used to take my dinner meals in this simple roadside open-air restaurant owned and run by a number of Ugandan women. I liked this place for two reasons; first, the place was not far from where I resided, second, food recipe here was simple, tasteful and nourishing; never mind surrounding poor sanitation and a possible typhoid fever since we are in Juba. Simple, cheap and nourishing food is definitely one of the items that are now in short supply in this country.
It was about 7.00 p.m. I was munching my dinner under the makeshift erected by the roadside under a glaring light bulb when this slim girl took her seat just across the small wooden table. When food was placed in front of her she snapped a grace, picked the fork and began to take her dinner with an apparent endearment.
Seeing that, I recalled her closing up with the Ugandan woman serving the food, whispering and pointing at this and that cooking utensil. I was watching her. She made a remark that it has been a long time since she had last taken matoke, jinjaro and the rest of the Ugandan recipes all in one plate; "Are you a Ugandan?" I asked expressing some curiosity. "Yes," she answered.
- I thought you were a Sudanese.
- No, I am Ugandan.
- Where have you been?
- I have just arrived from Awil...eeeh very far...almost three hours by plane.
- It is truly far.
The young woman managed to reveal to me on this small dinner table that she was a UN employee, and that coming back from Northern Bahr El Ghazal State she was surprised to find a lot of change in the neighborhood because of the demolition that had taken place during her absence that was intended to enlarge the small rock dominated lane, and that she managed to see many parts of that State including Abyei and that she had really missed this kind of food she is now fondly taking.
- There are a lot of Ugandans in this country.
- Yes. I even saw four Ugandan young men in North Bahr el Ghazal.
- (I remarked with a smile) Ugandans will soon swarm all those parts of the country (she gave a small nice smile with her head down).
"I'm afraid," I added with a small smile naturally that "one day you Ugandans will all be sucked into South Sudan if you keep coming in like that." She smiled larger.
The young Ugandan lady, whom I was about to take for a South Sudanese because of the close resemblance, landed on her dish with an apparent good appetite:-
- You like this kind of food very much.
- Eeeh...I have not tasted this food for a long time, (Using Ugandan accent though very clear English).
- What did you take there?
- Eeeh...bread and.... (This means that she didn't enjoy the food she used to take in those parts of the country).
- You don't like bread.
- Well, eeeh.... (She is forking through matoke, jinjaro and the rest while doodling attempting to shy away).
