I think that this is just one of the beautiful aspects of the prevailing peace in this country that there are so many roadside restaurants in Juba and in many other townships in the county that can be frequented by people like the writer of this article in the evenings and find cheap but nourishing food.
Nevertheless, the declaration of independence suddenly revealed the fact that South Sudan is seriously deficient in sources of food supplies, channels of distribution and skilled manpower to manage supply and distribution of food. This was and is still so and may continue to be like that because South Sudanese have not yet acquired the skills to start and run roadside canteens and roadside restaurants in the neighborhoods to say the least.
Largely, many Janubiyeen saw trading and still regard it as basically an alien Mundukuru profession, and that it is shameful or bothersome to trade; why should a nice looking young man-woman disturb him/herself in trading? Still, more South Sudanese place the blame on lack of capital, "where do I find capital to start business...eh." The writer of this article believes that the issue is largely a matter of disposition and mind set.
To solve this problem, it is in the opinion of this writer that governors and mayors in the different States may start to encourage locals to begin to establish canteens in the neighborhoods (ahyaa.) For it is not proper to see foreign faces running canteens and roadside restaurants inside the (ahyaa) after this expensive independence, anyway for how long shall this situation continue?
Right after the signing of the Comprehensive Peace Agreement in 2005 it was found out that there was a serious lack of accommodation, food and clean drinking water supplies all over South Sudan, disregard health services, education, good roads, public water supply, public electricity, public security and decent accommodation. Not least, there was an urgent need for skilled labor in all fields all over South Sudan in order to start the cycle of normal civil life. All these urgent issues needed to be addressed immediately; by whom, how and when?
Realizing the critical condition of supplies and services in South Sudan, Ugandan work force suddenly swarmed in with Rwenzori water supply to be the first urgent Ugandan benevolent intervention tool. Supplying clean drinking water in disposable plastic bottles at the beginning of the Comprehensive Peace Agreement (CPA) implementation stage showed Ugandan far sight business creativity, perspicacity, and quick responsiveness.
When this writer came to Juba 2007, he was told that conditions had already improved much better than they were a year ago. But, what drew his attention, among other things, was the prevalent Ugandan presence manifested in the following manner:
- Hawking business: Ugandans were everywhere hawking as still they do. You could see both strong bodied Ugandan men and women loaded with all kinds of goods walking tirelessly the whole day and under all weather conditions one would think that these are rather walking shops. The writer would wonder, when do these people rest, and did they eat really.
- Urgent clean drinking water supply: Rwenzori water distribution vans roamed about, and soon water tankers driven by Ugandans and then Ethiopians appeared in the streets of Juba distributing Nile water from door to door. In spite of this, there are strong muscle young Ugandans who still insist to use bicycles and brute force to supply water from the Nile and distribute it from door to door. This is definitely one job among many no Junubi can contemplate to do.
- Sewage cleaning: The sharp Ugandan eyes spotted the urgent need for sewage disposal in Juba and immediately introduced sewage disposal tankers on time to clear sewages. Imagine there was no service of the kind in Juba by then and up to this moment. The infamous newspaper in Khartoum called "Al Intibaha" reported that Ugandans were using sewage disposal tankers to supply drinking water with in Juba. This is impudent, isn't it? But when shall this system of sewage cleaning come to an end?
- Informal accommodation: To the best knowledge of this writer, there are few formally planned residential areas in South Sudan. But, Mayor Baballa gets furious whenever a hideous crime is committed in many of the unplanned public residential areas which he tends to connect to daylong-nightlong bars wedged inside the neighborhoods. Mr. Mayor, see that the streets into the interior are sufficiently large and properly lighted and leave the rest to the residents. Nevertheless South Sudan, Juba in particular, expects to receive more eager local job seekers, foreign workforce and serious investors in the coming years. This means that demand for accommodation and land shall continue. This writer and other concerned citizens are waiting to see how shall anti land grabbing people, the Mayor and the town planning authorities in Juba face the increasing demand for accommodation? Nevertheless, under these pressing conditions, increasing Ugandan presence in Juba demanded accommodation which they themselves have successfully provided. How did they do it?
- Roadside restaurants: Incoming Ugandan women did not waste time but prepared food agreeable to their taste along the roads where it could be practicable. And because food is tasteful and cheap perhaps, this led to a prosperous roadside restaurant business sector in Juba and elsewhere. Besides, Ugandan women and both Eritrean and Ethiopian women have their own roadside restaurants.
- Mini-bus transport: Soon Juba turned into a bustling large township and the need for transport became urgent. It was the Ugandan young men who first initiated this vital sector and started to supply and drive mini buses. They were soon joined by the Ethiopians.
- Boda-boda: Mini-bus business was not enough. The Ugandans soon introduced (boda-boda) service into Juba transport system. By the way, there is nothing that exasperates this writer and drives him crazy more than motorcycles running alongside, behind and in front of the vehicles using the same very narrow streets. But here is a situation that has imposed itself while the traffic authorities seem to be completely powerless to confront it. But, imagine there were no boda-bodas in Juba and in other parts of the country, how difficult it is to walk on foot into the interior of the neighborhoods under the heat or under the rain. Not only that Boda-bodas transport travelers quickly to and from the airport when taxi fare is so high, and one day this writer took a boda-boda to the airport, what could he have done.
- Second hand cars: Realizing Janubiyeen crave for vehicles, immediately Ugandans rushed their second-hand cars into South Sudan and these cars have congested the streets of the major towns of South Sudan. One thing; Ugandan cars come in with the driving wheels to the right and this is a clear violation of the traffic regulations in this young Republic that strictly demand to keep right. Why are the traffic authorities or some higher authorities so lax on this very serious issue, and until when shall vehicles with the right hand driving wheels be tolerated? Where on earth can one find vehicles with left hand and right hand driving wheels legally licensed and run side by side?
- Mechanics and related activities: As the number of vehicles kept increasing Ugandan mechanics soon appeared in Juba, and they are rendering a very good job by helping to maintain the growing number of vehicles. This means that up to this moment sufficient competent local mechanics have not yet appeared.
- Construction and related activities: One of the most important sectors Ugandans have managed to introduce and occupy is construction. One can hardly imagine how conditions could have been by now had Ugandan construction labor force not arrived, why? Because, some skilled South Sudanese construction labor is still stranded in North Sudan so it appears, while some others are scattered. In the meantime, the Ugandans seem to have succeeded to impose the Ugandan building style which does not allow enough light and ventilation through. They should know that South Sudan is a very hot country.
- Carpentry: Ugandans have proved that they know what they do. The way it looks, though the Ugandan carpenters who have rushed into Juba may not be the best in carpentry back home, yet they are doing the best they can. Therefore, if you have just finished building a nice house and would like to grace it with wooden doors and windows, there are good roadside Ugandan carpenters available.
- Iron works: If you are in the process of building a nice house and would like to secure it with a strong and attractive iron gate, iron doors and iron windows just contact any Ugandan roadside iron workshop. Now beside mechanics, this is one of the very few areas in which some ironwork technicians from North Sudan are competing; Ugandans offer you the Ugandan style, while North Sudanese offer the Arab style. Choose.
- Food Supply: Because South Sudan relies exclusively on imported food, and having realized the large food supply gap, Ugandan suppliers never blinked but rushed into South Sudan bringing in all kinds of consumables that include edible oil, meat, chicken, fish, onion, spices, hot pepper, banana, lemon, all kinds of vegetables, flour, salt, sugar, tea, matches, eggs, beans, jinjaro, potatoes, Irish potatoes, yoghurt, watermelon, biscuits, soap, tooth paste etc...
- Hair dressing: Formal and informal occasions are bound to pass by, and this prompted Ugandans to introduce roadside hair dressing salons for men, beside hair cutting and head-balling to leave it shining. By now most young and old men have gone bald-headed including some ministers and some senators. These people insist to go bald-headed to look like pumpkin or egg-like round, black and shining. Why, what is the message?
- Women beauty salons: Ugandan women immediately spotted the need for ladies hair dressing salons and therefore did not hesitate to establish roadside beauty salons and parlors, doing braiding and the rest of beauty requirements. Now women hair dressing has become a very serious business sector. If you want a proof, look at a Miss Malaika pageant held every year and you will definitely see Ugandan beauty finger traces behind.
- Selling used clothes - mainly jackets and trousers - is one of the notable Ugandan successful mobile businesses. As a result, one can imagine that all these smartly dressed men in Juba and other parts of South Sudan must have acquired their jackets from these roaming Ugandans who sell these items cheap and this writer is not an exception. Nice isn't it?
- Nail clipping: Incidentally, Ugandans have discovered that there are some Janubiyeen who have a few extra newly issued pounds to throw away. And so, there are some smart slim size Ugandans who walk around in town with their nail clipping equipments looking for those who need this kind of service.
- Newspaper selling: Ugandan women in particular intend to teach us lessons here how to persevere. Daily you can see the Ugandan girls roaming the streets of Juba selling newspapers and magazines. And recently some Janubiyeen have joined the trail of newspaper selling, what is the reason?
- Airtime: Ugandan women were the first to introduce this business in Juba. For some reason, however, some Janubiyeen have started to push Ugandans away from this sector, why, is it because it is easy or because it is more money making?
- Music and CD selling: Ugandans introduced this business a long time ago. Music is taste, but Ugandans have partially succeeded to popularize their local music brand among South Sudanese youth in Juba however, they sell all kinds of music and movie CDs.
- Housekeeping: This means that there is no job described as decent and fetches money that a Ugandan cannot do. Compare this to the attitude of our local young men/women who shy away from doing domestic service.
- Restaurant maids: Ugandan women are dominating in service rendering in good looking restaurants here in Juba and in other towns in South Sudan. Service rendering is one of the jobs our young men especially women shun, why...culture and attitude problems perhaps. Does this mean that Janubiyeen and Janubiyat have been affected by the traditional Arab North Sudan attitude that Arab women should not do any manual job, and that a good-looking young man/woman from a certain family should not do certain jobs, or what?
- Hotel catering: With the exception of a few Eritrean and Ethiopian owned hotels, the rest of the hotels recruit Ugandan, Kenyan, Eritrean and Ethiopian women and men. However, in some of these hotels or motels, some South Sudanese women and men are left to do the cleaning and scrubbing, not bad isn't it?
- Dry cleaning: This writer could not imagine that one day he could live to see dry cleaning facility in South Sudan, but thanks to the Ugandans because, dry cleaning is here in Juba and probably in Bentiu and in other towns in the new republic. Thanks to the Ugandans again, because beside automatic dry cleaning there are many roadside manual laundry places called "dobbi" all over the town.
- Mobile battery charging: Ugandans have introduced this sector some years ago. Many jobless people from near and far away neighborhoods flock into the center of the city every day in order to socialize, have a meal, many of them including this writer find chance to snooze under a tree and meanwhile have their mobile batteries charged; interesting, isn't it?
In addition to the Ugandan incoming goods and services, there are other Ugandan invisibles that South Sudanese benefit from such as:-
- Education: After the joyful birth of the Republic of South Sudan some financially capable parents scurried to Uganda and enrolled their dear ones at various schools. Definitely, this is so kind of the Ugandans. But, for how long shall this situation continue, and when shall the problem of education be addressed, and what can financially incapable parents do for the time being. This writer and other concerned Janubiyeen have been advised to be patient. On the other hand, this condition has made some Ugandans to believe that Janubiyeen are rich, and therefore could be tempted to take advantage. But, is it true really that Janubiyeen are rich, if not what is that can be done?
- Medical treatment: This writer has not yet learned that financially capable Janubiyuun fly to Kampala for medical treatment; rather, they prefer to fly to India and other far away destinations for the purpose of treatment. Does this mean that it is easy to fly to India and that treatment is excellent and cheap there? But definitely this means that medical treatment in South Sudan is not yet a priority, and that financially incapables like the writer of this article should have to wait and patiently endure many of the locally hard-to-cure diseases until conditions improve, when shall they improve?
What does all this mean? This definitely means a lot; that Ugandans are a very serious creative hard working people, they are modest because they can accept to do any job provided it gains a heavy-in-the pocket dollar, they value work, Ugandans value income no matter how little and therefore they spare no effort for that. Ugandans have rendered invaluable service to the people of South Sudan just at the time when no one came in to render the kind of badly needed assistance or a service, and why should foreigners rush in to do these kinds of simple jobs in our streets in the first place. The answer is complex and may be embarrassing to discuss in this article. One thing however; unlike the Kenyans and the Ethiopians who own banks, hotels and motels, some retail shops, restaurants and bars, this writer has not seen any white collar Ugandan.
Moreover, it means that the people of South Sudan have the whole world to learn from and practically everything to learn, from providing food to building a house down to nail clipping. Until we learn, Ugandans shall continue to come. All is well so far, but the negative aspect of all this is that this small national income shall continue to be drained and sent abroad. This means the Republic of South Sudan shall continue to be poor.

Original_Search_Title: Thinking Outside the Medical Box
Headline: Rwanda: Thinking Outside the Medical Box
Body_Text:
Our health sector has more challenges than I need to spell out- one of these being the myriad of tribulations that come with importing most, if not all, of our medical equipment.
There is the lab technician who cannot read the manual written in German, the power cut that makes it impossible to provide certain services, the flickering lights in the surgery ward...you get where I am going with this.
The technological solutions to our health problems are great but not designed- to a large extent-to fit our infrastructure and adapt to some of our circumstances.
This article is a simple feature of a few innovations that are designed to provide low-cost medical technology that can be manufactured locally and are energy efficient.
Do-it-yourself ECG pads
Why worry about the supply of the pads and conductive gel (used by ECG machines to monitor heart rate) when you can make them yourself using bottle caps, salt and flour?
mPedigree (my favorite!)
A fake drug spotting system, manufacturers tag their drugs with a code that consumers can enter into their phones, send via SMS for free and receive a 'yes' or 'no' in response as an indication of trustworthiness of the drug.
Solar-powered Sterilizer
Medical instruments used in medical procedures can be sterilized with this reflective TV-satellite dish that has a modified wine bottle filled with water suspended above it: the sunlight reflected off the dish is focused on the bottle, and the steam from the boiling water forced into a pressure cooker with the medical instruments. The sterilizer has been proven to reach
The moral of this story is that there are ingenious ways for us to solve some of the small technological obstacles
recommended heat and pressure levels.
The leveraged-chair design
Wheelchairs with gear-shifting capabilities come at quite the penny.This wheelchair is designed for the rough bumpy roads in rural areas.
Re:Motion's cheap durable prosthesis
This prosthesis for above-the-knee leg amputeesis designed to be worn even in muddy fields and dusty roads: it consists of five plastic pieces and four fasteners that are easy to produce and assemble.
Machine to treat Jaundice
Jaundice can be cured with blue light: the equipment required to do this is pricey in terms of power and the machines themselves. An energy efficient phototherapy device (that goes for $350) has been developed that uses cheap LEDs that last ten times longer than bulbs used in traditional machines.
Sanitary pad maker
Many women in rural areas cannot afford to invest in sanitary products every month (doesn't help that rugs and bark are not very hygienic at times). The MIT D-lab is currently testing a machine that makes pads out of banana pulp.
The machine would be made from easy-to-find materials like wood and require minimal power. If turned into a small business, it could also generate income for local 'pad manufacturers' who provide hygienic pads to their communities.
The moral of this story is that there are ingenious ways for us to solve some of the small technological obstacles that come with being a developing nation - cheap solutions that don't involve heavy shipping costs and electricity bills.

Original_Search_Title: Govt Writes Off Shs3 Billion in Taxes
Headline: Uganda: Govt Writes Off Shs3 Billion in Taxes
Body_Text:
At least 23 companies, including those that have been receiving cash handouts from government, have once again benefitted from the tax waivers, criticised by MPs as illegal and unacceptable.
The tax waivers come at a time when the economy is facing a financial distress.
Already, the government is struggling to find Shs7 billion to help thousands of children stricken by the nodding disease which has claimed hundreds of lives in northern Uganda yet in the supplementary request to Parliament, Finance has not funded the health ministry.
URA shortfall
For the last three months, Uganda Revenue Authority has been "walking a tight rope" and as such, the tax body has registered a shortfall of more than Shs78 billion, junior Finance Minister Matia Kasaija told the House last week.
However, this has not stopped Finance Minister Maria Kiwanuka from writing off Shs3.7 billion in unpaid taxes.
"I have seen private hotels appearing several times on the list of the beneficiaries yet hotel exemptions were abolished after Chogm 2007. They want to smuggle these tax exemptions and we are not going to accept," shadow Finance Minister Geoffrey Ekanya said.
"Aya and others must pay all the taxes and those who paid Shs3.7 billion for them must pay back that money. These are illegal exemptions and the taxpayers have lost," he added.
While it is not clear whether the companies and organisations listed have failed to pay the taxes and how they were selected, Ms Kiwanuka in her statement to Parliament dated January 23, said: "Article 152(2) of the Constitution obliges me to report to Parliament periodically on the exercise of powers conferred upon me by any law to waive or vary a tax imposed by law."
The minister in a statement presented for her by the Minister for General Duties, Mr Fred Omach, said the amount of unpaid tax written off by the government was as high as 3.7b.
However, the statement was criticised for falling short of giving a true and fair view of the country's financial position while other lawmakers led by Mr Ekanya accused the government of smuggling the statement.
Financial experts have accused the government of hiding behind tax waivers to capitalise needy entities at the expense of the taxpayer.
"We need to establish the people behind these companies and organizations benefitting from tax waivers," former shadow Finance Minister Oduman Okello said.
Scheme Beneficiaries
Among the companies listed are: Aya Investments Ltd, which appears 19 times on the list, Phenix Logistics (U) Ltd, Southern Range Nyanza Ltd, Lydia Home Textiles (U) Ltd, Oil Palm Uganda Ltd and Victoria Resort Hotel.
Some of the organisations on the list are Africa Development Foundation, All Nation Christian Care, Uganda Episcopal Conference, Arua Diocese, Diocese of Masaka, Uganda National Council for Science and Presidential Initiative on Banana.

Original_Search_Title: Memory Tape - Former Headmaster Would've Replaced Doctors With Witches
Headline: Tanzania: Memory Tape - Former Headmaster Would've Replaced Doctors With Witches
Body_Text:
The devil is as much my arch-enemy as a rat is to a cat. It has an unlicensed hobby of causing me grief without my permission, but from which it derives as much joy as a Simba fan does when 'his' team defeats Yanga; and vice-versa.
For several years, the devil had cheated innocent insects called mosquitoes that I was a blood bank. The criminal tiny creatures grabbed the chance by siphoning some of my blood, and spitting a bit of deadly poison into the veins; a replica, somehow, of someone depositing and withdrawing money in, and from one's account.
The mosquitoes literally donated to me, free of charge, a disease called malaria, which gives the victim a tiny taste of what life in hell is like.
Modern medicine failed to cure me, but the disease, and its inventor, the devil, were defeated by a herbal drug invented by my ancestors in the land of people who love, like, worship and idolize bananas, and 'lubisi', the brew from whose juice it is derived.
Strangely, the people there believe that malaria is caused by fierce sunshine - hence the name 'mshana', which, not so strangely, is also the name of people somewhere close to Kenya, who, I am reliably informed, specialize in being experts at not spending money carelessly.
The devil was defeated, but, like a boxer who sufferers a knock-out but survives to fight another day, he resurfaced with gigantic vengeance, by injecting, into my stomach, funny noises that conspired to compose a song that sounded like two middle-aged male frogs quarrelling over a beautiful girl. The stomach usually 'sang' the 'song' in public places!
Several modern drugs failed to expel the 'song', which was accompanied by painful aches. My savior was a traditional 'doctor' at Shimo la Matope in Dar, who originates from northern Tanzania, and doesn't believe in wearing a pair of trousers and an underwear.
The doctor, Alekisanda ole Dumisamila, gave me a cup of a concoction that tasted like juice derived from boiled and cooled cow dung, which could cause a weak-willed elephant to weep.
Thenceforth, I have never suffered an ailment that has prompted me to seek modern medical treatment. I thus didn't directly feel the impact of the recent doctors' strike, which, however, reminded me of a class boycott which I masterminded in secondary school in the early 70s.
Our headmaster, nick-named Fyeka-fyeka, caned the entire class, over suspicions that we were the source of a rumour that his wife had kicked him 'where it mattered most', during a mid-night quarrel.
We boycotted class in protest, because he didn't produce tangible evidence to support the suspicions. His response was historic: he sweet-talked villagers in the neighbourhood into lending him 45 goats for two days, at a handsome fee.
The goats were herded into our class, where he pretended to teach them, and loudly remarking that, they were more disciplined and receptive to teaching than we, the human students, who he described as a bunch of good-for-nothing fools.
Word got to the regional education officer, who reprimanded the headmaster, and subsequently transferred him. I guess that, if Mwalimu Fyeka-fyeka had been a senior health ministry executive, he would have dealt with the crisis cynically, by mobilising witches and wizards to replace striking doctors in hospitals - with disastrous consequences!
Mr Kaigarula is the revise editor, The Citizen

Original_Search_Title: Day in the Life - I've Made It a Habit to Do Up to 20 Press-U.S First Thing in the Morning
Headline: Kenya: Day in the Life - I've Made It a Habit to Do Up to 20 Press-U.S First Thing in the Morning
Body_Text:
Idd Aziz is a Kenyan Norway-based Afrofusion artiste. He is best know for songs like Makelele featuring STL. Idd is a father of three and a family man. He was recently in Kenya to establish his music career locally.
I wake up at 6am. I have made it a habit to do up to 20 press-ups before getting ready and helping my wife with the children.This involves dressing them up, preparing their breakfast and packing their lunch packs. I then take them to school on my way to work. I work as a music and dance teacher at a music school and teach up to 600 children with their parents in one year.
I guess it's because I have been there for a while so people have confidence in what I do, hence the attendance keeps going up. My classes go up to around 10am before I take a break and have a snack; probably bread and porridge or any small biting in my back pack.I then go back to class and teach up to 2pm. I then have a banana or apple depending on what I have in my back pack.
I then hit the gym and do some training upto 5pm before I pick my children from school. On getting home, we have dinner at 6pm and I later play with them as we watch TV upto around 8pm when I take them to bed. I then practise with my band upto around 10pm and read a newspaper before going to bed between 11pm to midnight. My weekends are also quite busy depending on my scedule. Sometimes I have a concert either locally or in other cities. If I'm not attending these concerts, I spend time with my children. I also spend some time writing music.

Original_Search_Title: The African Renaissance in Art
Headline: Kenya: The African Renaissance in Art
Body_Text:
The spirit of the African Renaissance is alive and well in Nairobi where visual arts are thriving and on display all over town.
Everywhere from Nairobi National Museum to Village Market to Paa ya Paa Art Centre, exhibition halls are full to overflowing with contemporary Kenyan art.
And from the Alliance Francaise and Goethe Institute in the city centre to Le Rustique and Osteria Restaurants to OneOff and Banana Hill Art Galleries, more intimate exhibitions of either solo or duo sets of artists' work are up for viewing.
Nairobi National Museum under the direction of its curator, Lydia Galavu, has been transformed into one of the most vibrant venues for the visual arts.
Currently, two exhibitions are notable - one untitled and teaming with Kenyan art specially selected by patrons prepared to take the works abroad in a few weeks' time; the other of elegant black and white woodcut and lithographic prints prepared by modernist Mexican artists, a joint project between the Mexican embassy and the Museum.
The other remarkable assemblage of contemporary Kenyan art specifically by Nairobi-based artists has been organised by the GoDown Art Centre to be viewed through next week at the Village Market.
Named Manjano in 2010 when the first Nairobi Province (now County) Annual Visual Art Exhibition was organised as a joint venture by the ministry of Culture and the GoDown, this third Manjano show has been "incentivised" according to the Art Centre MD Joy Mboya who's managed to attract donor support two years running to provide attractive cash prizes for adjudicated art.
Last year it was a clear-cut case for judges Liza MacKay of ISK and Carol Lees of OneOff Gallery: Samuel Githui won the Sh300,000 first prize for his art in the professionals category while Alex Mbevo received the Sh50,000 first prize in the student category.
This year, it wasn't quite so easy for the judges to pick prize-winning works, according to Maggie Otieno of the Arterial Network and Sandeep Desai, an avid East African art collector.
"Manjano has the potential to become the most important art competition in Kenya, but the artists will have to take the competition more seriously in future before it can live up to its potential," remarked Desai who also adjudicated with Fiona Fox, formerly of the Tate Modern in London.
Perhaps it was because the GoDown gave Nairobi artists too short a notice of the submission deadline this year that caused fewer art networks to be represented this time round.
In any case from the almost 200 artworks submitted by both students and so-called professional artists this year, almost half were rejected by the judges.
Among those now on show at the Village Market since last Thursday, the winning works are tied in my mind to several outstanding pieces that didn't win a cash prize.
First prize of Sh300,000 went to Omosh Kindeh for his diptych Dystopia, a pair of paintings offering a stark reminder of how congested Nairobi has become.
The second prize of Sh150,000 went to Michael Soi for one of his notoriously naughty Ladies of the Night series.
Reflecting the challenge the judges must have had, two artists tied for third place: Dennis Muraguri for his picturesque woodcut print of life in Nairobi and Paul Inditi for his stunning semi-abstract painting.
(Both winners are currently in exhibitions: Muraguri's Matatu Art at Le Rustique until February 22 and Inditi's with Gor Soudan at Alliance Francaise in a striking display of black, white and grey paintings through this weekend.)
If I had been a judge, I would have also been torn between artworks by Samuel Githui and Patrick Mukabi among others whom I feel merited at least an honourable mention.
Other art centres exhibiting regional and local artists include Banana Hill Art Gallery where the Tanzanian painter Thobias Minzi's one-man show dubbed Prospects of African Life opened on Saturday through March 2, meanwhile at OneOff Gallery, Beatrice Njoroge's exhibition of new art is up through the 22nd.
Restaurants have also been mounting exhibitions more consistently.
At Osteria in Karen, Patrick Kinuthia's multi-coloured semi-impressionist portraits of regional beauties is on show through February; while Le Rustique regularly mounts contemporary Kenyan art exhibitions.
So if anyone's in doubt that an African Renaissance is under way in Kenya, just check out the Nairobi art scene for yourself.

Original_Search_Title: Unity Govt Dysfunctional
Headline: Zimbabwe: Unity Govt Dysfunctional
Body_Text:
With the Government of National Unity (GNU) appearing to sing its swan song, little celebration is expected to mark its three year-old anniversary this week.
ZANU-PF and the two formations of the Movement for Democratic Change (MDC) forged the GNU mid February, 2009 but since then it has been a roller-coaster as the three partners in the coalition government continue squabbling and haggling over the implementation of the Global Political Agreement (GPA).
The hand-holding spectacle among the principals in the GNU; President Robert Mugabe, Prime Minister Morgan Tsvangirai and Deputy Prime Minister Arthur Mutambara, with all the accompanying glitz, glamour and the hope of the "winds of change" blowing through Zimbabwe on the day the GPA was consummated seems to have now faded away.
In its place are growing suspicions and political drudgery in the corridors of power.
Even the early support that the GNU seemed to have attracted from the Southern African Development Community (SADC) - the guarantors of the GNU along with the Africa Union - has evaporated.
In March last year in Livingstone, Zambia, SADC set the tone by its sharp rebuke of President Mugabe over the slow pace of implementing political reform.
The writing was always on the wall.
Underpinning the perception that the GNU was doomed to fail from the onset is the many names that the failing union has been called by in the past three years, almost reminiscent of the Biblical coat of many colours - "a marriage of inconvenience" and a "government of no unity".
All these nick-names have been used to highlight the characteristics of the union that brought together two former arch-rivals, President Mugabe and Prime Minister Tsvangirai.
Lovemore Madhuku, a constitutional law expert said; "The reality is that power resides with the President, but the other players in the inclusive government will always want to pretend that they also have some power, some responsibility, and this is what keeps creating this circus".
Critics of ZANU-PF are adamant the GNU has been most affected by President Mugabe's alle-ged string of unilateral appointments.
These include; the unilateral appointment of governors and ambassadors in 2010.
But now the latest troubles affecting the GNU have come from the re-appointment of the country's security chiefs by President Mugabe, allegedly without consulting to his partners in the unity government.
Rightly or wrongly, supporters of President Mugabe argue that the veteran ZANU-PF politician does not need to consult the Prime Minister to make re-appointments or any appointment for the matter due to his executive presidential powers.
Political analyst, Charles Mangongera, said because of unilateralism the GNU had dismally failed to create a conducive environment for free political activity by Zimbabwean citizens.
"This has largely been as a result of ZANU-PF's recalcitrance as its hardliners have stifled reform at every level of the governance framework. Right now, there is an administrative logjam as the parties to the GNU are pulling in different directions resulting in paralysis of government functions", said Mangongera.
The premier and his deputy, Mutambara, last week tried to save face over the disputed re-appointment of police Commissioner-General Augustine Chihuri, alleging that the principals in the GNU had agreed that he be installed in an "acting capacity" only.
The announcement was promptly rejected by the President's Office, which insisted that all the service chiefs had been fully re-appointed by President Mugabe.
ZANU-PF politburo member, Jonathan Moyo, criticised the two leader's joint press briefing at Tsvangirai's Strathaven home.
"How can you go to Tsvangirai's house to speak about government issues and expect to be taken seriously? A house does not become a government place just because you have put a coat of arms behind you. Even in a banana republic politicians do not behave like that", said Moyo.
But given the long-list of squabbles that the unity government has weathered until now, it is likely that the latest bickering over the service chiefs will also blow over.
Instead, observers are looking to the personal crises and dilemmas that affect each of the principals in the GNU rather than the collective problem, as the strongest signal that will give tell-tale signs regarding the future of the coalition government.
Backed by his ZANU-PF party, President Mugabe has called for elections to "finish off" the GNU, but persistent speculation is that the call may be prompted more by concerns around his personal affairs and ZANU-PF's succession race - rather than a desire to end a unity government which has given him a lease on power.
Mangongera highlighted that President Mugabe was also a "beneficiary" of the stability brought aboutby the GNU.
"I think that over the years that it has been in existence, the GNU has managed to bring some modicum of economic stability that has brought relief to too many Zimbabweans following years of hyperinflation. The decriminalisation of the use of foreign exchange, which had been going on for over a decade, restored confidence in financial transactions and this averted crippling shortages of basic commodities . . . ," said Mangongera.
Prime Minister Tsvangirai, for long touted as the strongest opposition candidate to stand up to President Mugabe, has seen several chinks weakening his political armour - denting his squeaky clean image in the court of public opinion.
The premier's gay rights gaffe, controversial 12-day marriage to Locardia Karimatsenga last year and the perception that he is given marching orders by his "kitchen cabinet", have placed his leadership skills under close scrutiny.
On the other hand, the political future of Mutambara is a fore-gone conclusion, with agreement within political circles that the robotics professor had has time in the GNU - and would be rendered "irrelevant" to the country's future, once the coalition government gives way to an elected government.

Original_Search_Title: NERC Begins Hearing On Illegal Electricity Trading
Headline: Nigeria: NERC Begins Hearing On Illegal Electricity Trading
Body_Text:
Abuja — A panel of inquiry constituted by the Nigerian Electricity Regulatory Commission (NERC) mandated to look into an alleged case of illegal trade in electric power by the property managers of Ocean Parade Towers in Ikoyi, Lagos State, yesterday began sitting in Abuja.
The panel which comprises members including the Chairman of NERC, Dr. Sam Amadi, its Commissioner (Legal, Licensing and Enforcement), Dr. Steven Andzenge, and Commissioner (Market Competition and Rates), Mr. Eyo Ekpo, is seeking to determine any existence of fraud or illegal practices in the procurement and sale of electricity to residents of the estate by its managers as alleged by the residents.
According to a statement from the Commission's Assistant General Manager, Media, Mrs. Maryam Abubakar, the residents' association of the estate which is referred to as the petitioner had, through its counsel, Mr. David Eguma, indicted the property managers, Central Realties, H.P.I. Africa Limited and Banana Island Property Owners, who are the first, second and third respondents of trading in electricity without licence from the regulatory authority.
They had in this regard approached NERC, seeking amongst others, a refund of the monies collected from its members for the sale of electricity by the respondents from 2008 till date.
The statement also explained that counsel to the third respondent, Banana Island Property Owners and Residents Association, Mr. Victor Obaro, in a preliminary objection on behalf of its client, stated that the petition was an abuse of judicial process considering the existence of a subsisting ruling of a Lagos High Court as well as a pending appeal on the subject.
Obaro in this regard requested to be given more time by the panel to file an amendment to his application, a request the panel granted and adjourned further hearing on the matter for March 2, 2012 when the preliminary objection will be heard.
Ocean Parade Towers is located on Banana Island, Ikoyi, Lagos State, while HPI provides infrastructural services for Banana Island Property Owners and Residents Association.

Original_Search_Title: He Brings Me So Much Joy
Headline: Kenya: He Brings Me So Much Joy
Body_Text:
I remember the morning of June 30, 1992 as if it were yesterday. I was only four, and was on my way to school.
I was strapped in the back of the car; dad was driving, while mom, who was expectant, was seated beside him. Normally, it was mom who would drop me off to school, but she was on her way to hospital.
She had told me that I was soon going to have a baby brother or sister, news that really excited me.
That evening, mom didn't come home. Dad told me that I had a baby brother called Ian. I had been eagerly waiting to see him, and couldn't understand why he and mom were still in hospital.
After two weeks of pent up curiosity, I finally convinced dad to take me see my little brother. I found mom lying in bed, and after a little chit chat, she took me to see Ian.
He was in a nursery. Unlike the other babies in the room however, Ian had numerous tubes criscrossing his body, some going into his nose and mouth.
Mom explained that he was sick, and that the tubes were for food and oxygen. But what I remember most from that visit was his chubby, face. He looked so lovely; I couldn't wait for him to come home.
Two months later, mom and Ian finally returned from hospital. I was so happy, my heart could have burst then. I adored my little brother, and would spent every free moment I had with him.
I could hardly wait for him to start crawling and walking, so that we could play together. One year, two years, three years went by, but still, Ian could neither walk nor talk.
Perturbed, I asked mom why he wasn't like other children his age. That is when I learnt that he had cerebral palsy, evn though at that stage, I didn't really understand what that meant. Later on, I would learn that his had been a breech birth. He was delivered feet first.
He was also a big baby - weighing 4kgs, and should have been delivered through a caesarean section, but that wasn't the case. Ian developed other complications during birth, such as a blood clot in the brain, a factor that probably led to his condition.
Ian needed round the clock care and attention, and I would sometimes get forgotten, even though my parents tried their best to give us equal attention.
With time, the strain of caring for Ian begun to strain mom and dad's relationship. Both of them worried too much about Ian. However, they were determined to hold onto their marriage.
During school holiday in May 2003, mom travelled to Kampala to visit me at school. I was in form one at Kabojja International School. However, during the visit, her high blood pressure shot up, and she was admitted in hospital.
Unfortunately, she went into a comma. She didn't wake up. Mom passed away on May 28, 2003, after a nurse who was attending to her accidentally pricked her lungs while inserting an oxygen tube.
Her death was as shocking as it was painful, but I had dad, and he made it easier to bear the pain. However, a short while after mom's death, dad fell into depression. He started to drink and smoke too much, and rarely ate.
On September 23, that same year, dad suffered a heart attract and died. In just three months, I lost both my parents. I was 15, while Ian was 11.
My world fell apart. So many questions raged within me.
Why had God taken away the two people I loved most? Who would educate me? Who would take care of me and Ian? I struggled with varied emotions, but the person I worried about most was Ian. I felt I could take care of myself, but Ian couldn't.
Amidst my mourning, for the sake of my brother, I decided to face life with courage because he needed me.
After we buried my father, our extended family had to decide who we would live with. Mom was Ugandan, dad Kenyan. Mom had been very close to her sisters, and so it was decided that Aunt Joan, mom's older sister, would take care of us.
Aunt Joan lives in Kampala and has a son. Just like that she became a mother of three. I want to thank her because she has been a good mother to me and Ian, without her, and goodwill from other family members, Ian and I would probably not have a bright future ahead of us.
Moving on...
Gradually, I learned to accept the loss of my parents, and focused on the future.
After completing my secondary education, I came to Kenya in 2006 to do my undergraduate. It was a rewarding experience. It gave me a chance to meet new people; it stimulated my faith, my thinking and my imagination, and I vowed to work hard in school so that I could help my aunt take care of Ian.
He is 19 now. He can't walk, and although he hears, he can't talk, even though he's got his own special language. He communicates through gestures, and other non-verbal skills. He has no bowel control, and therefore uses diapers.
He also has to be fed. He's like a child, in almost every way.
But he is very intelligent. He can identify his favourite artists on TV, and his eyes stayed glued on television when his favourite football team, Manchester United is playing. Amazingly, he understands and responds to English, Kiswahili, French, and bits of Luhya and Luganda.
Since his childhood, we've been communicating with him using these languages. My paternal family speaks to him in Luyha, my mother's relatives in Luganda, while I speak to him in English and Kiswahili.
Our cousins communicate with him in French. Ian can identify colours too. When I'm dressing him, I always ask him what he wants to wear. Just like you and me, he knows what he wants.
I've never been embarrassed about my brother. I introduce him to my friends, and talk about him a lot.
Some people ask why we haven't considered placing him in an institution, but this is something we would never even dream of doing. Ian is part our lives and he belongs with us.
Yes, taking care of him is very involving, and we've had to employ a caregiver, but he isn't a burden; he brings us so much joy. He is a jolly young man, always smiling. When I'm too tired or upset, a smile from him is all it takes to drive away the frustration.
We have integrated him in our daily routine. We take him everywhere we go - shopping, on holiday, to weddings, funerals - everywhere we go, he goes.
A basic day with Ian follows this pattern. He normally wakes up at 9am, and takes a bath. For breakfast, he prefers tea, juice and a banana. Now that he's a teenager, he's got a ravenous appetite, so lunch and supper are more elaborate.
He then watches TV, and later goes to the garden to watch birds. We regularly check on him to see if he needs a diaper change. When I'm looking after him, all I do is ask, "Is it time for your diaper change?" "Mmhh", means he needs one, but moving his head from side to side means that he doesn't.
I graduated in 2010 from Daystar University with a degree in Public Relations and Electronic Media. Thereafter, I joined Family Media as a health journalist, but quit last year to found my own company, Loshy Limited based in Kilimani, Nairobi.
Loshy is the pet name that mom gave me. Like most children, I daydreamed about what I wanted to do when I grew up. I considered the possibility of becoming a teacher or a pediatrician.
All my daydreams boiled down to working with children, perharps because of my brother. That is why I decided to found my own company, which focuses on childcare, early childhood education, and lays emphasis on a child's social, emotional, physical and intellectual development.
My goal is to support children's desire to be life-long learners. I am also in the process of developing a magazine for children between two and eight years.
It will be a learning tool that allows children to explore their talent on their own. It will have lots of colour, and various fun activities to try out.
I also have an upcoming children clothesline called Dune and Duet. We will create chic and trendy clothes for children from zero to 12 years. We launch in October this year.
Besides running my company, I'm a part time ballet instructor. I studied ballet at Kampala Ballet School while growing up. Speaking from experience, expressive dancing and creative movement is a good avenue for for flushing out stress.
Like James Brown says, "There is no problem that cannot be solved by dancing".
Since Ian lives with aunt Joan in Uganda, I don't get to see him as frequently as I'd want, but I call him regularly, and every three months, I travel home to see him. I help my aunt to pay the house help, buy food, diapers and medicine for Ian.
He has to take medicine daily to help to relax his muscles, and also to prevent seizures. On average, we spend Sh75, 000 a month on his needs.
Ian is very attached to me; he gets distressed whenever I leave Kampala for Nairobi. Only the promise of chocolate calms him down. Eventually, I want to live with him permanently.
For many young women like me, life is simply about going to college, getting a good job, and then getting married. It is more than that for me.
Every plan and decision I make revolves around Ian. For instance, the man I date, and eventually get married to will have to accommodate and accept Ian, because he will always be a part of my life.
Growing up with, and taking care of my brother has made me a better person, and taught me to appreciate every blessing in my life. Beacuse of him, I am extremely patient.
I've also developed a tough skin, and no situation in the world scares me. Ian has also taught me that for every challenge in life, there's always a way out, a solution.
Most people might say that Ian isn't normal. I disagree. I've learnt to see life through his eyes, and to him, there's nothing wrong with him. In fact, everything is as it should be.

Original_Search_Title: Saitoti - the Dark Horse
Headline: Kenya: Saitoti - the Dark Horse
Body_Text:
On the national platform, Prof George Saitoti comes across as a self-deprecating if not an irredeemably hesitant politician in regards to pursing the ends of raw politics: ultimate power.
Formerly an egghead at the University of Nairobi before the consummate network of former President Daniel arap Moi, a prescient political operative enticed him to politics in 1983 - as a nominated MP - Prof Saitoti's persona is singularly burdened by an unsavoury past that had him cornered and arguably reduced to an amenable marionette. "As a contender for the presidency, Prof Saitoti, 69, faces a Herculean task of exonerating himself from his past, particularly in regards to what is famously referred to as the Goldenberg scandal that saw Kenya lose billions of shillings in fake gold exports.
And despite the legal courts clearing him of the scandal in July 2006, in the public court, the jury is still out. In addition, his role during the clamour for plural democracy will be brought to bear for he was considered hawkish in his defence of a monolithic political arrangement. It will be recalled that it was immediately after his utterances in Parliament where he ridiculed pluralists, that Kenneth Matiba and Charles Rubia were picked up by Special Branch police to suffer incarceration that led to the deterioration of the health of these two leading lights of democracy," says Dr. Richard Bosire, senior political science and administration lecturer at UON.
By some estimates, the Goldenberg scam cost the equivalent of not less than 10 percent of the country's GDP. Weighing in, Prof Karuti Kanyinga of the Institute of Development Studies says Saitoti is saddled with political baggage emanating from his incestuous relationship with Kanu that may turn out to be the banana skin he slips on and falls. "He (Saitoti) is not of his own making. He was brought up by Kanu. He lived to practise politics during the peak of repression by the Kanu regime. He has never associated himself with political reforms and therefore will find it difficult speaking about 'change'. His Kanu past is his main reference point whereas some of those competing against him will be talking about multiparty reforms. Also I believe that he so fearful of this past," he says.
Human Rights Watch, an international organisation whose brief is partly to monitor human rights violations, accused Saitoti at the time of involvement in what was famously referred to as tribal clashes that rocked parts of the Rift Valley beginning in 1990 to the run up to elections scheduled in 1992. Understandably, Saitoti has dismissed the report.
But Internal Security minister may posses some saving grace regarding his past relation with Kanu if Prof Godfrey Muruiki, a senior history lecturer at UON's assessment is to pass muster. "Who was not in Kanu amongst those presently contesting for the presidency? Apart from one or two aspirants, the rest of the crowd were all key members of Kanu. And don't forget that your current President (Mwai Kibaki) was once a stalwart of the same Kanu regime. So if it's really about been found guilty by association, who can dare to throw the first stone? There are a lot of presidential candidates masquerading within that circle of aspirants as being quintessence of Mr Perfect. But at the end, it's up to the electorate to make their choice," says Prof Muruiki.
And according to Mwandawiro Mgangha, a former legislator, Prof Saitoti is the best candidate within the PNU arrangement because the guy "is very sober and if anybody is to bring out the issue of scams, almost all the other hopefuls have been involved in one or two scandals as well."
It's apparent that despite possessing gold-gilded curriculum vitae from the Ivory Towers, Prof Saitoti was not insulated fromacting obsequiously loyal while under the heel of Kanu. For his loyalty, he was paid with ridicule and atrocious pain but still remained devoted to political machinery that manifestly never had his back.
"President Moi ridiculed him intermittently in public forums but the guy (Saitoti) somewhat acted like he was eternally beholden to the Kenyan Leader. I remember one time when he was shuffled back to the position of Vice President and President Moi brazenly said publicly something to the extent that "I've given back Prof Saitoti the seat of Vice-President, hopefully now your sufurias (pots) will be full of food", an example of how titular his influence, including power base was at the time," says Dr Bosire.
A disclosure: Under Moi hardly anybody had a mind of their own. For Kanu operated like the private business of the former Kenyan leader. Individuals who dared to cross swords with former President Moi, and lived to tell the tale are far and in between. Back in the days, those who aligned their fortunes with the now 44-year old party were more inclined to making Faustian bargains rather than seeking the alternative, martyrdom
And as he stares to the unfolding future, his political career squats on a precarious cusp that may very well herald his oblivion from the big stage, unless he navigates the Herculean task of bagging the presidency. It turns out that his Kajiado North constituents have magnanimously and unequivocally told him to his face that he has no other choice but to gun for the Presidency for he is certain to receive nothing else from them, this time round.
Worth noting is the fact that in successive elections beginning in 1992 when the country first held competitive party elections after a 10-year hiatus running to 2002, the constituency has overwhelmingly voted for opposition presidential candidates, not Moi the Kanu candidate as was the norm in the larger Rift Valley province at the time. Tellingly on all those occasions, Prof Saitoti, then a top drawer in Moi's derided Kanu party,seemingly defied gloom predications to win one successive election after another. But as is expected, divergent opinion exists regarding Saitoti's outing as a constituency leader.
According to councillor Mwathi Marimpet Pere of Ongata Rongai ward, Prof Saitoti has what it takes to run a country. The civic leader says that despite the quiet demeanor of their MP, his work at the constituency level is unparalleled and worthy of praise. "Prof Saitoti is a peace maker. He avoids divisive politics. When he talks of building a country that is cosmopolitan, he means it. Look at his constituency for example. Allthe Kenyan communities reside here.
When ethnic clashes rocked the country recently, after elections were held in 2007, migrants running away from areas that had been hit by ethnic violence chose Kajiado North as their destiny of choice. That tells you much about the man himself. And even in the 1990's when tribal clashes rocked large swathes of the Rift Valley province in what was then referred to as tribal clashes, Kajiado North remained very peaceful," he says.
"Besides, during his tenure the road network has grown tenfold. When he took over the reigns of leadership here, there was only one national secondary school, namely Oloolaiser Secondary school which was put upin 1969. Now we have several government secondary schools and in some locations almost two primary schools. And if you were to travel to places like Shompole, which is a very nomadic enclave, Prof Saitoti has built schools in the region, dug boreholes and opened up the area for development. He is a doer not a talker."
On the opposite end is Njenga wa Rungee, the councillor of Ngong Town ward who says Prof Saitoti only thinks about himself and not about the residents of the constituency. He points out, for example, that despite the relatively short distance between Ngong and the capital, Nairobi, residents of this cosmopolitan joint continually face crashing endeavours in search of tap-water as residents rely heavily on donkeys to ferry jerry cans of water to their door fronts.
"The biggest liability to Saitoti is the local people whom he surrounds himself with. They don't tell him the truth. They have let him down because they only serve their personal interests and not the locals. See how some of the past administrators have enriched themselves over time. And he relies immensely on his relatives to run affairs here. Look at the chiefs, almost all are related to him by blood. It's this form of open patronisation that has seen him lose support on the ground with every passing day. And the only reason he wins elections is because he is seen as a government candidate. But what Kajiado North constituency has sought all along for is a leader, not a ruler. But all said and done, Prof Saitoti is a very experienced administrator and in terms of executive experience, save for President Kibaki, nobody else can hold a candle to him."
To date the constituency, established in 1966 has had only four legislators since independence - GK ole Kipury, John Keen, PhilipOdupoy and Saitoti- the latter serving the longest period.
While Uhuru, one of his erst while competitors is widely thought to be the presumptive heir of President Kibaki in the polls scheduled not later that March 2013, Prof Saitoti meantime comes through as the heir apparent to a leader unflatteringly described as " frail and diffident" by the Economist magazine, a weekly platform for highbrow types. In the event Uhuru's run for the presidency is wrong-footed on account of confirmed charges by the International Criminal Court (ICC), it remains a toss-up affair whether the Chairman of PNU would benefit majorly from the expected spin-off.
Routinely, ethnic faultlines rise above the surface in the run-up to a general election. This internecine phenomenon has on the extreme, black-eyed East Africa's biggest economy as the challenge of managing intra-ethnic strife in parts of the country always inundates its sensibilities. Sometimes pushing the country almost down the precipice like happened in early 2008.
Which makes a legitimate case that the country's political heart pounds to an ethnic beat, emboldening close political watchers too pine that were Uhuru to inadvertently give the presidential race a miss, Saitoti would naturally receive the backing of previous Uhuru backers. But Prof Saitoti may face a self-made hurdle. Since hinging his career to a political bandwagon, Saitoti has remained deafeningly mum regarding his Kikuyu ancestral roots instead opting to court the amorphous' cosmopolitan' ethnic tag. As he maneuvers to run for the presidency it's anyone's guess if the self-imposed sobriquet will act as an asset or a liability.
It's quite early to tell whether Prof Saitoti is a serious contender for the top seat, says Prof Muriuki, nevertheless, his candidature faces hard times ahead if the status quo remains, he says. "It will be very difficult for him to win because he will not have the numbers because if you look at Raila Odinga for instance, his ancestral Luo community is behind him, if you look at Kalonzo Musyoka, the Kamba communy's vote will most likely lean towards him and to some extent,were Musalia Mudavadi to run, largely his Luhyia ethnic group will cast votes for him. The same trend can be said of William Ruto and his Kalenjin community. Prof Saitoti lacks this kind of support. And it's a fact of Kenyan life that our politics is largely ethnically based."
According to Dr Bosire, "It is most likely that the Kikuyu elite who are currently fighting on Uhuru Kenyatta's corner will transfer their political loyalty to Prof Saitoti. Essentially this is in spite of Prof Saitoti, denial of his Kikuyu roots. What makes his candidature appealing amongst the Kikuyu elite, compared to the other Presidential candidates drawn from the Mt Kenya region is the fact that he has deep pockets. He can be relied upon to run a serious presidential campaign. And if he were to ascend to State House, he is likely to protect and further extend the elites interests. This cannot be said of say Vice President Kalonzo Musyoka for he lacks he necessary monetary pedigree that would endear him to the political elite drawn from within the larger Kikuyu community."
Pervasively, Saitoti is thought to be one of the wealthiest Kenyans today who answers to the enviable moniker of Mr New Money, though the source of his wealth, including its real worth, remains a fixation for speculative discussion, up till now. Justin Muturi, a confidant of Uhuru says, "Which sack is this that one is going to get ripe votes from? One must work hard to win the hearts and minds of the electorate countrywide. And in any event I'm not aware that Prof Saitoti has approached Uhuru in regards to discussing possible backing should Uhuru be red-carded from the race. There has been zero discussion. In any case, politics is very fluid and one cannot imagine that were Uhuru, for example not to run, that they would automatically be beneficiaries of the votes Uhuru was to receive.
My advice to Prof Saitoti and anybody else for that matter is to work diligently by talking to the people instead of paying lip service. They should forget completely what commentators are writing about. Politics is not like mathematics where you follow a particular formula. If Prof Saitoti is serious about his presidential bid, let him show his seriousness by transversing the length and breadth of this country. For now, what I know is that Uhuru's name will be on the ballot. Those thinking otherwise are day-dreaming."
According to Prof Munene, the Kajiado North MP has enough in his hands; leave alone the issue of addressing his ethnic extraction. "Representing a cosmopolitan constituency like Kajiado North, South of Nairobi, and being seen to be cosmopolitan is a plus. It shows an ability to rise above parochial considerations. The problem for Prof. Saitoti, however, is not in being or not being cosmopolitan. It is that his identity appears to be a political issue and he has not been able to address that." says Prof Munene. Adding, "His likely support bases are shaky. He is likely to be everyone's second choice and so when the first choices cancel each other, he might be left standing to become number one."
In the current Parliament, only President Kibaki has served uninterruptedly for a longer period than Saitoti. But the latter's lengthy tenurehood, if expected, has failed to translate into avote-grabbing machine, on a national scale. Indeed even when it boils down to the prosaic exercise of name recognition, Saitoti's brand conjures the image of a reluctant leader or simply a begrudging carpet bagger.
Perhaps it may have escaped the attention of the ubiquitous political commentators that Prof Saitoti's current position is immensely powerful. The provincial administration answers to him, including the formal internal security network. Besides Prime Minister Raila Odinga and the former Finance minister Uhuru, no other politician comes close to being defined as powerful and influential.
In addition, an occurrence in recent times left a lingering perception that Saitoti's political star maybe headed northwards. Setting precedent, President Kibaki contradicted his consort Lucy in public overmatters concerning Prof Saitoti in February 2009. A repeat has never been witnessed regarding another politician.
Coincidentally, the relationship and similarities between President Kibaki and his Minister for Internal Security and Provincial Administration run deep. Both count Mangu High School as their former High School. They both profess to the Catholic Faith. On the political front, both were dealt a bad hand by Moi, suffering humiliation as VPs.
To his credit, his admirers say that Prof Saitoti is no whiner, pointing out that while other MP shabitually complain about the size of their constituencies, Kajiado North, they say is geographically bigger than all the constituencies in Nairobi, combined. True? With hindsight, it's enticing to think of the proverbial statement: "If a man loves the labour of any trade apart from any question of success or fame, the gods have called him," said Robert Louis Stevenson, an American writer. Does Prof Saitoti fit the bill? Time will tell but for now, ignoring Prof Saitoti's candidature as the apparent presidential nominee of President Kibaki may look farfetched but it could easily happen. Whether he rides the roost of victory is another thing all together. Uhuru once walked on the same path.

Original_Search_Title: Making it a Valentine to Remember
Headline: Nigeria: Making it a Valentine to Remember
Body_Text:
This year's St.Valentine's Day celebration is around the corner and lovers all over the world no doubt are putting finishing touches to their preparation. CHALYA DUL and AMARACHI EGBEOGU in this report examines the level of preparation and expectations of some Nigerians towards making this year's celebration a Valentine to be remembered, despite the economic crunch facing the country.
The 14th day of February every year is regarded a special day for lovers all over the world as friends, families and lovers look forward to express love to their loved ones either by offering them gifts or taking them on outings, picnics and the likes. This day is known as the St. Valentine's Day.
Different people have different views on the celebration of Valentine's Day. While some people have never celebrated it for varying reasons attributed to religious and personal beliefs, others look forward to it as a special day to renew their vows and commitment to their loved ones.
Some people have had memorable Valentine's Day experience, while some are yet to. Generally, Valentine's Day is a day to show love, exchange gifts and organise special events for your loved ones.
It is only normal that many people would be planning this year's celebration with the hope of giving out gifts and expecting one in return.
Nina, a corps member serving in Abuja shares her plans for this special day: "I have been dating my boyfriend for about three years now and Valentine's Day celebration with him has always been a memorable one.
I hope this year's celebration will be better. Tony is the best thing that has ever happened to me. He has always been the one giving me treats, but this year I hope to give him a surprise and make it big! I intend to send a card and cake to his office; and after work have dinner with him at Banana Republic. Already, I have shopped for the best outfit for the event!"
Although a Muslim, Musa a civil servant in the FCT said she has been celebrating Valentine's Day ever since he got married to his wife who happens to be a Christian. "My religion does not recognise the celebration of St. Valentine's Day, but I have vowed to support my wife in whatever she practices and also makes her happy," Musa said.
"There is no crime in celebrating with your lover, especially on a day marked out for love. This year's valentine would be my wife's best because she has been a lovely and very supportive wife.
Despite our religious differences, she has always been there for me and the family. I intend getting her a brand new car to show my appreciation and also let her know she is the best," he concluded.
Ann, a single lady questioned the rule that mandates Valentine's Day celebration to be celebrated with a companion. "Who says that one must have a companion for this one day of the year? I intend taking a day off work; treat myself to a Spa or a long bubble bath, afterwards take a long drive. Some people get lucky on this day, but I intend spending it alone," she said.
"Sincerely, I celebrate Valentine's Day out of obligation rather than in the true sense of celebration. I only celebrate it because everyone around me does," says Daniel, a journalist based in Federal Capital City.
"In fact, my understanding of the celebration is to get a special gift for my girlfriend. I may spice it up with a little fun depending on my programme for the day because I am a very busy person," he said.
A lot of shows and events are already being lined up by different event promoters for the day. Most ladies supposedly will be rocking the day with a touch of red in their outfit as it is generally believed to be the colour the day represents.
Whatever your belief or understanding of the celebration, try to show love to someone in the spirit of the season, especially at this time the country is in dire need of love in the face of the present challenges it is going through. Let's make it a Valentine to remember.
On behalf of everyone at LEADERSHIP WEEKEND, have a Happy Valentine in advance.

Original_Search_Title: Manchester United, Arsenal to Turn Tide
Headline: Uganda: Manchester United, Arsenal to Turn Tide
Body_Text:
I have always placed the line that there are no easy games in the English Premiership in the 'lazy clichés' category, but I am obliged to move it to the 'truth be told' column this once; testament to the competitiveness of that league this season, each of the top seven teams has its work cut out at the weekend.
Man United and Tottenham have tough home fixtures, Arsenal visit inform opponents and Chelsea make one of the more dreaded trips in the league, leaving Manchester City with the easiest of the tops games, albeit only strictly in relative terms.
In lieu of that, here comes one for the punters intent of raiding the gaming houses and making a kill. Don't pay me if you hit the jackpot, but don't kill me if you don't either, so here goes.
Man United vs Liverpool
Luis Suarez is right when he says he will be buoyed by the boo boys, not deterred. Many will forget that Patrice Evra had had a very good game at Anfield in the FA Cup even as the abuse rained down from the Kop, until Dirk Kuyt got in behind him for the winner.
It is hard for goalkeepers and defenders to win in that situation because they are judged on the ones that get away, but being a striker Suarez can miss a host of chances including a penalty and still be the hero if he bangs in the equaliser or winner.
Because he was not injured, he has had a good rest and showed against Spurs on Monday that he is still the menace in the box that he was before the suspension.
Rather than preoccupy themselves with stopping the sneaky Uruguayan however, United would be better advised to concentrate on controlling the game as they did for an hour at Anfield. Instead of having a second winger (the Ashley Young ploy did not even work at Chelsea on Sunday), Alex Ferguson ought to draft in Tom Cleverly to join Michael Carrick and Paul Scholes.
With Valencia doing it right on the right, Rooney, Wellbeck and Cleverly can take turns drifting left but otherwise allow Evra freedom and space to get forward and get his revenge in a more telling way than if he was to stay back and help out with his nemesis.
Kenny Dalglish has to choose between retaining Andy Carroll and either losing the numbers battle in the middle or missing out on the pace of Craig Bellamy/industry of Kuyt. For Ferguson, the reminders that Wellbeck doesn't have to last entire games when Chicarito and Dimitar Berbatov are options have become too many to ignore.
Verdict: United win
Villa vs City
With destinations such as Old Trafford, White Hart Lane, Goodison Park, the Stadium Of Light for their rivals, Man City should ideally be happy that it is Villa Park that beckons, as the hosts have not won there for months now.
But wait a minute, this could turn out to be the most slippery of banana skins.
Villa's defence is creaking and the midfield is grossly over-matched, but there are other causes for hope. There are goals in Darren Bent, Robbie Keane and Gabriel Agbonlahor; Charles Nzogbia is finally hitting his stride; Shay Given will be inspired as will other former City players like Richard Dunne and the resurgent Stephen Ireland; more importantly will be the siege mentality created by press reports of a player-revolt and plans by the fans to demonstrate against the manager prior to kickoff. Remember Blackburn felling Arsenal and United under similar circumstances? Still though ...
Verdict: City win
Spurs vs Newcastle
The Magpies do not have the tactical nous and discipline to shackle Gareth Bale the way Liverpool did it on Monday, when Dalglish switched Glen Johnson to the left to allow for the more defensively solid Kelly to come in at right back and get help from Kuyt on the wing, Jay Spearing in the middle and Martin Skrtel at the back.
With home comforts and Bale getting more freedom, Spurs will be back in full flow and the defensive approach adopted by Joe Jordan and co at Anfield will go out the window.
However, on the pitch the two Dembas might not allow Harry Redknapp the perfect celebration for winning in court. The Senegalese 'twins' are bound to exert untold pressure on Ledley King's knees and Michael Dawson's back after the injury-prone pair put in a great shift to keep Carroll, Bellamy, Kuyt and Suarez at bay on Monday. In Johan Cabaye's absence and without natural width though, Ba and Cisse will need for Frenchmen Ben Arfa and Gabriel Obertan to be at their assisting best.
Verdict: Draw
Sunderland vs Arsenal
Sunderland are the form team of the Premiership, collecting points like they are going out of fashion. In typical Martin O'Neill style, they are playing work-till-you-drop football and are much stronger than the sum of their parts.
Stephane Sessegnon is in fine fettle, Frazier Campbell is plugging the hole left by first Asamaoh Gyan and then Nicklas Bendtner, Sebastian Larsson loves to play against Arsenal and the absence of superstars makes it difficult for tacticians to plot against them.
Still though, it will not be enough against Arsenal's midfield mastery and pace in the wide areas, which will all culminate in the most inevitable outcome of this season's Premiership - a Robin van Persie goal, perhaps a couple or even a treble.
Verdict: Arsenal win

Original_Search_Title: Dilemma of New Lunga District
Headline: Zambia: Dilemma of New Lunga District
Body_Text:
RECENTLY Zambia has had seven additional districts, bringing the number to 79 in the whole country.
Of the latest new districts is Lunga in Luapula Province. The Lunga District has been detached from Samfya District.
In reality, the new Lunga District in the whole of Luapula parliamentary constituency lies in the vast Bangweulu swamps east of Lake Bangweulu.
The Lunga District shares borders with four districts, Serenje in the south ,Chiluba in the north, Mpika east and Samfya in the west.
The Declaration of Lunga as a district by President Michael Sata is a great milestone in the history of the Lunga people who occupy this vast water logged area.
President Sata's creation of Lunga District has been a fulfilement of the campaign promises he made during the 2006 presidential and parliamentary election that the swampy Lunga area would have a district status once the PF party came into power. President Sata by then was in the opposition and was in Lunga area on a campaign trail.
Looking at the geographical and unique features of Lunga area , the President has printed a long lasting history in the minds of its inhabitants as it was unthinkable for this wetland to have full district status of its own.
The area of Lunga is made up of about 70 per cent water, vast marshes which are virtually isolated from one another .
The islands are Mutwamina , Kabulu , Mandwe , Matongo and Nsalushi in Chief Nsamba , Chafye , Mbo Yalubambe , Ncheta , Chibambo in Chief Bwalya Mponda)Isenga , Musumba , Mangomba and Bucinda under Senior Chief Kalimankonde and Lunga , Chibulu , Chisale in chief Kasoma -Lunga.
The estimated sizes of the islands range from 5 km long by 2km wide to 15 km long by 5 km wide.
The new district has come as a blessing in that people will see real development which is key but has lacked since the country got its independence 48 years ago.
Inspite having seen representation in parliament since 1964 Lunga area has remained severely under developed in all social and economic sectors.The area has the most severe and frightening poverty levels.
It is this state of affairs which made Chief Nsamba to state:" President Sata has brought joy , cheers and ululations among the people of Lunga District .President Sata's declaration of Lunga as a district simply means there will be meaningful development in the area to the delight of our people," he said.
Speaking to the author in Samfya recently,Chief Nsamba said "As chiefs we will do everything possbile to help Government achieve all that is required in setting up the district ," he said.
President Sata has ended our nomadic way of living because during the period of eight decades, Lunga area has been administered from four districts , Mpika , Luwingu , Fort Roseberry (Mansa) and Samfya .
Lunga area is reached by water routes from any point on the main lands in the north , south , east and west directions. Equally people who are on the inlands use waterways to reach or visit each other.
The modes of transport are dug out canoes and banana boats which are paddled .Due to long distances between the islands, bridges can be built between them. The average distance from one island to another is about 40 to 60 kilometres and the travelling period ranges from six to twelve hours.
A distance from Samfya to the furthest island in the areas of the new district can take up to two days.
This makes transport and communication the most single challenge authorities of this new district will have to face.
