Poor transport and communication has been seen as the main cause of high poverty levels in the area as they are hampered by the delivery of Government service to islands .Most of the water routes between the islands are not designated but only small routes (nceta as they are locally known ), exist for use by dug out canoes and small banana boats .
These modes of transport may not be ideal for the smooth operations of Government programmes.
However there are planned channels which connect chiefdoms namely , Senior chief Kalima Nkonde,Chief Kasoma -Lunga . Chief Bwalya Mponda and chief Nsamba.The area has also four sub chiefs ,Mweshi of Isenga island;Kambala of Chisalel;Ponga of Chafye and Milambo of Mangomba Island.
But now that the area has been afforded with full district status, the people will need better water ways and a good transport network as development plans will have to dictate for these.
Lunga District is probably going to be the only area in the country where motor transport will not be a necessity, even among Government institutions especially on the islands themselves, This is simply because there are no areas where motor vehicles can be used let alone boats-motored or paddled ones .
Individuals,including chiefs who own motor cars do not take them to their villages or places on the islands but keep ( them vehicles ) on the main land some 80 Km or more in some cases . In the same vein, Government will own both water and main land transport system.
Unlike during pre-independence days when the area was serviced by Bangweulu Water Transport (BWT), there has been no public water transport between Samfya and the four chiefs' areas since 1970s .
BWT a quasi government water transport company , linked the islanders with Samfya and Mpika when it provided service on the Samfya -Twingi , Ncheta -Nsalushi -Mbati route .
Mbati is on the eastary of the Chambeshi River in Mpika District. This opened up the Islands in the eastern swamps of Lake Bangweulu to those in the west .
These services faciliated the transportation of large quanties of fish to the Copperbelt through Samfya and Mansa.
Presently BWT operators on a short route to Samfya -Mbabala -Chishi -Chilubi using dilapidated and old fashioned barges .These services are complemented by water transport provided by Zampost which uses its relatively modern maritime vessel , the Post boat.
Since its launch as a passenger and mail carrier by first president Kenneth Kaunda in 1990, the post boat has never served in the Lunga due to poor navigation status of the channels in the swamps .
Now that the Lunga area is a district with its own budget for development,the islanders are of the view that Government will make everything possible to resolve the situation in as far as transport and communication facilities are concerned.
Chief Nsamba commended that lack of good water transport in Lunga area is undisputed .
The chief who is a member of the House of chiefs observed that this and many other factors have been largely responsible for poor Government services by the Samfya based departments to the islanders.
"The consequence of all this has been that the people felt abandoned and left to fend for themselves ," he said.
While the authorities of the new district will need to go on full throttle to carry out development in all the areas of the economy almost at once , opening up of water channels between the islands is of fundamental importance, especially if machine powered boats will be used by both Government and members of the public.
At the time of independence most of the main channels were in navigable conditions as they were constantly maintained.
The Government then employed hundreds of workers through the department of water affairs to clear , widen and deepen the canals. In some cases new channels were developed between Islands.
However few years after independence, maintenance of channels in Bangweulu swamps was relaxed and later abandoned altogether .
Owing to lack of maintenance for many years, channels disappeared and this later forced BWT to withdraw its cargo and passenger transport service to the swamps.
One good example of the channels which have disappeared is the famous route Kasoma-Lunga to Nsumbu and Chilubi islands .This channel was of significant importance for economic exchange between the people of Chilubi Islands and Lunga areas.
While billions of Kwacha have been spent on the development of numerous road networks in the districts on the main lands, no such amount has been spent on the development of water routes in the Bangweulu swamps.
But with the possible deployment of all necessary Government and their supporting staff to the new district , it is hoped that clearing of channels and canals between the islands will be revived.Once this is done, the islanders , whose livelihood depends mainly on fishing will then secure employment in services.
In any case, employing the islanders on the development and maintenace of canals will give them an opportunity to fill their pockets with money which they willl earn .
The area has no any kind of industry one could think of where people can secure jobs apart from employing themselves on catching fish.
Meanwhile,two months after President Sata gave Lunga area district status, clearing of channels has commenced using a newly acquired dredger.The initial works have started on the clearing of the main channel which connects Samfya District to the new Lunga District .
This was made known to the author by the Samfya District Commissioner Mr Godfrey Zimba.
Mr Zimba also disclosed that the process of setting up of the district has started by first engaging the area traditional rulers ,councillors and area Member of Parliament on the way forward.
He said the decision on where to locate the district headquarters will entirely come from the people themselves.He however mentioned that planning experts will guide the Government on this issue especially after a land audit has been done on the islands.
As earlier mentioned, the people of Lunga produce about 50 per cent of fish consumed on the Copperbelt .Because of this good production of fish, Samfya District , from which Lunga as been detached , earns substantial amount of revenue in form of levy charged on fish detained from routes on the Copperbelt.
But despite contributions the islanders make towards the development of Samfya, this development has not been enjoyed in Lunga area .
The authorities of the new Lunga District will therefore face numerous challenges in the provsion of better living standards to the people.
If any meaningful development has to succeed in Lunga District, first class priority must be given to the development of water ways and purchase of powered boats.
(The Author is former Provincial Information Officer for Copperbelt province from 1985 -1997).

Original_Search_Title: Enter the Manchurian Candidate
Headline: Kenya: Enter the Manchurian Candidate
Body_Text:
After a few days away, delightfully feeling like the puppet master as the numbskulls on social media proved me right, I feel the oh so delicious need to fire a final salvo with the best quote I got on my article last week from the Great Roy.
" You write an article saying that some of us, not all, but some of us use social media like monkeys. Then the very "some" spend the entire day throwing bananas all over the play and prove you right". I know, Roy, I know. Altogether now, "Awww bless". Those who know they have used social media platforms to spread hate were indeed wounded by my words and rightly so. The rest kept wondering "can't these fools read?" Beyond 142 or so characters? Clearly no. As Jalango reminds me often, even the daft and semi-illiterate are protected and embraced by our new constitution.
Now, speaking of our beloved constitution, I see that The Media Council of Kenya has expressed reservations over Thursday's court ruling that the eligibility Uhuru Kenyatta and William Ruto to contest for presidency should not be discussed publicly until a case touching on the issue is determined. The council said that while it had faith in the judicial system had the right to guarantee fair hearing for the accused, it hoped that the process would still uphold the freedom of speech and expression of all Kenyans as provided for in law. Judge Lenaola ruled that anyone who feels aggrieved by the order should present himself or herself to the court on February 17, 2012 when the case comes up for mention. I hope we will be there.
However what really puzzles me about the whole issue is, given that Uhuru and Ruto haven't filed any papers to run for President, how would we possibly ban something that hasn't happened? If you look at the caliber of Judges we have, there's no way that any decision they make in court could be determined by media articles, Bull's Eye and the like. Perish the thought and I stand to be corrected. Uhuru and Ruto have made no legal or formal bid to run for President and our courts and the very smart judges who sit there do not and cannot take public announcements in "prayer" meetings as legally binding. Surely.
When The Constitutional Court headed by Judge Lenaola made it's decision on the election date, they made it very clear that their decision, judgment and ruling wasn't and can't be made based on media articles and other noises on the streets, so why would this matter be any different? Another thing I'm puzzled about is who determines who can and cannot run for public office. Isn't that the work of the IEBC? I'll be watching what happens in court on February 17 - I have a feeling we missed something here.
However, I'm more intrigued to see what is truly and really happening as the unveiling and defrocking of presidential hopefuls begins to take shape. I believe a few will throw in the towel, another lot will line up behind another to simply ensure their own political survival. I love the debate Musalia Mudavadi has kicked off countrywide. However Raila, Musalia, Karua, Kenneth, Kiyiapi et al are as open as any politician can be (hazy); but have we given thought to the installment of a Manchurian Candidate? That's who we should be watching out for carefully.
What is beginning to tickle the small hairs on the back of my neck is the Manchurian. According to the Urban Dictionary, a modern day Manchurian candidate running for office, is one who publicly supports one group to win an election but uses his executive or legislative powers to assist an opposing group; it should not be confused with a sleeper agent who has been brainwashed into working for someone.
Senator Iselin in the Movie starring Denzel Washington was the original Manchurian candidate: he espoused McCarthyism but was in fact a Communist. Let me break it down for you. In resent times, many conspiracy theorists have tried to paint Barrack Obama as a Manchurian candidate. The attacks, mainly anonymous e-mails, wove together various threads - his middle name "Hussein;" schooling in Muslim Indonesia; his Chicago pastor's embrace of the anti-Semitic leader of the Nation of Islam, Louis Farrakhan; and his calls for dialogue with Iran - to portray Obama as the Muslim Manchurian candidate.
I believe in our time, we will see a Manchurian candidate, not one who's "sleeper" status is activated subconsciously by seeing the "Queen of Diamonds"playing card while playing solitaire. Our Manchurian will be activated consciously by money - plan and simple - however what will his job be. Think back to the claims of billions raised in readiness for the election campaigns. Our job as voters is to be keen and not let the rhetoric sway our judgment - now more than ever. The question we must ask of every candidate is, what is the end game? Therein will we find our answers and elect wisely.
Allow me to borrow and paraphrase from PRISCILLA NYOKABI KANYUA of Kituo Cha Sheria. On the issue of the gag order and also in looking at those who's are not names in the gag order : "Sunshine is the best antiseptic. When an idea is exposed to the public for debate, its merits and demerits are exposed and eventually, the public will know the truth and the falsehoods". We know the truth, what we need to be acutely weary of is the falsehoods. I pray we will be smart, sober and alert enough to see the difference between the two no matter that sort of wrapping they come in.

Original_Search_Title: Rally Set to Boost Mt Kenya's Huge Tourism Potential
Headline: Kenya: Rally Set to Boost Mt Kenya's Huge Tourism Potential
Body_Text:
The hills around Nyeri will reverberate with the roar of turbo-charged machines when the KCB Micro Banking Rally goes to the slopes of Mount Kenya on Saturday.
The event opens the 2012 Kenya National Rally Championship calendar and expectations have been very high in the town since the rally headquarters opened at Green Hills Hotel on Thursday.
With a big show of might expected this weekend, the Mount Kenya Tourism Circuit has taken this rare opportunity to market the region.
The circuit's chief executive officer, Simon Wachira, said a dialogue between them and Kenya Motor Sports Federation bore fruit as they were allowed to sponsor a section of the rally - with a clear view of the country's highest mountain - to the tune of Sh50,000.
"The money will go to every winner of the five different categories, each pocketing Sh10,000," Wachira said. "In addition, every participating driver will have a chance to pick a free raffle ticket on Thursday and on Sunday evening. They will also be given complementary accommodation to various hotels in the region."
The section runs from the Aberdare Country Club towards Chaka, then down to Kimathi University.
Wachira said that the organisers are taking this opportunity to promote tourism in the region which has so far been registering quite a good number of local and international visitors.
Among the hotels that have been given complimentary offers to the rally drivers are Serena Mountain Lodge, Banana Leaf, SweetWaters Tented Camp and Mpeta Guest House. Others are Falcon Height and Sportsman Arms in Nanyuki, Three Steers in Meru, as well as Outspan and Green Hills in Nyeri.
Ceremonial start
The rally begins with scrutineering on Saturday morning at the Green Hills.
A 2pm ceremonial start from the Nyeri KCB Branch will immediately be followed by a super special stage at the famous Ruring'u Stadium.
The rally restarts at 8am tomorrow with the assembled crews tackling 14 special stages around Nyeri before the ceremonial finish at Ruring'u.
Two-time champions Carl Tundo and Alastair Cavenagh are gearing up for a season of fierce competition as each strives to become the first of the Dalbit Petroleum Rally Team to clinch a third national title.
"Although we are obviously friends and team mates, it will be an all-out war between us this season, I suspect," joked Cavenagh.
The two Dalbit team drivers missed the last round of the KNRC in 2011 having just competed together - with Tundo navigating - in the Kenya Airways East Africa Safari Classic Rally in a MK2 Ford Escort which saw the pair retire when they were caught in a flash flood near Il-Bissel.
Raaji Bharij is unveiling a new car (EVO9) and hopes to take on the 'big bulls' of KNRC in awesome fashion.
"I am both excited and a bit anxious about this weekend. The new car is brilliant and such a step up from the trusty Subaru N10. I know we have the pace but to compete at the very top of this sport you need solid financial support and currently we are still in talks with sponsors. We will definitely do our best in Nyeri but I know I'm a bit rusty as I haven't rallied since June, but either way I'm sure we will have a lot of fun," said Raaji.
KNRC Division Two champion Onkar Singh Rai is fired up.
"We have put a crash-box for this season so it should be interesting and hopefully will be faster. My approach for 2012 will be the same as 2011 as I would like to gain more experience," Onkar said.
Sammy Nyorri was In Nyeri by Thursday well in time for yesterday's recce. He drove his new Toyota Vitz rally car all the way from Nairobi to Nyeri and it was doing fine. "I wish we had tarmac events....so smooth and enjoyable," Nyorri quipped adding: "I have tested on gravel in Athi River and it was nice but we have to take it slow in Nyeri just to make sure we give the baby car a finish."
Nehreen Ismail and his brother Riyaz Ismail have a rare sibling rivalry. Nehreen is navigating Rashid Kabi and Riyaz co-driving Joe Muchiri both in a similar Subaru gc8.
"We both hope for a finish but of course, bullying one another is healthy and enjoyable," Nehreen said.
Nairobi's Falgun Bhojak is navigating Mombasa based Paras Pandya in the new Subaru N10 previously driven by Khuram Bhutt.
"I am very excited and nervous at the same time as I'm trying to psychologically and physically prepare myself. I am one of the new kids on the block and as an athlete I do expect good results and performance from myself, plus I believe nervousness is healthy provided I deal with it positively," said Falgun.

Original_Search_Title: Is the Serrekunda Sandika to Relocate?
Headline: Gambia: Is the Serrekunda Sandika to Relocate?
Body_Text:
Foroyaa received information that the vendors at the Serrekunda Sandika market are ordered by the Kanifing Municipal Council (KMC) to move to any of the two areas already identified by Council. The two places said to be identified by the Council for the relocation are said to be Abuko and Old Jeshwang.
Foroyaa visited the vendors at the Sandika to confirm the story. Abdoulie Sowe, who claims to be among the founding fathers of the Sandika, confirmed that a verbal notice was given to them by the Council to move from the place. He said they are given two options to either move to Abuko or to Old Jeshwang. He said the reason for this according to Council is that the land they are presently occupying is a private land and the owner is demanding for it from Council. He said they were told to select a committee comprising 4 women and 4 men to go and evaluate the two places already identified for the purpose.
Mr. Abdoulie Sowe, a vendor at the Sandika, said they are being informed that all the two places have some facilities like running water and toilets.
Mr. Yaya Dampha, another vendor, said the decision taken by the K.M.C is not yet finalised as they at the Sandika would find the relocation difficult since many of their customers come from different places and find it easier to come to where they are in Serrekunda which is the business centre.
Juweh Sey said the Sandika is one of the biggest Sandika market in the country where people come to daily to buy locally produced cereals and fruits such as groundnuts, coos, maize, oranges, bananas, mangoes etc. He said most of the foodstuff they are selling come from the rural area. He said traders travel to different weekly markets (Lumoo) around the country to buy and bring them to the Sandika for the vendors to buy and sell. He said sometimes they buy and take credit from the traders who would give them few days to get their monies back to enable them travel again to the weekly markets.
Sey explained that the cost of transportation contributes to the increase of their prices. He said they pay for the cost of both transportation and unloading of goods at the Sandika which reflects in the retail price of the food item. He said this is an agreement they reach with the traders and that after paying for the transport they get back their money when the goods are bought.
Sey said he is a member of the Sandika Committee and that their main constraint is lack of running water, electricity and toilet at the place. He said the only toilet in the Sandika is no longer in use and is situated at a poor location that is too close to some stalls.
He said they pay duty to Council on a daily basis but that nothing is being done in terms to improving the conditions at the Sandika. He also revealed that they make contributions to pay the night watch men.
Mamadou Wurry Bah, an old man also selling at the Sandika explained similar concerns.
The Views of the K.M.C
Foroyaa got in touch with the Chief Executive Officer of the Kanifing Municipal Council (KMC) for clarification.
Mustapha Njie C.E.O confirms that council has formed a task force in connection to the Serrekunda Sandika to evaluate the two markets at Abuko and Old Jeshwang to be operated as the new "Sandika". He said an option is given to the vendors to choose any of the two places they think is more suitable for them. He said the vendors have already been informed that the place they are presently occupying belongs to the department of health which has over the years been requesting the land from Council. He went on to explain that the two markets built by the Council at Abuko and Old Jeshwang have enough space for these vendors to occupy as a Sandika. He said Council has spent resources to build these two markets for people to use but that whenever they ask the vendors to relocate it becomes a problem.
The KMC CEO further explained that the two markets have some facilities such as running water, electricity with cash power meters and toilets. He also said Council has their own policy of moving vendors from one place to another and that this is not done one day but a notice is normally given to allow them time to prepare for it.
He finally called on the vendors at the said Sandika to comply and select for themselves the most suitable among the two markets already identified for them to relocate.

Original_Search_Title: Taking Care of a Birth Tear
Headline: Uganda: Taking Care of a Birth Tear
Body_Text:
Mariam Namaganda, 37, a small scale business owner with four children, got a birth tear while giving birth to her fourth child. "I gave birth to a baby girl last year in June. But before, I had serious labour pains that forced me to go to hospital at night because I knew time had come for me to have my baby."
"I got surprised because by the time I started pushing, there was no midwife. After a few seconds though, one came to my rescue," she says. "After the baby was born, the midwife told me that I had gotten a tear because the baby was too big (three and half kilogrammes)," she adds.
"After stitching me, she advised me to apply hot salty water on the wound to heal quickly but instead, things just worsened when the stitches got out three days later before the area had cured. The wound started producing pus. I had to rush to Mulago Hospital where it was repaired," she narrates.
Towards child delivery, most women undergo painful labour though it is hard to tell whether they will get a birth tear in the process or not. A birth tear according to Dr Paul Kiondo, a gynaecologist at Nyondo Clinic, is also known as a perineal tear, which occurs when the tissues between the vagina and the anus (perineum) get torn. He says that in most cases, the tear happens when the baby is too big and cannot normally pass through the vaginal opening thus making the vagina to over-stretch.
"Even if the tear is to occur, the birth attendant has to support the walls when the baby is coming out so that it doesn't over tear," asserts the doctor. He adds that after the birth attendant has realised that the baby is too big and the mother won't push easily, it is better to cut the vaginal muscles gently to prevent it from tearing badly all the way to the anus.
He then explains that having unsupervised deliveries or giving birth in the presence of untrained or unskilled birth attendants, say, traditional birth attendants, increases the chances of birth tear cases. It is because such people lack knowledge on what exactly to do in the process.
Immediate action
According to Dr Kiondo, the vaginal walls should be repaired with stitches immediately after delivery to put them back in their normal position. He says that, repaired muscles heal easily and faster than the ones that are left unrepaired. "The opening of the anus has to always be tight, but then if the tear is not repaired, the person's control of stool will be lowered," he says, adding, "It also makes the woman's vagina very wide and it is bound to cause marital problems. No man would love to have sex with a wide woman."
After the repair, wash the wound at least three times a day with warm salty water. "Do not wash the wound with very hot salty water or a bathing sponge because it may cause harm in the process," he cautions.
Before anything is done about the tear, Dr Kiondo says that the wound has to be kept clean to avoid any infections that may arise in the process. He notes that one can use pain killers like panadol to ease the pain and should always take soft foods like avocados and bananas including foods with high fiber content to avoid constipation, which leads to straining the perineal area when pushing the stool because it increases the pain.
"And since stitches create sitting discomfort, one is free to sit in ways she feels like provided she is comfortable and also try to move around to avoid any complications like chest pain," he notes.
He cautions the use of antibiotic creams on irritating stitches because it delays the healing and it is most likely to react in the body after a certain period of time. Dr Kiondo reveals that it takes a short period like three weeks for the wound to heal properly because of the high blood supply around the area.
Prevention
Birth tears, according to the doctor, can be prevented or minimised if mothers deliver in the presence of a trained birth attendant so as to give support to the perineum. Also, to educate women on why it is important to attend antenatal programmes so that they get to know how to take care of themselves.
"I believe with the increment in the nurse-patient ratio, such problems can be reduced because the number of patients is always high and the people around cannot attend to them at once," he adds.
For mothers who get birth tears, it is advisable to resume sex intercourse six weeks after delivery because that is the time the body would have recovered from the effects of pregnancy and child birth.

Original_Search_Title: Missing Man Found Dead in Murang'a River
Headline: Kenya: Missing Man Found Dead in Murang'a River
Body_Text:
The body of a 38-year-old man who was reported missing has been found in river Gondo in Murang'a with his private parts chopped off.
Livestock trader Jonah Mwangi of Gatarakwa village, Mathioya, disappeared on Friday last week. According to the area chief Frashiah Njeri, Mwangi who was a livestock trader had left home last Friday and didn' treturn. Njeri said the disappearance took on a newtwist after another man from the same village went to the home of the Mwangi's brother and told him that he had been arrested by police officers after theywere found committing a crime.
The man asked Mwangi's brother to accompany him and look for Mwangi but he declined. Area DC Evans Matundura said villagers were shocked early the following morning after they found blood in a shamba near Mwangi's home. "The blood spots heightened fears that his life could be in danger prompting the villagers to comb bushes in the neighborhood and rivers, "said the administrator.
Matundura said Mwangis body was found tied with banana leaves to a stone in the river on Monday evening. The body had visible injuries on the head and it was suspected that assailants might have used metal bars to kill. The DC said a man has been arrested and will be charged in court after police are through with the investigations.
He described the suspect and Mwangi as notorious criminals who have been terrorizing the region and who spent their lives in and out of prison. The body was moved to Murang'a County Council Mortuary and the suspect is being held at Nyakianga police station. The administrator at the same time appealed for calm as he assured the residents of their safety.

Original_Search_Title: Police, UWA Raid Shrine Hunting for Pythons
Headline: Uganda: Police, UWA Raid Shrine Hunting for Pythons
Body_Text:
Uganda Wildlife Authority (UWA) backed by Police raided a shrine to remove three pythons which were suspected to be used for witch-craft practice by a traditional medicine woman in Buwama on Masaka road.
The whole day operation which attracted several local residents from the surrounding villages was led by Buwama Police station chief, Abbey Ngako.
However, the operations yielded no results since the reptiles were not trapped as planned by the UWA officials.
The shrine was set up by a traditional medicine woman from Mubende district, Jjaja Bberelimu Nambi who was assisted by several of her aides.
A traditional doctor and a Policeman removing items from the shrine. Photo by Ayiga Ondoga
At the site, raw eggs and ripe banana were placed in baskets in front of the holes where the three python live, a big fire place was set, drums and gourd were placed around the fenced shrine.
The officials forced Nambi and her team out of the site, where they retreated to a nearby shrub with their items and started smoking their pipes.
"These people are wasting their time here. They will not get the pythons because they are spirits and not the pythons in the wild," Nambi boasted in front of the gathered people and the officials.
UWA officers digging up the holes where the pythons were said to be hidding. Photo by Ayiga Ondoga
The UWA officials started opening up the holes but after three hours there was no success of reaching the reptiles.
The team later sought assistance from the contractors constructing the Kampala-Masaka highway, Reynold Construction Company (RCC) who used two excavators to dig down the soil but failed get the pythons. The only things excavated from the soil were three rats.
"I told people earlier that the reptiles will not be found because they are spirits. They may be the ones who have turned into rats," Nambi asserted.
The operation led to the destruction of the entire anthill and the shrine site.
As the Police and UWA officials were leaving the place, Nambi and the local people were jubilating for the unsuccessful operation.
Graders digging up the holes to get the pythons. Photo by Ayiga Ondoga.
Gerald Musoke, who resides in the neighborhood of the shrine said, "Why should people come and destroy the shrine when they (Locals) are not complaining about the presence of the pythons in the place.
Musoke believes that although the shrine was destroyed, the reptiles will come back in the area since they were spirits who did not fear people when they come to the site to see them (reptiles) the past days.

Original_Search_Title: Dreams From Selling Banana Juice
Headline: Uganda: Dreams From Selling Banana Juice
Body_Text:
The first time I meet him is at his Hotel Diplomate during an interview with his daughter, Angela Katatumba.
Before our interview, she is distracted by the sight of an elderly man calmly pacing up and about the hotel reception with a glass of ice cold water, she leaves me standing and dashes for a hug from him after which they indulge in a light conversation.
After brief introductions, he offers me an "African" handshake and starts off with a talk on what appears his favourite subject: Success. He requests the receptionist, who keeps referring to him as, "Your Excellency" to give me a free copy of his biography, Success is Around the Corner.
After two days of reading the book, I now appreciate why he wanted me to read it before the interview. Two days to the anticipated interview, the media carried reports of his attempted assassination so he postponed it to the following Saturday.
At the gate, the simplicity of the residence of the Consul of Pakistan strikes me. Expecting a gigantic gate with the finest coatings, huge fence with razor wire, the residence is just like any affluent man's Muyenga home.
As we sit near what was once a swimming pool that was closed to create space for official functions to munch on bread with tea, the temptation to ask about the assassination attempt is irresistible.
"God has always protected me. A stranger tipped me on phone about the evil plot two days earlier," he says in a matter of fact way before assuring me how he knows. "The Indian behind all this has failed to bring me down. He wrote to the government in Pakistan so they sack me and failed, tried to put a caveat on my property and failed, now even in trying to kill me, he is going to fail. I trust my God!" He goes into details of how he once had a party and introduced the Indian, who owns a string of properties in town, whose name for legal purposes we prefer to withhold, to his family members. "My elder brother and I trusted the fellow and now I am paying for over trusting people," he says.
As the atmosphere of the interview gets a little tense, a call comes from a Pakistan asking for a recommendation to be a dual citizen. Another opportunity to discover the profound humility of Mzee as he is fondly called by the young and old at home. He phones his secretary to come to office and type the letter, but being a weekend, she regrets letting him down, pleading that she is in Jinja and all he says is, "It is okay, don't mind, I will type it myself."
Mr Katatumba, tell me about your work as the Honorary Consul of Pakistan to Uganda? I implore him but before I finish the sentence, he holds my arm and reminds me. "Call me Doctor or Your Excellency Katatumba." I have an honorary PhD of Life Achievement from the United Graduate College and Seminary of the US." Thankfully, he gets back to the question.
In a bid to save expenses, the Government of Pakistan, he explains, closed its Ugandan embassy and remained with the one in Kenya, the option then was to appoint a focal person who would perform the duties of the embassy, help its citizens get recommendations, defend the image of the country and represent it in diplomatic ceremonies here.
Becoming an ambassador
During his tenure as the President of the National Chamber of Commerce and Industry, Katatumba met Pervez Musharraf who later became Pakistan's president while leading Ugandan business delegations to the rice growing nation. "When Musharraf ascended to the presidency and appointed, a business friend he trusted and had confidence in; he raised a lot of dust given that I am a Christian and would become the only one in the world representing the Islamic Republic of Pakistan." This prompts the question on the size of his pay cheque, but interestingly, he tells me that the service comes with neither a salary nor allowances!
"I cater for all my staff expenses and even this 32- roomed house is not for the Pakistan government," he says, marveling at his old mansion. The only rosy bit of the service comes with diplomatic privileges and immunity, for example, I am not subject to Ugandan law." He adds, "You journalists wouldn't have rushed here to cover the story of that man who wanted to finish me off if I was only rich. By the way, I am one of the few Ugandans with the title His Excellency."
He goes on to boast of the 120 countries and 350 cities he has visited pointing to the framed photos of him rubbing shoulders with a host of dignitaries, notably among them, former Libyan leader,Muammar Gaddafi and President Museveni, who in his book, he states, turned down his request for a hotel business partnership under unclear grounds.
However, the diplomatic service has not been a rosy journey as he will live to remember the July 2010 Al-Shabab orchestrated bombings. 18 Pakistanis were arrested as suspects. His challenge was to protect them, see to it that they get justice and the six-month long investigations came with constant calls from relatives, friends and the Pakistani government." All the 18 were eventually found innocent and set free.
"The good thing with Pakistanis is that they are hardworking and account for 70 per cent of the car imports in Uganda, so there are limited cases of people bothering me for financial bail out, I mainly handle the technical aspects of my ambassadorial duties."
"When are you retiring?" is a question that gets him putting his spectacles down and reminding me he is not paid but only rendering service. He goes the extra mile and says when the Musharraf administration left office, he was obliged to write two letters: one to indicate willingness to retire and the other for continuity of diplomatic service. President Asif Ali Zardari's regime decided to retain him.
With this experience and with the money, I wonder why he does not take advantage of the highly commercialised Ugandan politics and get his way to parliament or even cabinet. He puts back his spectacles, laughs then answers, "politics is nowhere near my imaginations." He adds, cases of cliques and in-fighting that makes politics a game of not who you are but how unfortunate you are.
Not even examples of clean politicians can change his stand. If he can, he says, he would discourage his children from joining, I am thus ticked to delve into his family.
"Would you go if you were appointed ambassador, I push him on, but he crosses his legs and sits with hands akimbo and says, "My friend, I am the ambassador of a nuclear republic, Uganda is a banana republic, I am better off where I am."
Family life

He goes the extra mile and says when the Musharraf administration left office, he was obliged to write two letters: one to indicate willingness to retire and the other for continuity of diplomatic service. President Asif Ali Zardari's regime decided to retain him.
With this experience and with the money, I wonder why he does not take advantage of the highly commercialised Ugandan politics and get his way to parliament or even cabinet. He puts back his spectacles, laughs then answers, "politics is nowhere near my imaginations." Not even examples of clean politicians can change his stand. If he can, he says, he would discourage his children from joining, I am thus ticked to delve into his family.
"Would you go if you were appointed ambassador, I push him on, but he crosses his legs and sits with hands akimbo and says, "My friend, I am the ambassador of a nuclear republic, Uganda is a banana republic, I am better off where I am."
Family life
His nine children, from his two wives whom he says have never fought or quarreled, are dotted all over the career world. The eldest son is a pilot, the second-born, a nurse is married to an American man, Angella Katatumba, the musician and her twin brother, Frank Rugirwa who owns Obama Restaurant.Another one is an advisor with Citi Group.
Jay Katatumba, his youngest son fell victim of his trial and error. He pushed him to study journalism in Malaysia only to learn later on that he was studying law instead. So, why would he desire to have a journalist in the family? "You don't know how I admire you people, you have knowledge on everything, you should get PhDs. Because of the lesson learnt the hard way, I don't coerce them to study anything and I give them equal treatment and opportunities."
Angella, he explains, manages some of his biggest businesses like Hotel Diplomate and Uganda Real Estates Developers because she has in-born leadership quality. "As for her music talent, may be it is from her maternal great grandparents' genes," he opines.
Tracing his business roots
After establishing rapport, I am content that I can now ask any question on his business life without him feeling edgy and thus I fire the first question on how, when and where he started his business journey.
At the age of seven while at Nyamitanga Primary School in Mbarara, he began selling banana juice to buses plying the Mbarara-Kabale route. At such an early age, he employed some older villagers to squeeze and package the juice while he went to school.
His nine children, from his two wives whom he says have never fought or quarreled, are dotted all over the career world. The eldest son is a pilot, the second-born, a nurse is married to an American man, Angella Katatumba, the musician and her twin brother, Frank Rugirwa who owns Obama Restaurant.Another one is an advisor with Citi Group.
Jay Katatumba, his youngest son fell victim of his trial and error. He pushed him to study journalism in Malaysia only to learn later on that he was studying law instead. So, why would he desire to have a journalist in the family? "You don't know how I admire you people, you have knowledge on everything, you should get PhDs. Because of the lesson learnt the hard way, I don't coerce them to study anything and I give them equal treatment and opportunities."
Angella, he explains, manages some of his biggest businesses like Hotel Diplomate and Uganda Real Estates Developers because she has in-born leadership quality. "As for her music talent, may be it is from her maternal great grandparents' genes," he opines.
Tracing his business roots
After establishing rapport, I am content that I can now ask any question on his business life without him feeling edgy and thus I fire the first question on how, when and where he started his business journey.
At the age of seven while at Nyamitanga Primary School in Mbarara, he began selling banana juice to buses plying the Mbarara-Kabale route. At such an early age, he employed some older villagers to squeeze and package the juice while he went to school.
At this point, he lectures me on how business is talent. "I think it is in born," he says, revealing that this is why he lashes out at people who disorient the youth with the talk of become job creators and not seekers. This, he says, is like telling us all to become writers or footballers yet it is talent. "Rather than preach the entrepreneurship gospel, we should instill work ethics in the youth, tell them about job loyalty and leave job creation to those with the inborn ability."
But in an economy where hundreds of thousands of graduates are churned out annually to compete in the small job market that absorbs very few. I take him on this one. "Don't misunderstand me, unemployment is everywhere, life is all about struggling and grabbing opportunities but the ultimate is, those who can create jobs do so, the rest must work," he explains. Talking of opportunities, he continues to narrate his business journey.
"I saved the money from the juice business and bought a bicycle and at school, there were only two bicycles, the Headmaster's and mine." He adds proudly, "Later on, I graduated to a motor bike which I hid from my father who would get concerned on seeing me with more than Shs200."
While the other teenagers roamed villages and were taken hostage by adolescence, during his senior four holidays at Ntare School, he saw an advert in the newspapers calling for a contractor to manage Nganwa hostel on behalf of government. He applied and won the tender to run the hostel for two years.
How he maneuvred his way through the competition from both rich men and graduates in Mbarara, still puzzles him but all he recalls is that when he went to Old Kampala S.S for his A-level studies, he hired a trusted friend to run the tender on his behalf.
When he went to Makerere University, for a degree in Economics and Political Science, his business eye saw the opportunity of opening a laundry business for the students. He convinced Northcote Hall's administrator to directly deduct payment from "boom", a government allowance to students then. The success was so impressive that it gave him impetus to open a car washing bay.
With a natural customer care instinct, he ensured the car owners were served by smart, uniformed staff and given receipts. This made his washing bay a darling for most car owners around and far from Wandegeya. But this business success became his undoing when people he had trusted devised a way of shooting down his rising business star.
They began piercing car tyres and siphoning fuel from customers' cars. Before he knew, customer loyalty had vanished in thin air. He saw a silver lining in this dark cloud and after graduation, rather than accept the many job offers, he linked up with his brother who had studied architecture, opened DCM (Design, Construction and Materials). He would draw house plans and get the house ready.
All a client had to do was pay the money and await the house warming. The company rapidly grew into one of the biggest and most reliable contractors in town and in no time, he had began scooping multi million contracts. "I won a tender of US$2m from Kabale PTC to build dorms, class rooms and several other contracts across the nation," he reminisces.
Going to exile
At the prime time of the business, Idi Amin's soldier came to buy materials, but given the strict rules, he could not get them because they were booked. This enraged the army man who asked to meet the owner of the prosperous business. Scared and fearing for his dear life, he sneaked to exile in Kenya. Again, this was an opportunity to expand his business acumen.
"I opened DCM Kenya while remote controlling the Uganda Chapter and but one of my managers sabotaged it," he notes sadly. As though to fill the gap, he opened a tour company in Nairobi and while asleep, he was so incensed by the noise of the planes that hovered over his roof that he asked how much they cost and saved money to buy one while getting training to become a pilot. Before he knew, planes were part of his tour company's car hire and rental business, "In 1983, I personally flew in the Chief Justice, Wako Wambuzi, to swear in Prof. Yusuf Lule."
Opening Hotel Diplomate
The hotel, atop Muyenga Tank Hill with a clear view of the city was among the first private hotels in the country. Quite astonishing it was a residential house he failed to complete before going to self-imposed exile in 1976. While there, he kept pressing a brother to sell it off. "My brother refused to yield to my pressure when I came back in 1983, I completed it," he says, holding his head as he recalls how the hotel became a safe haven for United Nations, World Bank guests and fetched a lot of money, with rooms going for as much as $200 per night.
On the Katatumba suites, he says, "East African Insurance Company in Kenya was losing business and wanted to sell all their six buildings, I was contacted by a friend and I boarded my plane to Kenya from where we agreed to buy the suites on loan."
He stresses the need for trust worthiness, citing how much as his father was fairly well off as a Ssaza chief and medical doctor at Mbarara Hospital, he never gave him capital, playing his deals fair and persevering saw him through." With the accumulated capital, he later expanded to Katatumba Resort in Mbarara in 1983. But to say his business life has been rosy would be grossly wrong. "At one time, I lost $1m to a crook who duped me into an imaginary airline business, but life moves on, I don't fight those who fight me."
A satisfied man
When I ask him to project himself in the next five years, he says, "I am satisfied with what I have. However clever you are, you can not own the world, why do I need all the money for? I have even sold off some of my properties abroad." His net worth is a question I ask with mixed feelings but he closes his eyes, makes a rough estimate and zeroes down on, "About $10-15m."
"I believe in God and miracles," he says putting his palms together as though ready to pray, and explains why. One time he was diagnosed with chronic asthma and prostate cancer. He came back with tablets the doctors told him to swallow daily till death, but for a man who once lost a child aged four, this sounded normal. While at Muyenga mansion, he tuned in to watch televangelist Benny Hinn and the preacher said, "You are watching me and you have asthma and prostrate cancer, stand up and pray and be healed." He obliged and on from then on, all medical tests show he healed from the two ailments.

Original_Search_Title: Haven Among the Trees of Masaba
Headline: Uganda: Haven Among the Trees of Masaba
Body_Text:
If you have never travelled to the Eastern town of Mbale, you will be forgiven for assuming that good places for "happening" only exist in affluent town centres.
Until recently, when I had the opportunity to go to Elgon Villas, The Eco tourism centre, in Mbale 3 kilometres from the centre of the town along Mbale-Bumboi off Republic Street, I was one of those people who believed that all "good places" only existed in towns.
Elgon Villas is adjacent Nkonkonjeru ridge, the shimmering stony hills that look like a white sheet. The ridge is probably the highest around Mbale town, no wonder many a new comer would take it for Mt Elgon.
Although the exterior décor is often neglected by many a hotel, at Elgon Villas, it counts as much as the interior; it is a fine place for outdoor parties and good outdoor living room.
And nestled between the attractive and green exterior, the Elgon villa picnic centre, sits between big trees that stand high in the compound. The entrance appears like it is guarded by three towering trees.
The director, Mr Namangala Mwambu told me that the green colour in the compound signifies nature and life, while the white colour that dominates most buildings stands for righteousness.
The Eco tourim centre has vast bird species, water falls, various sites for watching Wanale mountain and a thick forest to preserve nature. I learnt that the Elgon Villas was intended to create a rendezvous that caters for both local people and foreigners who wish to meet in a conducive and pleasant environment while at the same time attracting clientele from an upcountry setting.
I was impressed by the way the environment is naturally modeled and the way food is prepared and served. The sophistication at the villas is not only reflected in the beautiful ever green compound, modern buildings and the Christian approach to the service, but also in the décor and menu.
A couple of days ago when my colleagues and I ventured to this fabled and idyllic place, the Chef, David Asiro welcomed us with a local dish-traditionally prepared matooke and malewa mixed in thick groundnut source.
The Mushroom soup starter was also very delicious as was the Magira, traditional Bugisu mashed beans. I tasted Elgon Villas Paste; strips of back bacon fish fillet and queen prawns in a white wine, cream and tomato source topped with cashew nuts and served with pasta twists at only Shs10,000 and there was no regret about all this.
Being a place that attracts European tourists, Indians and Africans, there is an abundance of different types of foods and drinks from which to choose, including fish and chips served with real malt vinegar. We delighted in the Caribbean cocktail that comprised Banana, Mango, Lime and Orange and the Nairobi night cocktail that has coffee, cream, ice cream and Ice.
Some revelers near us seemed to be enjoying the strawberry delight which I learnt is made of straw berries, ice cream and soda. At 'Elgon Villas' quality, style and value reign in service. The quality of food served was commensurate with the standard of the place. It was a wonderful visit.

Original_Search_Title: Kachasu Reveals Her Little Secret
Headline: Zimbabwe: Kachasu Reveals Her Little Secret
Body_Text:
EVERY athlete has a secret to success, some of it bordering on superstition.
For Precious Kachasu, world-class wheelchair tennis, basketball and javelin player, who won a silver medal for Zimbabwe at the Maputo all Africa Games last year, the secret is in her breakfast.
"It is very difficult to run the wheelchair on an empty stomach as much as it is difficult to run the wheelchair with a stomach full of starch. You have to be careful with what you eat on the match day," she said.
"Some athletes do not worry about their diet and take heavy stuff such as sadza or even bread before a game. My breakfast comprises a portion of rice, one glass of fresh milk and either an apple or banana before taking to the field," she said adding that light diet makes her fit to drive the wheelchair.
Indeed, the 25-year-old's every movement with her wheelchair exudes an aura of confidence and invincibility.
Donning a yellow round neck T-shirt proudly displaying the inscription "Right to Play", with two souvenir badges on each side that she brought from Qatar where she attended a leadership course, Kachasu curved a banana smile.
The University of Zimbabwe student had just arrived from Qatar where she attended a Youth Leadership Camp that was held from January 9-19.
"It was something else to me as I was travelling for the first time in my life. I had never dreamt of myself getting to some of these countries," she said.
She was confident of putting everything she learnt there into practice here in Zimbabwe.
The course presented her with a chance to start learning leadership and administration skills before she retires from playing competitively, but Kachasu dismissed any chances of retiring anytime soon.
"Despite taking part in leadership courses, I am not thinking of retiring anytime soon. I had a chance to learn a number of things from the course itself and from other participants from other countries.
"I am going to engage the Paralympics committee and see if they are going to assist me in sharing what I learnt there with other athletes living with disabilities."
Born on October 7 1986, Kachasu started playing the three sporting disciplines at a tender age as a student at Mutare Rehabilitation Centre where she represented the school in Danhiko Games.
"I have not won so many medals playing basketball, as I play for the University team but I have won a number in wheelchair tennis.
Kachasu was part of Team Zimbabwe in Mozambique last year.
"I won a silver medal at the All Africa Games that were held in Maputo last year in javelin."
Apart from being a sport person, Kachasu is in her second year for the Bachelor of Arts Degree basing on history, war and strategic studies at the University of Zimbabwe.

Original_Search_Title: Age Has Nothing to Do With It
Headline: Uganda: Age Has Nothing to Do With It
Body_Text:
Robert Muwereza may still be a student at Kyambogo University, but the author has a number of titles to his name. Besides writing short stories, Muwereza also writes drama scripts. His dream is to work in the film industry. He talked to Bamuturaki Musinguzi
When did you start writing? And what motivated you?
I was 11 years. I used to write and send stories to newspapers that had children's sections. When I was in Senior One at City High School, we used to read literature books. The book, African Child by Camara Laye turned my mind around. The idea of writing a book was born. One year later, I wrote a story about the life of a poor African child titled The Wounded Soul most of which was my life experience growing up in a very poor family.
How do manage to combine writing and poetry and from which do you derive the most pleasure?
The art of writing is God-given. Poetry is circumstantial. Sometimes, it is out of emotions that I write. Sometimes, I cry while writing poems, sometimes I smile. Any poet will tell you that poetry is from the heart. And the best poem is that which you write out of emotions, not thinking. Most of my books are fiction. Poetry and writing are linked. Each enhances the other.
Do you plan to venture into writing long stories in future?
Yes, I do. Writing long stories is a stage I have just reached. I am currently working on a novel that I hope will have over 300 pages.
You seem to tackle a number of topics in your works? Do you intend to specialise as you gain experience?
No, at the moment, I am looking at being Robert Muwereza the author. I want to be the best I can be and I am doing a lot from which probably I will find what will make me the best .
What hurdles have you encountered as a young writer?
The biggest one is people undermining me and do not believe a young man like me, a student, can write a good story. Worst of all, my own family does not believe in me. They do not see sense in this "business of novels". Actually ,that is why I am doing Statistics and Economics at Kyambogo University after which, I will take on literature or join a movie academy in California, USA. The second challenge is being discouraged by people, even my close friends. Publicity and technical support is another challenge.
Ugandans have a poor culture of reading. How should they be encouraged to read more?
Many people in Uganda are poor and troubled, too entangled and stressed that they cannot concentrate to read a story and finish it. One should start by reading one page of anything before them, someday they will read 100 pages. I am working with some people to put up a literature festival majorly to encourage and uplift the reading culture in Uganda.
What is your advice to young aspiring writers?
Writing and publishing is possible. Writing can change your life and future. You can turn those thoughts, ideas and stories into a novel the world may like.
You have released a number of short novels, what is your best?
My favourite book is Anna Banana. With that book, I feel a success managing to surface in the life of Anna, an American who falls in love with a young Ugandan man who is a king to be. The world around them turns into a bitter one. Their journey of life is marked by the will to live, the courage to survive and the power to love. One thing, like any artist, we have a saying "The next work is the best one".
How would you gauge the Ugandan book industry and novels in particular?
In Uganda, we have a very poor book Industry where writing is given less attention. My first book was not received well in Uganda. That is why I actually took on the foreign market, so I am not known here. We have a lot of talented authors and some have died with untold stories because of the poor structure of the Ugandan book Industry.
Few publishing houses will accept a fiction manuscript, but will gladly welcome textbook manuscripts. I am lucky to have my books published in America.

Original_Search_Title: What Exactly Did NRM Gain From Kyankwanzi Honeymoon?
Headline: Uganda: What Exactly Did NRM Gain From Kyankwanzi Honeymoon?
Body_Text:
The country is in a pitiless crisis; traders have closed shops because of skyrocketing interest rates, inflation and weak currencies, businesses are collapsing because of constant power cuts, underpaid teachers are threatening to strike, the Speaker of Parliament has threatened to demonstrate over impassable roads in her constituency.
Parliament is in riotous mode over the never ending corruption scandals, youth are threatening to demonstrate naked over their funds, a full Cabinet minister with imaginary royal blood in her veins resigns because to err is human, the former Premier Kintu Musoke calls on Museveni to sack corrupt debris from his Cabinet; nodding diseases is affecting humans in the north, while Banana Wilt disease is out of control, 88 per cent of Ugandans are not connected to the national grid, government is hiring out drug-fuelled paramilitary agents to beat up the public, a state agent arrogantly and with impunity shoots into a crowd; continuous wastage of taxpayers' money, general dissatisfaction over an inept and inconsiderate government and as for health services, don't even go down that route.
So, with such a calamitous situation one would have thought that the National Resistance Movement (NRM) retreat in Kyankwanzi would discuss these pertinent issues and come up with sustainable solutions. Instead, they embarked on a honeymoon of wining and dining spree as they showed off in the media consuming tonnes and tonnes of meat, courtesy of the already over burdened tax payer.
They also had time to celebrate the 63rd birthday of the Prime Minister Amama Mbabazi till they dropped and while at the celebration, they came up with an absurd proposal to expedite the appointment of deputy Resident District Commissioners in charge of patriotism and youth so as to promote patriotism in the country. Which reminds me of North Korea which taught its people how to mourn when their leader died. Talk of "patriotism"!
Instead of thinking of austerity measures and the numerous challenges the country is facing, they were instead all dressed up in military fatigues, spent mornings running all over the place, practising shooting ranges of which a senior Cabinet minister missed all his targets; taken into military drills and taught how to take cover, crawl and roll, stripping and assembling of a gun like they were preparing to march onto Benghazi. Two whole weeks spent on doing absolutely nothing constructive apart from dancing themselves lame!
On each day of the retreat (from January 12-23), Mr Museveni would begin his sessions by attacking and blaming me, Kazoora, for most of his government's failures yet I have been out of Parliament for seven years. He would also heap blame on the colonialists who left the country 50 years ago. But after 26 years in power, what do you expect? Regurgitated and recycled ideas mingled up with escapism.
And at the closure of each day of the honeymoon, plenty of my sources would call me to tell me what the General had been saying. When Miria Matembe was in Cabinet, the President accused her of leaking Cabinet proceedings to me. I wonder who he will blame now yet I still know what transpires in Cabinet. I am now inundated with calls from worried NRM MPs asking me why Museveni hates me so much. The reason is because I do not waver in my beliefs and convictions.
The NRM Kampala Central MP Mohammed Nsereko sums it up all well when he says "I am not like a piano, whose buttons are tuned to play specific sounds. I am now more preoccupied with the problems of my people. I cannot go to Kyankwazi to enjoy fellowship when my people are languishing in poverty with neither electricity nor medicine".
Indeed, Museveni was just using the Kyankwanzi honeymoon to gauge the crowd for loyalty. It is high time Museveni got to know that when the show is over and you become extraneous, then it's time to get off the stage.
Maj. Kazoora is a consultant on democratic governance, legislation & institution reform.

Original_Search_Title: Luzira A4C Rally Ends Peacefully
Headline: Uganda: Luzira A4C Rally Ends Peacefully
Body_Text:
Kampala — An Activists for Change (A4C) rally held in the Luzira suburb of Kampala yesterday ended peacefully, with no major incidences of disorder.
Notably, Police exercised restraint in crowd control and traffic moderation as the opposition leaders and their supporters went through the three-hour rally.
Police deployment was at its maximum with a minor incident at Silver Springs Hotel in Bugolobi that forced police to disperse swelling crowds that had obstructed traffic.
At 6.45pm yesterday, the Forum for Democratic Change (FDC) leader, Dr Kiiza Besigye, and his entourage were allowed passage through the city centre after the rally. They were, however, not allowed to move in a convoy with police improvising to incooperate commuter taxis and other vehicles between the convoys.
Kampala Metropolitan Police Chief Andrew Kaweesi personally oversaw the operation, which saw A4C officials allowed a peaceful procession through the suburbs of Luzira, Kitintale, Bugolobi and Nakawa.
At the rally, Dr Besigye insisted on the importance of a Uganda led without the power of the gun. "Amin in 1971 passed my home in Luzira with guns to liberate Uganda. The same guns were turned against the citizenry. It is the same guns that kicked him out eight years later. The same guns that brought the NRM into power have now been turned on the citizenry. For the future of this country, this must end," Dr Besigye said.
Dr Besigye blamed the rising cost of living on what he called the unnecessary and illegal injection of trillions of Shillings by the Central Bank into the economy. "Mr (Emmanuel) Mutebile (Central Bank Governor) illegally printed Shs2 trillion which was used in the recent election to buy votes and compromise hearts. It is this money that has made your hard-earned money useless," Dr Besigye said. He called for national unity in the quest for change in the form of leadership in the country.
FDC Women League leader Ingrid Turinawe called on women to join A4C. She asked the population not to throw stones at security personnel. "They are under a lot of pressure. They have more problems than the rest of the population. You would rather throw meemvu (ripe bananas)at them.
Before the rally, Dr Besigye and A4C officials visited the Port Bell scene where on Sunday one person was killed and three others injured after Constable Santos Komakech Makmot fired indiscriminately into an angry crowd that had been evicted from a road reserve by Kampala Capital City Authority law enforcement officers.
Today, A4C intends to hold a similar rally in Kawempe.

Original_Search_Title: Want to Stay Sharp? Go the Cardio Way
Headline: Kenya: Want to Stay Sharp? Go the Cardio Way
Body_Text:
Cardiovascular exercise, as the name suggests, stands for any form of exercise that elevates your heart rate and keeps it that way for an extended period of time. It is perhaps the most important form of exercise you can engage in, for several reasons.
It is common knowledge, for instance, that regular participation in cardiovascular exercise is a key pillar in the fight against the escalating cases of heart disease and obesity in urban societies all around the world.
This form of exercise has also been demonstrated to significantly affect one's mood in a highly positive manner, helping to combat both stress and depression.
Even your memory will benefit from regular cardiovascular exercise and you will be less likely to lose your mental acuity and lucidity in your senior years, compared to an individual who lives a more sedentary life.
How much is enough?
If you are generally happy with the way your body looks and you just want to keep your arteries unclogged and your heart healthy, then you should aim for at least 20 minutes of light to moderately vigorous cardiovascular exercise every day. This could be anything from simple walking, swimming or a group exercise class at a health club.
It is recommended that every adult should notch up this basic minimum of cardiovascular exercise, everyday in order to keep heart disease and metabolic disorders like diabetes at bay.
For anyone looking to shed body fat and trim the waistline a little bit, a much more vigorous approach may be necessary. You should aim for at least 30 to 60 minutes of vigorous cardiovascular exercise every day.
One kilogramme of body fat contains roughly 7,000 calories, meaning that if you aim to burn at least 500 calories a day, it is feasible to sustain a weight loss rate of about half a kilogramme a week or two kilogrammes a month. This is the recommend safe rate of losing weight without triggering other health complications and unwanted side effects.
What is the best time to do cardio?
Many people erroneously believe that cardiovascular exercise should only be done early in the morning, to maximise its effectiveness, but there is no scientific research that lends any credibility to this notion. I maintain that the best time to do cardio is whenever you have the time.
If morning works best for you, go for it; but remember that your muscles are cold and stiff in the morning, so a proper warm up is extremely essential to keep injuries at bay. Some of us may experience restlessness and difficulty sleeping if we do intense cardiovascular exercise later in the day, particularly in the late evening.
All in all, you will need to observe yourself and figure out what time works best for your body as well as your schedule.
It is imperative to realise that not all cardiovascular exercise needs to take place in a gym. Running your errands in town on foot is a great way to sneak in some cardio into your day at no cost and without necessarily going out of your way.
Can I combine cardio and weight training?
It is probably optimal to separate your strength training and cardio workouts, doing each on a separate day for maximum effectiveness. A good cardio workout will take a lot out of you, and you are unlikely to have the strength to engage in an effective weight training session.
On the flip side, doing weight training before cardio will probably leave you extremely exhausted and unable to sustain an intense cardiovascular workout for a meaningful length of time, besides greatly increasing the risk of injury.
There are, however, cases where it is extremely beneficial to combine both strength and cardio training into a single package, like during circuit training. Circuit training is a great exercise option for individuals who are short on time but are still looking for a comprehensive, all inclusive workout that will help shed fat and tone muscle. It is my favourite option.
Should I eat before or after?
I personally don't recommend doing intense cardiovascular exercise on an empty stomach because it is possible that your blood sugar levels could dip to dangerous levels, precipitating a fainting spell at worst or curtailing your physical abilities.
On the other hand, cardiovascular exercise on a full stomach can be very uncomfortable, contributes to developing stitches and generally just curtails your performance.
What you need is a compromise. I recommend eating light, carbohydrate heavy food, at least 2 hours prior to your cardio session.
Marathon runners rely on a technique called carbo loading, where they eat lots of pasta and other carbohydrate rich foods the night before race day to help beef up their energy stores and ensure optimum performance.
At the very least you can keep some fruit like an apple or a banana in your gym bag to snack on 30 minutes before your session. It will greatly improve your productivity.
Should I drink water during cardio?
The trick to staying hydrated is to start early, as early as 48 hours before your cardio session. Research has proven that you can hardly replenish your fluid levels once you are exercising because you generally sweat out much more than you can take in.
You need to start early so that what you drink as you exercise is merely to sustain your level of hydration rather than actively trying to re-hydrate.
Make it a habit to drink the recommend eight glasses of water everyday, regardless of whether you are exercising or not. And make it a point to keep a water bottle close to you at all times during exercise.
Raymond is a fitness consultant.

Original_Search_Title: Locals Struggle With Soaring Inflation
Headline: Uganda: Locals Struggle With Soaring Inflation
Body_Text:
The opposition in Uganda is again trying to revive last year's street protests against the record high cost of living. Last Thursday, Forum for Democratic Change figurehead Kizza Besigye was teargassed and put in 'preventive detention' ahead of a planned rally in Kampala, which seems to have become daily routine in Uganda's capital. Still, the opposition vows to continue the resistance.
For Sarah Ndagire, the struggle against rising prices of food and fuel has not eased a single moment since it started a year ago. The 25-year-old cleaner is one of the many individuals behind the headlines about 'runaway inflation' and teargassed politicians. Ndagire barely manages to make ends meet.
"In 2010, a bunch of matoke (steamed green banana) cost 8,000 shilling (3.40 euros), now I pay 15,000 shilling. A kilogram of rice used to be 1,000 shillings, now 3,500. It affects me, but there is nothing I can do," Ndagire explains in her brick-built yet ramshackle home in Kampala's Kiwatule area. She and her husband, who sells newspapers in the streets, together earn about 225,000 shilling (95 euros) a month. They have four children.
Tailoring course
Ndagire is an example of how Uganda's inflation - which has come down from a more than 30 percent high, but still stands at about 27 percent - bars ordinary citizens from climbing up the social ladder. "I started a tailoring course so that I could earn more money," Ndagire says, "but I had to quit because I could not pay the fees." Now, Ndagire does not know where to find the money to pay the school fees for her children.
High lending rates
Ndagire voted for , during the elections in February last year, which saw Uganda's president Yoweri Museveni extend his 25-year-old rule with yet another term. The elections signalled the start of the 'walk to work'-protests against inflation and bad governance, which were brutally suppressed by the authorities.
The opposition now tries to rekindle the protests by capitalizing on a new phenomenon, the increasing costs of borrowing for consumers and businesses. Ironically, the high lending rates are meant to bring down the inflation and ease the public's pain.
Electricity subsidy scrapped
But many Ugandans see them as just a new problem with which the government is trying to solve the previous one. To make matters worse, the government scrapped its subsidy on electricity, causing a price rise of 36 percent for households and even 69 percent for businesses.
Sarah Ndagire does not have electricity, let alone a bank loan. In that sense, the latest economic woes do not affect her. They do affect Kampala's many retailers though. Like Nathan Mukisa, who sells stationery in the city centre.
Orphans
"The rising costs of electricity and loans cause the wholesalers - from whom we buy our supplies - to increase their prices," explains 27-year-old Mukisa in the shop where he rents three shelves; they are stocked with staples, pens and printing paper.
"That means that our expenditures increase, so we earn less." Mukisa says he makes an average daily profit of 10,000 shilling (4.25 euros), barely enough to sustain himself and the four orphans he took custody of after his brother, who owned an orphanage, died.
Shop closed
Mukisa shares his shop with seven other retailers. Together they pay the rent and electricity. "We cannot afford a back-up generator, so when the power goes off, which is happening ever more often, we sometimes have to close the shop. That means no income."
Like Sarah Ndagire, Nathan Mukisa supports the opposition's ideas. Yet, just like Ndagire, he does not expect things to change soon.

Original_Search_Title: A Country of Paradoxes
Headline: Nigeria: A Country of Paradoxes
Body_Text:
A prominent human rights lawyer, Chief Femi Falana, in one of the numerous interviews he granted, described the geographical entity called Nigeria as a Banana republic.
Likewise, a francophone website 'Afrique en Ligne', in one of its references to the country, described it as 'un pays de paradoxes' (a country of paradoxes).
Apparently, this last description unarguably fits the country's current status perfectly well, considering her soaring corruption profile.
Yes, a country of paradoxes because Nigeria is one of the world's largest producers of crude oil, and at the same time, one of the largest importers of refined petroleum products.
Nigeria is a country of paradoxes to the extent that super corrupt leaders who in other climes, would be in jail for embezzlement and fleecing the public treasury are celebrated here. They are crowned kingmakers, and are accorded red carpet reception at public events.
Worse still, they parade themselves flamboyantly and lord over the larger population of impoverished and hopeless citizens who neither have the means to pursue justice nor the power to fight oppression and injustice.
Nigeria is a country of paradoxes because she boasts of citizens who have won a global award as the happiest people on the surface of the earth, who, even under the most excruciating pains, will always manage to put on the best of smiles.
The country is full of paradoxes because the people will rather than push back when pushed to the wall, break the wall and find themselves an escape route at the other end. A country of paradoxes indeed!
But why do Nigerians complain about injustice and bad governance? Why do they complain about corruption and marginalisation? Nigerians have, on several occasions, demonstrated total lack of will power, resolve and commitment to resist oppressive, unpopular and anti-people policies.
Because they lack fighting spirit, the scope and limitations of their weak protests and actions are always predicted so accurately thus, encouraging a handful of mindless and highly corrupt individuals, who have bulldozed their way into the corridors of power, to hijack the national cake for themselves and for their cronies, leaving jobless, impoverished and hungry Nigerian majority to scramble for the crumps.
Nigerians had one of the brightest opportunities of a lifetime recently, to break and dismantle once and for all, the satanic clique of some very important criminals, who have constituted themselves into a cabal, holding the destiny of Nigeria, and indeed, that of Nigerians to ransom, but they failed woefully. A country of paradoxes!
It was not about fuel subsidy removal. It was about looting of public treasury; about oppression and bad governance; it was about corruption, the cankerworm that has caused a serious damage to the good image of Nigeria making her a laughing stock before the comity of nations.
The so-called organised labour that literally dragged out Nigerians from their homes and offices to join in protesting corruption and gross injustice, only turned around to betray their trust, abandoning them in the midst of the struggle without achieving the purpose for which the protest was organised.
Why then, would the cabal not be waxing stronger by the day since (as it now appears) neither man nor the gods can tame it? Why wouldn't the politicians continue to loot the treasuries since nobody is going to jail?
The rationale behind the unleashing of soldiers against harmless protesters in a democratic dispensation still needs some explanations.
If the country has such dedicated soldiers that are ready to defeat all threats to the country, why then, have they not been long sent after the real enemies of this country, - the cabals that milk this country dry, or against Boko Haram that has been holding the country to ransom for a long while now?
Who and who constitute the so-called cabal that even the Federal Government, with all the powers conferred on it, seems to be afraid of?
Is it not a direct insult on the collective intelligence of all Nigerians, that in a country of over 150 million people, a few criminals, who have found their way to the pinnacle of power, are allowed to run down the economy with such impunity?
Nigerians are tired of listening to tales on oil cabals. The Federal Government surely knows who they are. There is no need shielding them.
The soldiers should not occupy the streets of Lagos, they should be occupying the bedrooms of the cabals so that they will not have a peaceful sleep. They should infest their sitting rooms like ants so that they will know no peace.
Nigerians want to enjoy the blessing of oil like their counterparts in the Organisation of Oil Exporting Countries (OPEC). Nigerians want to pay as less as N17 per litre of fuel as it is done in Saudi Arabia, or if possible, N4.65k as obtained in Venezuela...
This so-called cabal must be reminded that power belongs to the people and that a single individual cannot win a war against his community. Every act of injustice perpetrated by man against his fellow man shall not go unpunished.
It shall surely be redressed; if not now, then later, and if not by man, then certainly by God, for the triumph of evil over good is only, but temporal.

Original_Search_Title: Gachorwe Fire Victims Given Clothes, Food
Headline: Kenya: Gachorwe Fire Victims Given Clothes, Food
Body_Text:
More than 30 families who were rendered homeless by fire on Friday in Gachorwe village in Banana, Kiambu, yesterday received bedding, clothes, utensils and food donation.
The fire victims have been living at a church near the village.
